mood: Kanakangi has an austere, pristine, and inward-looking mood. Its all-shuddha scale gives it a calm, restrained, and contemplative character, often felt as serene, devotional, and quietly dignified rather than ornate or dramatic. The raga can suggest purity, simplicity, and a sense of prayerful submission, with a gentle golden warmth hinted at by its name, 'the golden-bodied one.'
description: Kanakangi is the 1st melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system and the 1st rāgam in the 1st chakra, Indu. Its mnemonic name is Indu-Pa and the mnemonic phrase is sa ra ga ma pa dha na. It is a sampurna rāgam with all seven swaras in both ascent and descent, using only shuddha swaras: S R1 G1 M1 P D1 N1 S / S N1 D1 P M1 G1 R1 S. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Salagam, the 37th melakarta. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Kanakāmbari. In the original Venkatamakhin asampurna melakarta scheme, Kanakāmbari is also the 1st melakarta, but with an audava-sampurna structure: S R1 M1 P D1 S / S N1 D1 P M1 G1 R1 S. The raga is described as meaning 'the golden-bodied one' and is considered a very rare, austere melakarta with relatively few compositions. Graha bhedam on Kanakangi yields Kamavardani.
gamaka_usage: Kanakangi is generally rendered with restrained, even treatment rather than heavy ornamentation. Because it is a pure shuddha melakarta, the raga identity depends more on plain, careful phraseology than on elaborate gamaka. R1, G1, D1, and N1 may take gentle kampita or subtle slides where needed, but the oscillation should remain light and controlled. S and P are usually held steady. The swaras should sound clean and uncluttered, with smooth transitions and minimal embellishment, preserving the raga’s austere and meditative flavor.
notable_features: Melakarta #1; first rāgam of the Indu chakra; mnemonic name Indu-Pa; shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Salagam (#37); called Kanakāmbari in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school; asampurna Venkatamakhin version is audava-sampurna; janyas include Karnataka Shuddha Saveri and Lavangi; graha bhedam yields Kamavardani; the name is glossed as 'the golden-bodied one'; regarded as an austere ragam with few compositions.

mood: Ratnangi has a calm, composed, and luminous mood, with a gentle devotional undertone. Its shuddha and relatively restrained swaras give it a serene, balanced character rather than intense drama, evoking clarity, poise, and a gem-like radiance. It suits reflective, auspicious, and prayerful settings, and can feel quietly uplifting rather than emotionally turbulent.
description: Ratnangi (pronounced ratnāngi, meaning "the one with a gem-like body") is the 2nd melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system of Carnatic music. It belongs to the 1st chakra, Indu, and its mnemonic name is Indu-Sri. The scale is sampoorna, using shuddha rishabham, shuddha gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and kaisiki nishadham: S R1 G1 M1 P D1 N2 S / S N2 D1 P M1 G1 R1 S. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Phenadhyuti. It is the shuddha madhyama equivalent of Jalārnavam, the 38th melakarta. In the older asampurna melakarta tradition compiled by Venkatamakhin, Phenadhyuti is the 2nd melakarta, with a different ascent: S R1 M1 P D1 P N2 S / S N2 D1 P M1 G1 R1 S. Ratnangi has a few minor janya ragas, with Revati noted as a very popular one. The raga’s notes, when subjected to graha bhedam, yield Gamanashrama and Jhankaradhwani. It is a foundational melakarta and serves as an important parent scale in Carnatic pedagogy.
gamaka_usage: The source text does not explicitly detail gamakas for Ratnangi, but as a melakarta with shuddha R1, G1, M1, D1 and N2, the raga is generally rendered with controlled, measured ornamentation rather than heavy oscillation. The swaras are typically approached cleanly and with smooth jaru-like transitions in scalar passages, while the stable anchor notes S, P, and often M1 are held plainly. Because the scale is structurally simple and non-vakra, gamaka treatment is usually restrained and even; any oscillation on R1, G1, D1, or N2 would be subtle and context-dependent rather than expansive. Compared with more gamaka-rich ragas, Ratnangi’s identity lies more in its exact note content and plain scalar clarity than in elaborate kampita patterns.
notable_features: Ratnangi is the 2nd melakarta and the 2nd raga in the Indu chakra. Its mnemonic name is Indu-Sri. In the Dikshitar school it is known as Phenadhyuti. It is the shuddha madhyama equivalent of Jalārnavam (38th melakarta). Graha bhedam on Ratnangi yields Gamanashrama and Jhankaradhwani. The asampurna melakarta version (Phenadhyuti) uses a different arohana: S R1 M1 P D1 P N2 S. Revati is mentioned as a popular janya raga.

mood: Ganamurti has a serene, devotional, and dignified mood, with an austere classical beauty that feels calm and spiritually uplifting. Its plain, tightly defined melakarta structure gives it a composed, stable character rather than one of intense drama, so it often evokes reverence, inner stillness, and a sense of musical purity. The raga suits contemplative listening and devotional expression, especially in settings where clarity and solemn grace are desired.
description: Ganamurti (meaning 'the idol of music') is the 3rd melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system. It belongs to the 1st chakra, Indu, with mnemonic name Indu-Go and mnemonic phrase 'sa ra ga ma pa dha nu'. Its swaras are shuddha rishabham, shuddha gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham. As a melakarta, it is a sampoorna rāgam with all seven notes in both ascent and descent. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Jhalavarali, the 39th melakarta. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Gānasāmavarāḷi. In the original Venkatamakhin asampurna melakarta scheme, Gānasāmavarāḷi is also the 3rd melakarta, but its ascent is shadava-sampurna: S R1 M1 P D1 N3 S / S N3 D1 P M1 G1 R1 S. The raga has minor janya ragas associated with it. The text also notes that shifting its notes by graha bhedam yields two other melakarta ragas: Vishwambari and Shamalangi.
gamaka_usage: The provided text does not give explicit gamaka rules for Ganamurti. From its melakarta structure and note set, it is generally treated with clear, restrained intonation rather than heavy oscillation. R1, G1, D1, and N3 are typically rendered with careful kampita or light oscillatory treatment as needed for phrase identity, while M1 and P are usually held steady. Because the scale is compact and symmetrical, smooth jaru between adjacent swaras and clean landing on S and >S are important. Compared with more gamaka-rich ragas, Ganamurti is usually approached with precision and clarity so that its distinctive shuddha swaras remain well defined.
notable_features: Melakarta #3; first chakra Indu; mnemonic name Indu-Go; mnemonic phrase sa ra ga ma pa dha nu. Shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Jhalavarali (#39). In the asampurna system, Gānasāmavarāḷi is shadava-sampurna with arohana S R1 M1 P D1 N3 S. Graha bhedam from Ganamurti yields Vishwambari and Shamalangi. The Muthuswami Dikshitar school name is Gānasāmavarāḷi.

mood: Vanaspati has a serene, composed, and devotional character. Its shuddha rishabham and shuddha gandharam give it a restrained, austere color, while the chatushruti dhaivatham and kaisiki nishadham add a gentle lift and warmth. As a melakarta, it feels balanced and complete rather than highly ornate, suiting contemplative or prayerful settings and evoking calm devotion, dignity, and inward focus.
description: Vanaspati is the 4th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system and belongs to the 1st chakra, Indu, with mnemonic name Indu-Bhu and mnemonic phrase sa ra ga ma pa dhi ni. It is a sampoorna scale in the modern melakarta system, using shuddha rishabham, shuddha gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, chatushruti dhaivatham, and kaisiki nishadham. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Bhānumati/Bhanumati. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Navaneetam (melakarta 40). In the older Venkatamakhin asampurna system, Bhānumati is the 4th melakarta and is shadava-sampurna, with the ascent omitting gandharam: S R1 M1 P D2 N2 S, while the descent is complete. The raga has at least one noted janya, Rasāli. Graha bhedam on Vanaspati yields the minor melakarta Mararanjani. The source text also notes compositions in this raga and its Dikshitar-school name, showing its importance in both theoretical and compositional traditions. The name Vanaspati literally means "lord of the forest," and the raga is recognized as a foundational melakarta with clear pedagogical value in the melakarta scheme.
gamaka_usage: The source text does not explicitly describe gamakas, but from the scale structure Vanaspati is typically rendered with restrained, measured ornamentation rather than heavy oscillation. R1 and G1 are usually approached with delicate kampita or gentle slides to preserve the austere flavor of the raga; M1 and P are generally held steady as anchor notes; D2 and N2 may take light oscillation or jaru-like transitions in phrases leading back to S. Because it is a melakarta with shuddha R and G, the swaras should remain clearly articulated and not be over-ornamented. The raga’s identity depends on clean phraseology and balanced treatment of the notes rather than elaborate gamaka density.
notable_features: Melakarta #4; 1st chakra Indu; mnemonic name Indu-Bhu; mnemonic phrase sa ra ga ma pa dhi ni. Shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Navaneetam (#40). In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school the raga is called Bhānumati/Bhanumati. In the asampurna system it is shadava-sampurna with arohana S R1 M1 P D2 N2 S and avarohana S N2 D2 P M1 G1 R1 S. Graha bhedam from Vanaspati yields Mararanjani. The text mentions Rasāli as a better-known janya raga associated with Vanaspati. The raga is also pedagogically important as an early melakarta in the first chakra.

mood: Manavati has a bright, composed, and devotional character, with a clear and orderly melodic contour that feels stable and uplifting. Because it is a complete melakarta with no vakra movement, it tends to project a straightforward, dignified serenity rather than intense pathos or romance. The raga can evoke a calm, prayerful atmosphere and a sense of inner poise, making it suitable for contemplative listening and devotional expression.
description: Manavati is the 5th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system and belongs to the 1st chakra, Indu; its mnemonic name is Indu-Ma and the mnemonic phrase is sa ra ga ma pa dhi nu. Its scale is S R1 G1 M1 P D2 N3 S / S N3 D2 P M1 G1 R1 S, using shuddha rishabham, shuddha gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, chatushruti dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham. As a melakarta, it is a sampoorna rāgam with all seven notes in both directions. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Pāvani, the 41st melakarta. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, the 5th melakarta is called Manōranjani, and the asampurna version has the same notes but a different arohana: S R1 M1 P D2 N3 S / S N3 D2 P M1 G1 R1 S, making it shadava-sampurna. The raga has minor janya ragas associated with it. A notable theoretical feature is that Manavati’s notes, when shifted by graha bhedam, yield Kantamani. The name is glossed in some sources as meaning "the bride," and the raga is regarded as a foundational scale in Carnatic music with a graceful, melodic quality.
gamaka_usage: As a melakarta with a plain, complete scalar structure, Manavati is generally rendered with relatively direct prayogas rather than heavy vakra ornamentation. R1, G1, D2, and N3 are the characteristic swaras and are typically given gentle kampita or subtle oscillation to bring out the raga’s vivadi color, while M1 and P are usually held steady. The ascent and descent should remain clean and symmetrical, with smooth jaru between adjacent notes where needed. Because the source does not specify special gamaka rules, the safest treatment is restrained ornamentation that preserves the raga’s straightforward melakarta identity and avoids excessive gamaka that would obscure its scale.
notable_features: Melakarta #5; 1st chakra Indu; mnemonic name Indu-Ma; mnemonic phrase sa ra ga ma pa dhi nu; shuddha madhyamam equivalent of 41st melakarta Pāvani; in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school the corresponding 5th melakarta is called Manōranjani; asampurna form is shadava-sampurna with arohana S R1 M1 P D2 N3 S; graha bhedam from Manavati yields Kantamani; uses vivadi swaras R1 and G1; the name is sometimes explained as meaning "the bride."

mood: Tanarupi has a calm, composed, and dignified mood with a gentle devotional undertone. Its scale feels bright yet restrained, with a sense of clarity and balance rather than dramatic tension. The raga can evoke peaceful contemplation, inner steadiness, and a solemn, prayerful atmosphere, making it suitable for reflective listening and devotional settings.
description: Tanarupi is the 6th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system and belongs to the 1st chakra, Indu. Its name is explained as meaning “the one that embodies tanam.” The mnemonic name is Indu-Sha and the mnemonic phrase is sa ra ga ma pa dhu nu. The scale uses shuddha rishabham, shuddha gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, shatsruthi dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham, making it a sampoorna rāgam with all seven notes in both ascent and descent. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Raghupriya, the 42nd melakarta. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Tanukeerti. The asampurna melakarta version Tanukeerti is described as audava-vakra-sampurna, with a 5-note ascent and zig-zag descent, but the same note material. Tanarupi has no janya rāgams associated with it. Graha bhedam on Tanarupi yields no other melakarta rāgam.
gamaka_usage: The source does not give detailed gamaka prescriptions for Tanarupi, but as a melakarta with only shuddha R1 and G1 and the higher D3/N3, it is generally treated with clear, measured intonation rather than heavy oscillation. The swaras are typically rendered in a straightforward sampoorna framework, with smooth jaru between adjacent notes and careful emphasis on the structural notes S, M1, P, D3, and N3. Because the raga is not bhashanga and does not use vakra prayogas in the melakarta form, gamaka treatment is expected to be relatively simple and stable compared with more ornament-heavy ragas. In practice, any kampita is usually restrained, with R1 and G1 approached cleanly and D3/N3 held with precision rather than broad oscillation.
notable_features: Tanarupi is the 6th melakarta and the 6th rāgam in the Indu chakra. Its mnemonic phrase is sa ra ga ma pa dhu nu. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Raghupriya (42nd melakarta). The asampurna equivalent Tanukeerti is audava-vakra-sampurna with arohana S R1 M1 P N3 S and avarohaṇa S N3 D3 N3 P M1 G1 R1 S. Tanarupi has no janya rāgams. Graha bhedam on this raga yields no other melakarta rāgam.

mood: Senavati has a calm, composed, and slightly austere emotional color, with a devotional undertone. The scale’s shuddha rishabham, sadharana gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and shuddha nishadham create a restrained, meditative atmosphere rather than overt brightness. It can suggest quiet prayer, introspection, and a dignified serenity, making it suitable for reflective and devotional expression.
description: Senavati is the 7th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system and belongs to the 2nd chakra, Netra, with mnemonic name Netra-Pa and mnemonic phrase sa ra gi ma pa dha na. Its scale is S R1 G2 M1 P D1 N1 S / S N1 D1 P M1 G2 R1 S, making it a sampoorna melakarta with all seven notes in both directions. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Gavambhodi, the 43rd melakarta. In the older asampurna melakarta system compiled by Venkatamakhin, the corresponding raga is called Senāgrani, and the scale is rendered with vakra prayoga in both ascent and descent. The raga has minor janya ragams associated with it. Graha bhedam on Senavati yields the melakarta ragas Latangi and Sooryakantam. The raga is also known in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school as Senāgrani.
gamaka_usage: As a melakarta with vivadi swaras R1, D1, and N1, Senavati typically requires careful kampita and jaru treatment to soften the inherent tension of these notes. R1 and G2 are usually handled with gentle oscillation and connected phrases rather than stark flat rendering, while M1 and P are comparatively stable anchor notes. D1 and N1 should be approached with controlled oscillation and smooth slides, especially in descending phrases, to preserve the raga’s sober character. Because the scale is structurally straightforward, the raga’s identity depends on precise phraseology and nuanced gamaka on the vivadi notes rather than heavy ornamentation.
notable_features: 1st rāgam in the 2nd chakra Netra. Mnemonic name: Netra-Pa. Mnemonic phrase: sa ra gi ma pa dha na. Shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Gavambhodi (43rd melakarta). In the asampurna melakarta system, Senāgrani uses the same notes but with vakra prayoga in arohana and avarohana. Graha bhedam from Senavati yields Latangi and Sooryakantam. The raga uses vivadi swaras (R1, D1, N1).

mood: Hanumatodi, popularly called Todi, is a weighty, deeply expressive raga with a serious, introspective, and emotionally rich character. It is often associated with devotion and pathos, yet it also has a calm, meditative dignity that can feel profoundly soothing when rendered slowly. Because it is said to be sung largely in the lower register and is difficult to handle due to its complex prayogas and intonation, it creates an atmosphere of gravitas, yearning, and inward reflection rather than brightness or exuberance. It suits contemplative settings, devotional expression, and moments of emotional depth.
description: Hanumatodi, more popularly known as Todi, is the 8th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system and is sung very often in concerts. It is called Janatodi in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. It is the 2nd rāgam in the 2nd chakra, Netra, with mnemonic name Netra-Sri and mnemonic phrase sa ra gi ma pa dha ni. It is a sampoorna rāgam using shuddha rishabham, sadharana gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham and kaisiki nishadham. The raga is described as difficult to perform because of its complexity in prayoga and intonation, and it is also noted for being sung in all lower notes. It is classified as a rakti raga, indicating high melodic content. Hanumatodi is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Bhavapriya (44th melakarta). Its Western equivalent is the Phrygian mode. It is distinct from Hindustani Todi; the Hindustani raga Todi corresponds to Carnatic Shubhapantuvarali, while Carnatic Todi corresponds in note content to Hindustani Bhairavi thaat but sounds very different because of gamaka treatment. The raga has many janya ragas, including Asaveri, Janatodi, Dhanyasi, Punnagavarali and Shuddha Seemanthini. It is also highly fertile for composition, with many kritis, varnams, padams, javalis and other forms composed in it by major composers.
gamaka_usage: The raga depends heavily on nuanced gamaka and precise intonation. The source emphasizes that its flavour comes from complex prayoga rather than plain scalar movement. In practice, the lower register is especially important, and the raga is typically rendered with weight and depth. R1 and M1 are the most stable swaras and can be prolonged without gamakam. G2, D1 and N2 require careful handling with oscillation and phrase-based treatment; G2 in particular is central to the raga’s identity and is shaped through oscillation and slides rather than being left plain. The characteristic Carnatic Todi treatment differs from Bhairavi-like handling by avoiding the Bhairavi-style use of D2 and by relying on the specific Todi phraseology and gamaka contour. The overall effect is one of slow, deliberate, richly ornamented phrases with strong emphasis on the raga’s lower octave identity.

notable_compositions.name: Roopamu Joochi
notable_compositions.composer: Tiruvarur Ramaswami Pillai or Muthuswami Dikshitar or Ramaswami Dikshitar

notable_features: Melakarta #8; 2nd rāgam in the 2nd chakra Netra; mnemonic name Netra-Sri; mnemonic phrase sa ra gi ma pa dha ni. Shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Bhavapriya (44th melakarta). Western equivalent: Phrygian mode. In Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Janatodi. It is a rakti raga and is especially known for its complexity in prayoga and intonation. It is sung very often in concerts and has a very large body of compositions. Its notes, when shifted by graha bhedam, yield five other major melakarta ragas: Kalyani, Shankarabharanam, Natabhairavi, Kharaharapriya and Harikambhoji. The raga is distinct from Hindustani Todi; Carnatic Todi corresponds to Hindustani Bhairavi thaat in note content, but the two sound very different because of gamaka usage.

mood: Dhenuka has a calm, devotional, and composed character, with a restrained brightness that suits prayerful and reflective moods. As a melakarta with a complete, symmetrical scale, it tends to feel orderly and grounded rather than highly ornate, evoking serenity, steadiness, and a gentle spiritual uplift. It can also carry a slightly plaintive, introspective shade because of its scale color, making it suitable for contemplative listening and devotional expression.
description: Dhenuka is the 9th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system of Carnatic music. It is the 3rd rāgam in the 2nd chakra, Netra, with mnemonic name Netra-Go and mnemonic phrase sa ri ga ma pa dha ni. Its scale is S R1 G2 M1 P D1 N3 S / S N3 D1 P M1 G2 R1 S. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Dhunibinnashadjam. As a melakarta, it is a sampoorna rāgam with all seven notes in both ascent and descent. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Shubhapantuvarali, the 45th melakarta. The raga is also known from the older asampurna melakarta tradition, where Dhunibinnashadjam is listed as the 9th melakarta in Venkatamakhin's original scheme. Dhenuka has a few janya rāgams associated with it, though the source does not list them individually. Its notes under graha bhedam yield three other melakarta rāgams: Shanmukhapriya, Chitrambari, and Shoolini.
gamaka_usage: As a melakarta scale, Dhenuka is generally rendered with clear, well-defined swaras rather than heavy oscillation. Sa, Pa, and the upper Sa are typically held steady as structural anchors. R1 and D1 are usually approached with gentle kampita or light jaru depending on phrase context, while G2 and N3 may take modest oscillation to bring out the raga color. M1 is normally stable and plain. Because the scale is complete and symmetrical, gamakas should not obscure the identity of the swaras; the raga’s character comes more from phrase choice and controlled ornamentation than from extreme gamaka density. Compared with closely related ragas, Dhenuka’s treatment is more restrained and direct, avoiding excessive embellishment that could blur its melakarta identity.
notable_features: Melakarta #9; chakra 2 (Netra), 3rd rāgam in that chakra. Mnemonic name: Netra-Go. Mnemonic phrase: sa ri ga ma pa dha ni. Called Dhunibinnashadjam in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. Shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Shubhapantuvarali (45th melakarta). Graha bhedam on Dhenuka yields Shanmukhapriya, Chitrambari, and Shoolini. The source notes that it has a few janya rāgams associated with it, but does not enumerate them.

mood: Natakapriya has a calm, balanced, and dignified mood with a gentle devotional undertone. As a melakarta with a straightforward sampoorna scale, it tends to feel orderly, stable, and unforced rather than intensely dramatic. The raga can evoke quiet contemplation, a composed theatrical grace, and a soft inwardness that suits reflective or devotional settings more than overtly passionate expression.
description: Natakapriya, meaning "the one dear to theatre," is the 10th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system. It belongs to the 2nd chakra, Netra, and its mnemonic phrase is "sa ra gi ma pa dhi ni." Its scale uses shuddha rishabham, sadharana gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, chatushruti dhaivatham, and kaisiki nishadham. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, the same melakarta is called Naṭābharanam. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Shadvidamargini, the 46th melakarta. In the original Venkatamakhin asampurna system, Naṭābharanam is treated as a vakra shadava-sampurna form with zig-zag phraseology rather than a strictly linear scale. Natakapriya’s notes, when subjected to graha bhedam, yield three other major melakarta ragas: Vachaspati, Charukesi, and Gourimanohari. The text also notes Sindhu Bhairavi as one of the janya ragas associated with this melakarta.
gamaka_usage: As a melakarta, Natakapriya is generally rendered with clear, stable treatment of the scale tones rather than heavy vakra ornamentation. R1, G2, D2, and N2 are typically approached with gentle kampita or light oscillation where phrase context demands, while S and P are held steady. M1 is usually a firm anchor. Because the raga is structurally straightforward in the modern melakarta form, gamakas should support the linear identity of the scale without excessive zig-zag phrasing; in the asampurna Naṭābharanam tradition, however, phrases are explicitly vakra and may use more curved, phrase-based movement. Compared with closely related melakarta ragas, the treatment should preserve the distinct shuddha-rishabha/sadharana-gandhara color and avoid introducing anya swaras.
notable_features: 4th rāgam in the 2nd chakra Netra; mnemonic name Netra-Bhu; mnemonic phrase sa ra gi ma pa dhi ni. Shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Shadvidamargini (46th melakarta). In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, the 10th melakarta is called Naṭābharanam. Graha bhedam from Natakapriya yields Vachaspati, Charukesi, and Gourimanohari. The asampurna version is vakra and shadava-sampurna, with arohana S G2 M1 P N2 D2 N2 S and avarohana S N2 D2 N2 P N2 P M1 G2 G2 R1 R1 S.

mood: Kokilapriya has a bright, serene, and devotional character, with a gentle sweetness suggested by its name and its clean melakarta structure. It tends to evoke calmness, inward devotion, and a composed, uplifting feeling rather than intense drama. The scale’s restrained color makes it suitable for reflective, prayerful music and for conveying a soft, pleasing emotional atmosphere.
description: Kokilapriya, meaning "the one dear to the koel," is the 11th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system of Carnatic music. It belongs to the 2nd chakra, Netra, and its mnemonic name is Netra-Ma with the mnemonic phrase sa ra gi ma pa dha na. Its scale uses shuddha rishabham, sadharana gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, chatushruti dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham, making it a sampoorna rāgam with all seven notes in both ascent and descent. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Suvarnangi, the 47th melakarta. In the original asampurna melakarta system compiled by Venkatamakhin, the corresponding raga is Kokilāravam, where the same notes appear in vakra phrases and the scale is shadava-sampurna, with six notes in ascent and full seven in descent. The raga is theoretically significant because its notes, when subjected to graha bhedam, yield Rishabhapriya melakarta. It has a few minor janya rāgams associated with it. The raga is not among the most commonly heard melakartas, but it remains important in pedagogy and theory as a clear example of the 11th melakarta structure and its relationship to the asampurna tradition.
gamaka_usage: As a melakarta with a straightforward sampoorna scale, Kokilapriya is generally rendered with moderate, controlled gamakas rather than heavy oscillation. R1 and M1 are typically stable anchor notes, while G2, D2, and N3 may take light kampita or gentle slides to bring out the raga’s color. P is usually held steady. Because the scale is not vakra and contains no anya swaras, the gamaka treatment should preserve the plain scalar identity; phrases should flow cleanly with smooth jaru between adjacent swaras and restrained ornamentation compared with more heavily gamaka-driven ragas. In the asampurna Kokilāravam form, the vakra phrase structure would naturally invite more zig-zag movement, but the melakarta form itself is best kept direct and balanced.
notable_features: 11th melakarta; 5th rāgam in the 2nd chakra Netra; mnemonic name Netra-Ma; mnemonic phrase sa ra gi ma pa dha na; shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Suvarnangi (47th melakarta); asampurna equivalent is Kokilāravam in the Venkatamakhin tradition; graha bhedam from Kokilapriya yields Rishabhapriya; the asampurna form is shadava-sampurna and vakra. Vardhani is noted as a janya of Kokilapriya, with R1 omitted in ascent while retaining the descent.

mood: Rupavati has a bright, graceful, and pleasing character, with a sense of elegance suggested by its very name meaning “the beautiful one.” As a melakarta scale, it feels complete and balanced rather than highly dramatic, lending itself to calm appreciation, devotional uplift, and a refined, composed emotional space. It can evoke a serene, aesthetically pleasing atmosphere suitable for contemplative listening and devotional expression.
description: Rupavati is the 12th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system and belongs to the 2nd chakra, Netra. Its mnemonic name is Netra-Sha and its mnemonic phrase is sa ra gi ma pa dhu nu. The scale uses shuddha rishabham, sadharana gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, shatsruthi dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham, making it the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Divyamani, the 48th melakarta. In the older Venkatamakhin asampurna tradition, Rupavati is also the original name of the 12th melakarta, but there it appears as a svarantara-shadava form with a different scale outline. The raga is noted as one of the few ragas given the same name by the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. Rupavati’s notes, when used for graha bhedam, do not yield any melakarta rāgam. It has a few minor janya ragams associated with it.
gamaka_usage: The source does not specify detailed gamaka treatment for Rupavati, so only general melakarta practice can be inferred. The raga’s defining swaras are best rendered with clean, stable intonation to preserve its plain scalar identity: S, P, and often M1 are typically held steady, while R1, G2, D3, and N3 may take light kampita or gentle oscillation as needed for expressive phrasing. Because it is a straightforward sampurna melakarta without vakra movement or anya swaras, excessive gamaka or heavy oscillation would obscure its scale identity; smooth jaru-like transitions between adjacent notes are appropriate, especially in phrases linking M1-P-D3-N3 and N3-D3-P-M1. Compared with more gamaka-rich janya ragas, Rupavati is likely to sound clearer and more scalar, with ornamentation used sparingly and tastefully.
notable_features: Melakarta #12; 6th rāgam in the 2nd chakra Netra; mnemonic phrase sa ra gi ma pa dhu nu; shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Divyamani (#48). In the asampurna (Venkatamakhin) tradition, Rupavati is the original name of the 12th melakarta, but with svarantara-shadava structure: arohana S R1 M1 P S S and avarohana S N3 D3 N3 P M1 G2 S. Graha bhedam on Rupavati does not yield any melakarta rāgam. The text notes that it is one of the few ragas given the same name by the Muthuswami Dikshitar school.

mood: Gayakapriya has a calm, devotional, and dignified mood. Its shuddha rishabham, antara gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and shuddha nishadham give it a clear, restrained brightness rather than intense pathos or romance. The raga feels composed and prayerful, suitable for reflective listening, solemn praise, and an atmosphere of spiritual poise.
description: Gayakapriya is the 13th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system and belongs to the 3rd chakra, Agni, with mnemonic name Agni-Pa and mnemonic phrase sa ra gu ma pa dha na. Its scale is the standard sampoorna melakarta form: arohana S R1 G3 M1 P D1 N1 S and avarohana S N1 D1 P M1 G3 R1 S. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Dhavalambari, the 49th melakarta. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Geyahejjujji. In the original Venkatamakhin asampurna melakarta scheme, Geyahejjujji is the 13th melakarta and uses a vakra shadava-sampurna structure with arohana S R1 M1 G3 M1 P D1 S and avarohana S N1 D1 P M1 G3 R1 S. The raga has a few minor janya ragas, with Kalagada noted as occasionally heard in concerts. A theoretical graha bhedam on Gayakapriya yields Dhatuvardani.
gamaka_usage: Gayakapriya is generally rendered with restrained, phrase-oriented ornamentation rather than heavy oscillation. As a melakarta with shuddha R1, shuddha M1, shuddha D1, and shuddha N1, these swaras are usually treated as relatively stable anchor notes. G3 may take a light kampita or gentle glide in melodic phrases, while D1 and N1 can also receive mild oscillation depending on the prayoga. R1 is typically approached cleanly from S or N1, and M1 is often held steady as a pivot note. Because the raga is sampoorna and structurally straightforward, gamakas are used to shape the line and maintain dignity rather than to create intense emotional tension. Compared with more heavily gamaka-laden ragas, Gayakapriya tends to sound plain, clear, and balanced.
notable_features: Melakarta #13; 1st rāgam in the 3rd chakra Agni; mnemonic name Agni-Pa; mnemonic phrase sa ra gu ma pa dha na; shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Dhavalambari (#49); called Geyahejjujji in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school; original Venkatamakhin asampurna form is vakra and shadava-sampurna; graha bhedam yields Dhatuvardani; Kalagada is a noted janya.

mood: Vakulabharanam has a calm, composed, and devotional character. Its shuddha rishabham, antara gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatam, and kaisiki nishadam create a bright but restrained color that feels orderly and dignified rather than intensely emotional. The raga can evoke serenity, reverence, and a gentle inward devotion, making it suitable for contemplative and prayerful settings. Because it is a melakarta with a complete scale, it also has a balanced, expansive feel that supports both calm reflection and structured melodic elaboration.
description: Vakulabharanam is the 14th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system. It is the 2nd rāgam in the 3rd chakra, Agni, with mnemonic name Agni-Sri and mnemonic phrase sa ri ga ma pa dha ni. Its scale is S R1 G3 M1 P D1 N2 S / S N2 D1 P M1 G3 R1 S, using shuddha rishabham, antara gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatam, and kaisiki nishadham. As a melakarta, it is sampoorna in both ascent and descent. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Nāmanārāyaṇi, the 50th melakarta. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is known as Dhātivasantabhairavi or Vātivasantabhairavi. The asampurna melakarta form Vātivasantabhairavi uses the same notes but with vakra descent: S N2 D1 M1 G3 M1 P M1 G3 R1 S. The raga has minor janya ragas associated with it, and Vasantabhairavi is mentioned as one heard occasionally in concerts. Vakulabharanam’s notes under graha bhedam yield Keeravani, Hemavati, and Kosalam. It corresponds to the Western Phrygian dominant scale and the Hindustani equivalent is raga basant mukhari.
gamaka_usage: The source does not give detailed gamaka rules for Vakulabharanam, but as a melakarta with shuddha rishabham and antara gandharam, the raga is generally rendered with smooth, measured oscillations and clear phrase-based treatment rather than heavy ornamentation. R1 and D1 are typically handled with light kampita or gentle janta-style emphasis in melodic movement, while G3 and N2 are important color tones that should be articulated cleanly. M1 and P are usually steadier anchor notes. In the asampurna form, the vakra descent requires careful jaru-like movement through M1 and G3, giving the raga a more winding contour than the straight melakarta version.
notable_features: Melakarta #14; 2nd rāgam in the 3rd chakra Agni; mnemonic name Agni-Sri; shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Nāmanārāyaṇi (50th melakarta). Asampurna counterpart is Vātivasantabhairavi with vakra descent. Graha bhedam from Vakulabharanam yields Keeravani, Hemavati, and Kosalam. The text also notes a minor janya called Surya/Srothoswini with scale S G3 M1 D1 N2 S / S N2 D1 M1 G3 S.

mood: This is an auspicious, deeply soothing raga with a calm, balanced, and reflective emotional color. It tends to evoke peace, gentle devotion, and a touch of pathos, making the listener feel settled, inward-looking, and emotionally purified rather than agitated. Its character suits early morning and dawn-like settings, where it can suggest freshness, prayer, and quiet contemplation.
description: Mayamalavagowla is the 15th melakarta raga in Venkatamakhin's melakarta system and is one of the most important foundational ragas in Carnatic music. Originally known simply as Malavagowla, the prefix "maya" was added after the advent of the 72-mela scheme. Its number 15 is assigned through the Katapayadi sankhya system. It is the 3rd raga in the 3rd chakra, Agni, with mnemonic name Agni-Go and mnemonic phrase sa ra gu ma pa dha nu. It is a sampurna raga with all seven swaras in both ascent and descent, and it is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Kamavardhini (Pantuvarali), the 51st melakarta. The raga is ancient and highly rakti in nature, with strong melodic appeal. It is also described as a sarva svara gamaka varika rakti raga. It is especially significant pedagogically: all basic swara exercises such as Sarali, Jantai, Dhaattu, and related beginner lessons are traditionally set in this raga, a practice associated with Purandara Dasa's systematization of Carnatic teaching. The first mini-songs (geetam) after basic exercises are often set to Malahari, a janya of this raga. It has many janya ragas, including Bowli, Jaganmohini, Gowla, Gowlipantu, Lalita, Nadanamakriya, Revagupti, Saveri, and Malahari. It corresponds to Bhairav in Hindustani music. The raga is also a murchana karaka mela because graha bhedam on madhyamam and rishabham yields Simhendramadhyamam and Rasikapriya respectively. It is a morning raga, especially suited to the first prātaḥ-sandhyā-kālam (dawn).
gamaka_usage: The gamaka treatment is distinctive and central to the raga's identity. Unlike Shankarabharanam, where the G-M relationship is more direct, in Mayamalavagowla the gandharam is typically oscillated in phrases like M,G M,G M,G while madhyamam is held steady. There is no bold M-P gamaka as in Shankarabharanam. Rishabham and dhaivatam are also usually rendered with oscillation in relation to shadjam and panchamam respectively, so the stable notes are mainly S, M, and P. The nishadam may occasionally be held flat before resolving upward to S for expressive effect. In practice, the raga depends on careful kampita on G, R, D, and controlled jaru-like movement between neighboring notes, while avoiding a heavy, linear treatment that would weaken its characteristic sweetness and symmetry.

notable_compositions.name: Avadh sukhdāyi bājē badhāyī ratan simhāsan par
Kshira sagara shayana raksha shara nivasini

notable_features: Melakarta #15; 3rd chakra Agni; mnemonic name Agni-Go; mnemonic phrase sa ra gu ma pa dha nu. Shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Kamavardhini (Pantuvarali, #51). Ancient, highly auspicious, and a foundational teaching raga for beginners. It eschews vivadi swaras and has uniform spacing and symmetry, which makes it easy to learn. It is a murchana karaka mela: graha bhedam on madhyamam gives Simhendramadhyamam and on rishabham gives Rasikapriya. It has many janya ragas. The text also notes that Muthuswami Dikshitar's first composition is in this raga and mentions his gouḻānta kritis on Goddess Nilothpalamba, one of which is in Mayamalavagowla.

inventor: Venkatamakhin (asampurna form); standardized in the modern melakarta scheme by Govindacharya
mood: Chakravakam has a restrained, devotional, and compassionate mood. It tends to evoke sincerity, humility, sympathy, and a gentle pathos rather than exuberance. The raga feels calm and inward-looking, suitable for devotional contemplation and emotionally tender expression, with a dignified warmth that can also suggest empathy and quiet resolve.
description: Chakravakam or Chakravaham is the 16th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system and the 4th melakarta in the 3rd chakra, Agni. Its mnemonic name is Agni-Bhu and the mnemonic phrase is sa ra gu ma pa dhi ni. The scale is S R1 G3 M1 P D2 N2 S / S N2 D2 P M1 G3 R1 S, making it a sampoorna raga with all seven swaras in both ascent and descent. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, this raga is called Tōyavēgavāhini. It is the suddha madhyamam equivalent of Ramapriya (52nd melakarta). The raga is noted for its devotional and compassionate character and is considered similar to Ahir Bhairav in Hindustani music. In the asampurna melakarta tradition, Tōyavēgavāhini is listed as the 16th melakarta with the same note set and same ascent/descent. Chakravakam has several janya ragas, with Bindumalini, Malayamarutam, and Valaji mentioned as popular ones. It is also theoretically related through graha bhedam, yielding Sarasangi and Dharmavati when the notes are shifted while preserving relative frequencies.
gamaka_usage: The Ri and Ga are rendered a bit flatter than in Mayamalavagowla, giving the raga a more restrained feel. The Da and Ni are treated somewhat like in Hari Kambhoji, contributing to its devotional essence. In practice, the raga favors gentle, measured gamaka rather than heavy ornamentation: R1 and G3 are typically approached with subtle oscillation and careful intonation, while M1 and P are comparatively steady. D2 and N2 should be sung with a smooth, devotional contour, often with light jaru-like movement into and out of the upper tetrachord. The overall treatment should avoid making the scale sound bright or overly forceful; the characteristic effect comes from controlled, slightly flattened phrasing and restrained kampita on the key notes.
notable_features: Melakarta #16; 4th melakarta in the 3rd chakra Agni. Mnemonic name Agni-Bhu; mnemonic phrase sa ra gu ma pa dhi ni. Asampurna name: Tōyavēgavāhini. Suddha madhyamam equivalent of Ramapriya (52nd melakarta). Graha bhedam on Chakravakam yields Sarasangi and Dharmavati. Closely resembles Ahir Bhairav in Hindustani music. The raga is associated with devotion, sympathy, and compassion. The source also notes that it differs from Mayamalavagowla in treatment, with flatter Ri-Ga and Hari Kambhoji-like Da-Ni.

mood: Suryakantam has a bright, radiant, and uplifting character, with a clear and open melodic contour that feels expansive, dignified, and orderly. Its complete, symmetrical scale gives it a sense of steadiness and clarity, so the emotional effect is more one of calm optimism, auspiciousness, and luminous poise than deep yearning or pathos. It suits devotional and contemplative expression well, while also carrying a subtle brilliance and freshness that makes it feel noble and sunlit.
description: Suryakantam, also spelled Sooryakantam, is the 17th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system of Carnatic music. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is known as Chāyāvati. It belongs to the 3rd chakra, Agni, and its mnemonic name is Agni-Ma with the phrase sa ra gu ma pa dhi nu. It is a sampūrṇa scale with the notes shuddha rishabham, antara gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, panchamam, chathusruthi dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Gamanashrama, the 53rd melakarta. In the older asampurna melakarta tradition of Venkatamakhin, Chāyāvati is also the 17th melakarta, but the ascending scale omits panchamam: S R1 G3 M1 D2 N3 S. The raga has several janya rāgams, with Vasantha, Sowrashtram, and Bhairavam noted as popular concert derivatives. Graha bhedam on Suryakantam yields the melakarta rāgams Senavati and Latangi.
gamaka_usage: As a plain melakarta scale, Suryakantam is generally rendered with relatively straightforward, well-defined swaras rather than heavy vakra ornamentation. R1, G3, D2, and N3 are typically given light to moderate oscillation or graceful slides as needed in phrases, while M1 and P are usually held more steadily. Because the scale is symmetrical and non-bhashanga, the raga’s identity comes more from clean phraseology and the characteristic ascent-descent than from special foreign-note handling. Compared with vakra or heavily gamaka-driven ragas, Suryakantam is expected to sound more direct and scalar, with gamakas used tastefully to enliven the notes rather than obscure the scale.
notable_features: Melakarta #17; chakra 3 (Agni); mnemonic name Agni-Ma; mnemonic phrase sa ra gu ma pa dhi nu. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Gamanashrama (#53). In the asampurna melakarta system, Chāyāvati uses the same notes but omits panchamam in the arohana. Graha bhedam from Suryakantam yields Senavati and Latangi. Popular janya ragas associated with it include Vasantha, Sowrashtram, and Bhairavam.

mood: Hatakambari has a bright, forceful, and slightly austere emotional color. Its vivadi note-combination gives it a sense of alertness, classical dignity, and tension that resolves into strength rather than softness. The raga can evoke heroic resolve, wonder at its unusual scale structure, and a serious devotional atmosphere. It is often felt as expansive and disciplined, suitable for music that is contemplative yet powerful.
description: Hatakambari (pronounced Hātakāmbari) is the 18th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system of Carnatic music. It is the 6th rāgam in the 3rd chakra, Agni, with mnemonic name Agni-Sha and mnemonic phrase sa ra gu ma pa dhu nu. Its scale is S R1 G3 M1 P D3 N3 S / S N3 D3 P M1 G3 R1 S, using shuddha rishabham, antara gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, shatsruthi dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham. As a melakarta, it is a sampoorna rāgam with all seven notes in both ascent and descent. It is the shuddha madhyama equivalent of Vishwambari, the 54th melakarta. In the original Venkatamakhin asampurna melakarta scheme, the corresponding raga is Jayashuddhamālavi, where the scale is shadava-sampurna with vakra prayoga: S R1 G3 M1 P N3 S / S N3 D3 N3 P M1 G3 R1 S. Hatakambari has minor janya ragas associated with it. The raga’s notes under graha bhedam yield Gavambhodi. It is a vivadi melakarta because of the R1, G3, D3, and N3 combination.
gamaka_usage: As a vivadi melakarta, Hatakambari is generally rendered with careful, controlled ornamentation rather than heavy kampita. R1 and G3 need precise intonation and are often linked with subtle jaru or light kampita so the scale does not sound harsh. M1 and P are usually steadier anchor notes. D3 and N3 are also handled with caution, often approached through smooth slides in descent and clean articulation in ascent. In the melakarta form the scale is non-vakra and fully sampurna, so scalar movement is possible, but the raga’s identity depends on exact placement of the vivadi swaras and smooth transitions between adjacent notes.
notable_features: Hatakambari is the 18th melakarta and the 6th raga of the Agni chakra. It is the shuddha madhyama counterpart of Vishwambari (54th melakarta). In the asampurna melakarta tradition it is called Jayashuddhamālavi and is shadava-sampurna with vakra descent. Graha bhedam on Hatakambari yields Gavambhodi. The raga is associated with a few minor janya ragas. It is a vivadi melakarta due to the presence of R1, G3, D3, and N3.

mood: Jhankaradhvani has a calm, devotional, and composed character. As a melakarta with a straightforward sampurna scale, it tends to feel balanced and orderly rather than highly dramatic. The shuddha madhyamam and the relatively soft contour give it a serene, reflective quality that suits prayerful or contemplative settings, with an undercurrent of gentle auspiciousness.
description: Jhankaradhvani, also spelled Jhankaradhwani, is the 19th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is known as Jhankārabhramari. It is the 1st rāgam in the 4th chakra, Veda, with mnemonic name Veda-Pa and mnemonic phrase sa ri gi ma pa dha na. Its standard scale is S R2 G2 M1 P D1 N1 S / S N1 D1 P M1 G2 R2 S, making it a sampoorna melakarta. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Shamalangi (55th melakarta). In the older asampurna melakarta tradition of Venkatamakhin, the same scale is treated with vakra prayoga: S R2 G2 M1 P D1 N1 D1 P D1 S / S N1 D1 P M1 G2 R2 G2 R2 S. The raga has a few minor janya ragas associated with it. Graha bhedam on Jhankaradhvani yields Ratnangi and Gamanashrama. Compositions mentioned in this raga include works by Thyagaraja, Koteeswara Iyer, Dr. M. Balamuralikrishna, and Muthuswami Dikshitar.
gamaka_usage: As a plain melakarta, Jhankaradhvani is best approached with relatively restrained gamaka treatment compared with more ornate janya ragas. The source text does not specify detailed gamaka rules, but by inference the stable scale tones R2, G2, M1, D1, and N1 are rendered cleanly with light oscillation where stylistically appropriate. Since it is not described as vakra in the standard form, phrases should remain direct and scalar, avoiding heavy kampita that would obscure the raga’s straightforward identity. In the asampurna version, the zig-zag prayogas imply more phrase-based handling, but the core note set remains the same.

notable_compositions.name: jhankAradhvani rAgA sannutE shankari pAlaya candramukhI

notable_features: Melakarta #19; first rāgam in chakra 4 (Veda); mnemonic name Veda-Pa; phrase sa ri gi ma pa dha na. Shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Shamalangi (#55). In the asampurna melakarta tradition it is called Jhankārabhramari and uses vakra prayoga. Graha bhedam yields Ratnangi and Gamanashrama. The text notes that it has a few minor janya ragams, but does not name them.

mood: Natabhairavi has a grave, expansive, and dignified emotional color. It is described as inciting feelings of grandeur and devotion, so the listener experiences reverence, inner stillness, and a solemn devotional uplift rather than lightness or playfulness. The raga can also carry a gentle pathos, giving it a contemplative, prayerful, and emotionally deep character.
description: Natabhairavi is the 20th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Shanmukhapriya (melakarta 56) and corresponds to the Natural minor scale (Aeolian mode) in Western music. In Hindustani music it corresponds to Asavari thaat. It is the 2nd rāgam in the 4th chakra, Veda, with mnemonic name Veda-Sri and mnemonic phrase sa ri gi ma pa dha ni. The scale is sampoorna, using all seven swaras in both ascent and descent. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, this melakarta is known as Nārīrītigowla. The raga is noted for its grandeur and devotion, but it has not found as much prominence in concerts as Kalyani, Todi, Sankarabharanam, or Kharaharapriya. Its notes and general prayoga are said to resemble Shanmukhapriya except for madhyamam, and it can be confused with Shanmukhapriya if the phrase from panchamam to gandharam is emphasized.
gamaka_usage: Natabhairavi is generally treated in a relatively direct, scalar manner because it is a sampoorna melakarta with no vakra prayoga in its standard form. The source material does not prescribe a special gamaka scheme, but the raga is best brought out with clear emphasis on R2, G2, D1, and N2, using smooth jaru between adjacent notes and restrained oscillation rather than heavy kampita on every swara. M1 and P are typically stable anchor notes. The phraseology around P to G2 is important, since the text notes that Natabhairavi can resemble Shanmukhapriya if that region is handled in a certain way; thus, careful phrase choice matters more than elaborate gamaka density. Overall, the gamaka treatment should preserve the raga's sober, dignified minor-mode color.
notable_features: Melakarta #20; 4th chakra Veda; mnemonic name Veda-Sri; mnemonic phrase sa ri gi ma pa dha ni. Shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Shanmukhapriya (#56). In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Nārīrītigowla. Graha bhedam on Natabhairavi yields five other major melakarta ragas: Kalyani, Sankarabharanam, Hanumatodi, Kharaharapriya, and Harikambhoji. The text also notes that Natabhairavi is less commonly featured as a main concert raga than Kalyani, Todi, Sankarabharanam, and Kharaharapriya. Its standard form is sampoorna and non-vakra, while the asampurna version Nārīrītigowla uses vakra prayoga in both ascent and descent.

mood: Keeravani carries a plaintive yet serene and deeply expressive mood. It evokes devotion, introspection, and a gentle sense of longing or pathos, while also retaining an elegant, composed, and nocturnal calm. The raga often feels contemplative and emotionally rich, with a harmonic-minor-like color that can suggest solemnity, tenderness, and quiet drama.
description: Keeravani is the 21st melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system and the 3rd rāgam in the 4th chakra, Veda. Its mnemonic name is Veda-Go and the mnemonic phrase is sa ri gi ma pa dha nu. The scale is sampoorna, using all seven notes in both ascent and descent: S R2 G2 M1 P D1 N3 S and S N3 D1 P M1 G2 R2 S. It uses chathusruthi rishabha, sadharana gandhara, shuddha madhyama, panchama, shuddha dhaivatha, and kakali nishadha. Keeravani is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Simhendramadhyamam, the 57th melakarta. It is also known in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school as Kiraṇāval̥i. The rāgam is popular in western music as the harmonic minor scale, and the source notes that it is said to have been borrowed into Hindustani music from Carnatic music. Keeravani has many janya rāgams associated with it, including Kalyanavasantam, Chandrakouns, Sāmapriya, and Vasantamanohari. Its notes, when shifted by graha bhedam, yield Hemavati, Vakulabharanam, and Kosalam.
gamaka_usage: As a melakarta, Keeravani is generally rendered with clear, well-defined swaras rather than heavy oscillation on every note. R2 and G2 are important and are typically given gentle kampita or nuanced slides, with G2 especially shaped carefully in phrases like S R2 G2 M1 and M1 G2 R2 S. D1 and N3 are also handled with controlled gamaka and smooth jaru transitions, especially in the descent N3 D1 P M1 G2 R2 S. M1 and P are usually held steady as anchor notes. The raga’s identity depends on the precise handling of the minor-third-like G2 and the leading-tone-like N3, so these notes should not be rendered too flat or too heavily oscillated in a way that blurs the scale’s harmonic-minor flavor.

notable_compositions.name: Bhaktha Palana Seya
notable_compositions.composer: Ponniah Pillai of the Tanjore Quartet

notable_compositions.name: nijamuga rAma nI pAdamula nitya nammina nnau brOvumu

notable_features: Melakarta #21; chakra Veda; mnemonic name Veda-Go; mnemonic phrase sa ri gi ma pa dha nu. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Simhendramadhyamam (#57). The Muthuswami Dikshitar school names the 21st melakarta as Kiraṇāval̥i. Graha bhedam from Keeravani yields Hemavati, Vakulabharanam, and Kosalam. The raga is widely recognized for its harmonic minor resemblance and has many janya ragas, with Kalyanavasantam specifically noted as a popular janya.

inventor: Govindacharya standardized the present melakarta form; the name may originally have been Harapriya and was later adjusted to fit the Katapayadi formula.
mood: Kharaharapriya has a deeply plaintive, tender, and expansive emotional character. It evokes pathos, compassion, and a gentle inward-looking sadness rather than harsh grief. The raga often feels fluid and unhurried, creating a reflective atmosphere suited to devotion, longing, and emotional depth. Listeners may experience a sense of calm melancholy, warmth, and spaciousness, with imagery of quiet evening introspection or a serene devotional setting.
description: Kharaharapriya is the 22nd melakarta rāga in the 72-melakarta system and the 4th rāga in the 4th chakra, Veda, with mnemonic name Veda-Bhu and mnemonic phrase sa ri gi ma pa dhi ni. It is a sampoorna rāgam with the scale S R2 G2 M1 P D2 N2 S / S N2 D2 P M1 G2 R2 S. The notes are chatushruti rishabham, sadharana gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, chatushruti dhaivatam, and kaisiki nishadam. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Hemavati, the 58th melakarta, and its prati madhyamam equivalent is Hemavati. The raga is known for its even spacing, versatility, and flexibility, allowing elaborate melodic improvisation and long ālāpanās. It is strongly associated with Karuna rasa and is typically rendered in slow, medium, or medium-fast tempo to bring out bhava. The name may have originally been Harapriya, with etymologies linking it to Shiva (Hara) or to Rama as the slayer of Khara. Saint Tyagaraja is credited with reviving and popularizing this ancient raga through many compositions. It has many janya ragas, including Abheri, Abhogi, Andolika, Brindavana Saranga, Kāpi, Madhyamavati, Mukhari, Reetigowla, Shree, Dhanashree, Udayaravichandrika, and Sriranjani. In graha bhedam, its notes yield five other major melakarta ragas: Kalyani, Hanumatodi, Natabhairavi, Shankarabharanam, and Harikambhoji.
gamaka_usage: The raga permits several kinds of gamakas and is treated with a fluid, flexible approach. R2 and M1 can be held relatively steady without heavy oscillation, while G2, D2, and N2 are typically given gentle to moderate kampita and slide-based treatment to preserve the raga’s emotive depth. Jaru phrases and smooth transitions are important, especially in phrases moving through G2–M1, M1–P, and P–D2–N2. The raga’s even spacing allows gamakas to be used liberally, but they should remain graceful rather than forceful. Compared with more bhashanga or vakra ragas, Kharaharapriya’s gamaka treatment is more open and expansive, supporting long, flowing phrases and elaborate ālāpanā.

notable_compositions.name: Sri Rama Nee Namam Emi Ruchira

notable_features: It is the 4th rāga in the 4th chakra (Veda), with mnemonic phrase sa ri gi ma pa dhi ni. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Hemavati and the prati madhyamam equivalent relationship is explicitly noted. The raga is highly versatile because of its even swara spacing and broad gamaka freedom. It is one of the melakarta scales with a very large number of janya ragas. Graha bhedam on Kharaharapriya yields Kalyani, Hanumatodi, Natabhairavi, Shankarabharanam, and Harikambhoji. The text also notes that Tyagaraja revived this ancient raga in the 18th century. A film-song example cited is 'Arādhisuve madanāri' from Babhruvahana (1977), and M.S. Viswanathan used the raga in songs such as 'Maharajan Ullagai' and 'Maadhavi Ponmayilal'.

mood: Gourimanohari has a calm, graceful, and emotionally balanced character. Its shuddha madhyamam and smooth sampurna scale give it a composed, dignified feel that can sound devotional and serene, yet also capable of gentle pathos and reflective tenderness. It often evokes a poised, inward-looking mood rather than intense drama, making it suitable for contemplative listening, prayerful settings, and lyrical expression with a soft, flowing emotional contour.
description: Gourimanohari or Gaurimanohari is the 23rd melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, the corresponding 23rd melakarta is called Gourivelāvaḻi. It is the 5th rāgam in the 4th chakra, Veda, with mnemonic name Veda-Ma and mnemonic phrase sa ri gi ma pa dhi nu. Its scale is S R2 G2 M1 P D2 N3 S / S N3 D2 P M1 G2 R2 S. The notes are shadjam, chatushruti rishabham, sadharana gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, panchamam, chatushruti daivatam, and kakali nishadham. It is a sampurna rāgam and the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Dharmavati (59th melakarta). The Western equivalent given is the melodic minor scale, and the Hindustani equivalent is Raag Patdeep. The raga has a few minor janya ragams associated with it. Graha bhedam on Gourimanohari yields three other major melakarta ragams: Vachaspati, Natakapriya, and Charukesi.
gamaka_usage: The source text does not specify gamaka treatment directly, but as a melakarta with R2, G2, D2, and N3, the raga is typically rendered with smooth, well-controlled oscillations and slides rather than heavy kampita on every note. R2 and G2 are usually given gentle gamaka or jaru-based connection, M1 and P are generally held steady as structural anchors, and D2 and N3 are often approached with delicate oscillation or glide to preserve the raga’s soft, reflective color. The scale should sound fluid and balanced, avoiding harsh emphasis that would disturb its calm, melodic character.
notable_features: Melakarta #23; 5th rāgam in the 4th chakra Veda; mnemonic name Veda-Ma; mnemonic phrase sa ri gi ma pa dhi nu. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Dharmavati (59th melakarta). Graha bhedam yields Vachaspati, Natakapriya, and Charukesi. The Muthuswami Dikshitar school uses the alternate name Gourivelāvaḻi for the same melakarta position. The text also notes a modern musical tribute, 'Shades of Blue: A Musical Tribute to Venmurasu,' as being considered a definition/exploration of the raga’s expressive range.

mood: Varunapriya has a poised, balanced, and luminous character. Its complete seven-note structure gives it an orderly, expansive feel, while the combination of chatushruti rishabham, sadharana gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, shatsruti dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham lends a restrained brightness rather than exuberance. The raga can evoke calm contemplation, devotional uplift, and a dignified sense of clarity, making it suitable for reflective and prayerful expression.
description: Varunapriya is the 24th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system of Carnatic music. It is the 6th rāgam in the 4th chakra, Veda, and its mnemonic name is Veda-Sha; the mnemonic phrase is sa ri gi ma pa dhu nu. It is also called Viravasantam in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. Its scale is sampoorna, with the notes S R2 G2 M1 P D3 N3 S / S N3 D3 P M1 G2 R2 S. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Neetimati, the 60th melakarta. Varunapriya is structurally complete and symmetrical in note content, and the available sources note that it does not have janya ragas associated with it. A graha bhedam on Varunapriya yields Ragavardhini. The raga is recognized as a monsoon-associated scale in some modern discussions, and it is represented in compositions by Tyagaraja, Koteeswara Iyer, M. Balamuralikrishna, and Muthuswami Dikshitar.
gamaka_usage: The provided sources do not specify a detailed gamaka grammar for Varunapriya. As a melakarta, its swaras are generally rendered in a clear, stable manner so the scale identity remains prominent. S, M1, and P are typically held steady, while R2, G2, D3, and N3 may take light kampita or gentle oscillation depending on phrase context. Since it is not described as vakra or bhashanga, gamakas should support the plain scalar outline rather than obscure it. Compared with more heavily ornamented janya ragas, Varunapriya is best approached with measured, restrained ornamentation.
notable_features: Melakarta #24; 4th chakra Veda; mnemonic name Veda-Sha; mnemonic phrase sa ri gi ma pa dhu nu; shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Neetimati (#60); no janya ragas listed in the provided sources; graha bhedam from Varunapriya yields Ragavardhini; called Viravasantam in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school.

mood: Mararanjani has a calm, composed, and devotional character. Its scale creates a restrained and serene atmosphere, with a poised inwardness that suits reflective and prayerful expression. The raga tends to feel dignified and unforced rather than dramatic, evoking quiet devotion, balance, and a gentle sense of stillness.
description: Mararanjani (pronounced māraranjani) is the 25th Melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system of Carnatic music. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Sharāvati. It is the 1st rāgam in the 5th chakra, Bana, with mnemonic name Bana-Pa and mnemonic phrase sa ri gu ma pa dha na. Its scale is sampoorna by definition, with all seven notes in both ascent and descent: S R2 G3 M1 P D1 N1 S and S N1 D1 P M1 G3 R2 S. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Kantamani, the 61st melakarta. The raga has a few minor janya ragas associated with it, including the better-known Janasammodini. A theoretical relationship noted in the source is that Mararanjani’s notes, when shifted by graha bhedam, yield Vanaspati, a minor melakarta rāgam.
gamaka_usage: The source does not specify detailed gamaka treatment, but as a melakarta with shuddha madhyamam and shuddha dhaivatham/nishadham, the raga is generally rendered with clear, well-defined swaras and smooth scalar movement. R2, G3, D1, and N1 are typically approached with gentle oscillation or light kampita as needed for melodic expression, while M1 and P are usually held more steadily. Because the scale is straight and sampoorna, jaru-based transitions between adjacent notes are natural, and there is no indication of vakra or anya-swara handling. Compared with more heavily gamaka-driven ragas, Mararanjani is likely to favor balanced, measured ornamentation rather than extreme oscillation.
notable_features: Melakarta number 25; 5th chakra Bana; mnemonic name Bana-Pa; mnemonic phrase sa ri gu ma pa dha na; shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Kantamani (61st melakarta); associated janya ragas include Janasammodini; graha bhedam from Mararanjani yields Vanaspati.

mood: Charukesi has a poignant, devotional, and emotionally touching character. It often evokes a sense of tender longing, quiet sorrow, and inward reflection, yet it remains graceful and immediately recognizable. The raga can feel prayerful and contemplative, suiting devotional settings as well as music that seeks to express pathos with elegance rather than heaviness. It is also described as suitable for singing at all times, giving it a broad, balanced emotional appeal.
description: Charukesi is the 26th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system and belongs to the 5th chakra, Bana, with mnemonic name Bana-Sri and mnemonic phrase sa ri gu ma pa dha ni. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Tarangini. It is a sampoorna rāgam with all seven swaras in both ascent and descent, and is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Rishabhapriya (melakarta 62). Its scale corresponds to the Aeolian dominant / Mixolydian b6 type in Western theoretical terms. The raga is widely used in devotional music and is generally easy to identify by ear. The source notes that only a few minor janya rāgams are associated with Charukesi. It has notable graha bhedam relationships: shifting the tonic yields Vachaspati, Natakapriya, and Gourimanohari. The raga is also popular in Hindustani music and has inspired many film songs and modern adaptations across languages. The text mentions that some renderings of Muthuswami Dikshitar's Maye Tvam Yahi are sung with a higher shatshruthi rishabham than the original chathushruthi rishabham. It is often appreciated for its close-to-the-heart, prayerful quality and its ability to convey emotion with clarity and elegance.
gamaka_usage: Charukesi is typically rendered with smooth, expressive gamakas that support its poignant devotional mood. R2 and G3 are usually given gentle oscillation and connected by graceful slides, while M1 and P are comparatively steadier anchor notes. D1 and N2 are often handled with light kampita or jaru to preserve the raga's plaintive color. S and P are generally held cleanly as structural points, while the characteristic color comes from the soft inflection around R2-G3 and D1-N2. Because the raga is sampurna and not vakra, its identity depends more on characteristic phrase-shapes and emotive oscillation than on zig-zag scale motion. Compared with closely related major ragas, Charukesi’s treatment tends to be softer and more lyrical, avoiding overly forceful stress on any note.
notable_features: Melakarta 26; 2nd rāgam in the 5th chakra Bana; mnemonic name Bana-Sri; called Tarangini in the Dikshitar school; shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Rishabhapriya (62nd melakarta); equivalent to an Aeolian dominant / Mixolydian b6 scale; used in devotional music; described as poignant and easy to identify; graha bhedam from Charukesi yields Vachaspati, Natakapriya, and Gourimanohari; some modern and film-song adaptations exist in multiple languages; the text notes a variant rendering of Maye Tvam Yahi with higher shatshruthi rishabham in popular practice.

mood: Sarasangi has a serene, polished, and expansive mood with a bright yet composed emotional color. As a melakarta built on the harmonic-major-like interval pattern, it often feels graceful, uplifting, and slightly luminous, evoking calm devotion as well as a sense of refined wonder. It can suggest a dignified, prayerful atmosphere rather than intense pathos, making it suitable for contemplative listening and for music that feels balanced, elegant, and emotionally clear.
description: Sarasangi is the 27th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system and belongs to the 5th chakra, Bana, with mnemonic name Bana-Go and mnemonic phrase sa ri gu ma pa dha ni. It is a sampurna rāgam with all seven swaras in both ascent and descent. The scale uses chathusruthi rishabham (R2), antara gandharam (G3), shuddha madhyamam (M1), shuddha dhaivatham (D1), and kakali nishadham (N3). In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Sowrasena. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Latangi, the 63rd melakarta. In Western music it is identified with the Harmonic major scale. The raga has minor janya ragas associated with it, notably Kamala manohari and Nalinakanti. Its notes, when shifted by graha bhedam, yield the melakarta ragas Dharmavati and Chakravakam.
gamaka_usage: Sarasangi is generally rendered with restrained, elegant gamaka treatment rather than heavy oscillation. R2, G3, D1, and N3 may take light kampita or gentle oscillatory touches in sangatis, while M1 and P are usually kept comparatively steady as structural anchor notes. Jaru and smooth slides are common in phrase transitions, especially in descent, but the raga’s identity depends on the precise intonation of G3, D1, and N3, so these swaras should not be over-blurred. Compared with more gamaka-dense ragas, Sarasangi tends to sound cleaner and more linear, with controlled ornamentation that preserves its bright, harmonic-major-like character.
notable_features: Melakarta #27; 3rd rāgam in the 5th chakra Bana; mnemonic name Bana-Go; called Sowrasena in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Latangi (#63). Graha bhedam from Sarasangi yields Dharmavati and Chakravakam. The raga is sampurna and non-vakra, with no anya swaras or vivadi swaras in its basic form.

mood: Harikambhoji has a smooth, balanced, and melodious character with a calm, reassuring warmth. It tends to feel open and unforced rather than intensely dramatic, making it suitable for devotional and lyrical expression. The raga can evoke a gentle sense of contentment, grace, and serenity, with a dignified classical poise that supports both contemplative and pleasing musical moods.
description: Harikambhoji is the 28th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system and the 4th rāgam in the 5th chakra, Bana. Its mnemonic name is Bana-Bhu and its mnemonic phrase is sa ri ga ma pa dha ni sa. The scale is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Vachaspati (64th melakarta). Historically, the Wikipedia text links it to ancient Tamil music: the pentatonic Mullaipann evolved into the seven-note Sempaalai by adding ma and ni, and Sempaalai pann is said to correspond to Harikambhoji. It is also identified as the Carnatic equivalent of Hindustani Khamaj thaat and the Western Mixolydian mode. Harikambhoji has many janya ragas, with Kambhoji being the most famous and frequently sung; Mohanam is also noted as a janya. The raga is theoretically significant because graha bhedam on its notes yields five other major melakarta ragas: Kalyani, Sankarabharanam, Natabhairavi, Kharaharapriya, and Hanumatodi. The text also notes that in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is known as Harikedāragowla. Additional modern references describe Harikambhoji as one of the ancient ragas with roots in folk tradition, reinforcing its long-standing place in Carnatic music.
gamaka_usage: Harikambhoji is generally rendered with smooth, flowing ornamentation rather than heavy vakra effects. As a plain sampoorna melakarta, it supports clear, balanced prayogas with relatively stable S, P, and M1. The rishabha (R2), gandhara (G3), dhaivata (D2), and nishada (N2) are typically given gentle Carnatic gamaka treatment—often subtle kampita or light oscillation—so the raga retains its warm, rounded character. The gandhara and nishada especially benefit from soft oscillation or slide-like movement, while the madhyama and panchama are usually held steady. Compared with more heavily ornamented janyas such as Kambhoji, Harikambhoji itself is less dependent on complex zig-zag phrases and more on clean, well-shaped scalar movement.
notable_features: Harikambhoji is a sampoorna melakarta with all seven swaras in both ascent and descent. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Vachaspati (64th melakarta). The raga is historically linked to ancient Tamil Sempaalai and is considered the Carnatic counterpart of Hindustani Khamaj and the Western Mixolydian mode. It is the 4th rāgam in the 5th chakra Bana, with mnemonic name Bana-Bhu. Graha bhedam on Harikambhoji yields Kalyani, Sankarabharanam, Natabhairavi, Kharaharapriya, and Hanumatodi. Its janya ragas include the famous Kambhoji and Mohanam, and the text also lists Kuntalavarali as a janya with scale S M1 P D2 N2 D2 S / S N2 D2 P M1 S. Modern references also describe it as an ancient raga with roots in folk tradition.

mood: Śankarābharaṇaṃ has a mellifluous, smooth, and majestic character. It evokes a calm, expansive, and dignified emotional space, often felt as serene confidence rather than intense pathos. Because of its balanced scale and broad scope for elaboration, it can suggest devotion, grace, and grandeur, making it suitable for both contemplative and celebratory settings. Listeners often experience it as uplifting, polished, and classically complete, like a broad open landscape with clear light.
description: Dhīraśankarābharaṇaṃ, commonly known as Śankarābharaṇaṃ, is the 29th Melakarta rāga in the 72-melakarta system. It is a sampurna rāga with all seven notes in both ascent and descent: S R2 G3 M1 P D2 N3 S / S N3 D2 P M1 G3 R2 S. The scale corresponds to Bilaval in Hindustani music and to the Western major scale (Ionian mode), making it one of the most widely recognized and universally resonant scales. The rāga is described as mellifluous, smooth, and ideal for melodious yet laid-back majestic presentation. It is glorified as 'Sarva Gamaka Māṇika Rakti Rāgaṃ' because of its rich gamaka possibilities. It is the 5th rāga in the 5th chakra, Bāṇa, with mnemonic name Bāṇa-Ma. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of the 65th melakarta, Kalyāṇi. The rāga has an exceptionally large number of janya rāgas, many of which are highly popular and independently important. The source also notes that its notes are evenly spaced, giving excellent scope for elaboration, interpretation, and phrase exploration. In practice, it is one of the most frequently chosen major concert rāgas, along with Todi, Kalyāṇi, and Kharaharapriya. The rāga is also associated with the white keys of a piano/organ/keyboard in simplified form.
gamaka_usage: This rāga is especially known for extensive gamaka usage; the source explicitly calls it 'Sarva Gamaka Māṇika Rakti Rāgaṃ'. The swaras are not treated as plain fixed pitches in performance: R2, G3, D2, and N3 commonly take kampita and oscillatory ornamentation, while M1 and P are often held more steadily as structural anchors. Jaru/slide phrases are important in connecting S–R2–G3–M1 and M1–G3–R2–S, and in descending phrases through N3–D2–P–M1. Because the scale is symmetrical and evenly spaced, the raga tolerates rich ornamentation without losing identity. Compared with more austere ragas, Sankarabharanam is less about sparse note choice and more about graceful, flowing gamaka-laden phrases. The major scale framework allows both gentle and grand treatment, but the raga should still sound smooth and dignified rather than heavily forceful.
notable_features: Melakarta number 29; 5th rāga of the 5th chakra Bāṇa; mnemonic name Bāṇa-Ma; shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Kalyāṇi (65th melakarta). Its notes under graha bhedam yield five other major melakarta rāgas: Kalyāṇi, Hanumatodi, Natabhairavi, Kharaharapriya, and Harikambhoji. The 7th graha bhedam produces a structure with both madhyamams and no panchamam, so it is not a valid melakarta. The rāga is noted for having a very large number of janya rāgas and for being one of the most popular and foundational scales in Carnatic music. It is also said that many compositions in this rāga exist by almost all composers. The source mentions 39 Nottu Svara compositions by Muthuswami Dikshitar based on Western major scale notes.

mood: Naganandini has a poised, bright, and expansive character, with the clean symmetry of a complete melakarta scale. Its ascent and descent feel orderly and luminous, creating an atmosphere of serenity, devotion, and quiet majesty. The higher R2, G3, D3, and N3 lend it an uplifting, auspicious color rather than a plaintive or heavy one, so the raga often suggests inner steadiness, reverence, and a gentle sense of wonder.
description: Naganandini is the 30th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system of Carnatic music. It is the 6th rāgam in the 5th chakra, Bana, with mnemonic name Bana-Sha and mnemonic phrase sa ri gu ma pa dhu nu. Its scale is S R2 G3 M1 P D3 N3 S / S N3 D3 P M1 G3 R2 S, using chathusruthi rishabham, antara gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, shatsruthi dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham. As a melakarta, it is a sampoorna rāgam with all seven notes in both ascent and descent. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Chitrambari, the 66th melakarta. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Nagābharanam. The raga has a few minor janya ragas associated with it. Graha bhedam on Naganandini yields two other minor melakarta rāgams: Bhavapriya and Vagadheeshwari.
gamaka_usage: The source text does not specify detailed gamaka prayogas, but as a melakarta with a straightforward sampoorna structure, the swaras are generally rendered with clear scalar movement and controlled ornamentation. R2, G3, D3, and N3 are typically handled with the characteristic Carnatic oscillatory treatment appropriate to their positions, while M1 and P are usually steadier anchor notes. Because the scale is clean and non-vakra, phrases tend to move smoothly by stepwise jaru-like transitions rather than heavy zig-zag ornamentation. Compared with ragas that use anya swaras or vakra phrases, Naganandini’s identity comes more from its exact swara set and the bright color of the higher notes than from special phrase-specific gamaka rules.
notable_features: Melakarta #30; 6th rāgam in the 5th chakra Bana; mnemonic name Bana-Sha; mnemonic phrase sa ri gu ma pa dhu nu; called Nagābharanam in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school; shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Chitrambari (66th melakarta); graha bhedam yields Bhavapriya and Vagadheeshwari; has minor janya ragas associated with it.

mood: Yagapriya has a serene, composed, and devotional character. Its scale is bright yet restrained, with a calm dignity that can evoke quiet devotion, inner stillness, and a sense of auspiciousness.
