The raga tends to feel orderly and balanced rather than emotionally turbulent, making it suitable for reflective, prayerful, and spiritually centered moods.
description: Yagapriya is the 31st melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system and belongs to the 6th chakra, Rutu, with mnemonic name Rutu-Pa and mnemonic phrase sa ru gu ma pa dha na. It is a sampoorna scale with all seven notes in both ascent and descent: S R3 G3 M1 P D1 N1 S / S N1 D1 P M1 G3 R3 S. The raga uses shatsruthi rishabham, antara gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and shuddha nishadham. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is known as Kalāvati. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Sucharitra, the 67th melakarta. The source notes that Kalāvati has also been adopted in Hindustani music. Yagapriya has a few minor janya ragas associated with it, though none are listed in the provided text. Its notes, when subjected to graha bhedam, do not yield any melakarta ragas.
gamaka_usage: The provided text does not specify detailed gamaka treatment. As a melakarta with a straightforward sampurna structure, the swaras are generally rendered in a clean, scalar manner. R3 and G3 are typically handled with gentle oscillation or connected movement in practice, while M1 and P are usually steadier anchor notes. D1 and N1 are also expected to be treated carefully and distinctly, with smooth jaru-like transitions between adjacent notes rather than heavy kampita. Since the source gives no special prayoga rules, no unusual gamaka restrictions can be asserted beyond standard melakarta intonation.
notable_features: It is the 1st rāgam in the 6th chakra Rutu. The source explicitly states that it is called Kalāvati in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Sucharitra (67th melakarta). Graha bhedam on Yagapriya does not produce any melakarta rāgams. The raga is a melakarta, so it is by definition sampoorna and uses the same seven notes in ascent and descent.

mood: Ragavardhini has a rare, striking, and intellectually arresting melodic color. Its vivadi structure gives it a tense brightness and a sense of alertness, so the listener often experiences it as serious, energetic, and dignified rather than soft or leisurely. It can suggest intense devotion, inward resolve, and a subtle sense of wonder born from its unusual scalar personality. In elaboration, it evokes an atmosphere of exploration and expansion, with a feeling of forward movement and concentrated emotional force.
description: Ragavardhini (also transliterated Ragavardini) is the 32nd melakarta raga in the Carnatic music system and a janaka raga in the 72-melakarta scheme. It belongs to the 6th chakra, Veda. Its scale is sampurna in both ascent and descent: S R3 G3 M1 P D1 N2 S / S N2 D1 P M1 G3 R3 S. As a melakarta, it contains all seven swaras in both directions and serves as a parent scale for derived janya ragas, though specific popular janyas are not identified in the provided material. Ragavardhini is not a commonly heard melakarta in mainstream concert practice, and its identity is shaped strongly by its vivadi content, especially the presence of R3 in conjunction with G3. This gives the raga a distinctive, uncommon melodic personality that stands apart from more frequently performed melakartas. Like other melakarta ragas, its full character emerges not merely from the scalar outline but from phraseology, intonation, and careful handling in alapana, composition, and improvisation.
gamaka_usage: Ragavardhini requires careful and disciplined gamaka handling because of its vivadi structure. The R3-G3 zone is the most delicate area: R3 must be rendered with precise intonation and clearly separated from G3, without excessive smearing that would blur the raga’s identity. G3 can take a measured kampita, while R3 is best approached with restraint, often as a sharply defined swara or with a light oscillatory touch rather than heavy shaking. D1 and N2 may also admit controlled kampita or subtle nokku depending on phrase context, especially in descending contours such as N2-D1-P. M1 and P generally act as steadier anchoring notes and can serve as nyasa points. Jaru phrases can be effective in descent, especially around N2-D1-P and G3-R3-S, but the slides should preserve the exact swara positions. In ascent, scalar clarity is important, and over-ornamentation can weaken the raga’s uncommon profile. Overall, the raga benefits from a balance of plain-note definition and selective ornamentation: enough gamaka to give life and Carnatic identity, but not so much that the vivadi intervals lose their sharp character.
notable_features: Melakarta 32 in the 72-melakarta system; belongs to the Veda chakra. It is a sampurna janaka raga with a non-vakra scale and vivadi content, notably the use of R3. The raga is considered relatively uncommon in concert usage. Graha bhedam on Ragavardhini yields Varunapriya, as noted in the provided source snippet. No confirmed Hindustani equivalent, time association, or composer-specific canonical compositions were established from the provided material.

mood: Gangeyabhushani has a bright yet uncommon melodic color shaped by the close R3–G3 relationship and the contrast of D1 with N3. The raga can create a contemplative, devotional, and slightly austere atmosphere, with an undercurrent of wonder because of its unusual scalar flavor. Rather than sounding overtly romantic or playful, it tends to suggest inward focus, seriousness, dignity, and a subtle spiritual intensity. In elaboration, it can feel meditative and poised, while still retaining a distinctive tension that gives it individuality among melakarta ragas.
description: Gangeyabhushani is a Carnatic melakarta raga, specifically Melakarta number 33 in the standard 72-melakarta system. It is a sampurna melakarta, containing all seven swaras in both ascent and descent: S R3 G3 M1 P D1 N3 S / S N3 D1 P M1 G3 R3 S. It belongs to the Rtu chakra, being the third melakarta in that group. As a janaka raga, it serves as a parent framework from which janya ragas may be derived. Its note material gives it a distinctive vivadi color, especially because of the use of shatsruti rishabha alongside antara gandhara. Like other melakarta ragas, Gangeyabhushani is not merely a scale but a melodic entity whose identity emerges through characteristic handling of swaras, phraseology, and gamaka.
gamaka_usage: Gangeyabhushani requires careful handling of its swaras to bring out raga bhava and to avoid reducing it to a bare scalar pattern. The most sensitive area is the lower tetrachord, where R3 and G3 occur in close proximity; these should be intoned with precision, with controlled kampita and measured emphasis so the vivadi character is preserved without blurring the distinction between the two notes. G3 can take a gentle oscillation, while R3 is best treated with restraint and clarity rather than heavy shaking. M1 and P generally act as relatively stable resting or anchoring tones. In the upper tetrachord, D1 is often approached from P with a soft slide or delicate oscillation, and N3 can be highlighted with a mild kampita resolving to the upper shadja. Jaru connections in phrases such as P D1 N3 >S and >S N3 D1 P help smooth the raga’s angular intervallic feel. Descending phrases like M1 G3 R3 S should be rendered with care so that the R3–G3 region remains distinct and aesthetically balanced. Overall, gamakas in this raga should be deliberate, not overly dense, emphasizing precision, repose on M1/P, and subtle oscillatory treatment on the more sensitive swaras.
notable_features: Gangeyabhushani is the 33rd melakarta and belongs to the Rtu chakra. It is a sampurna janaka raga with the scale S R3 G3 M1 P D1 N3 S / S N3 D1 P M1 G3 R3 S. Its use of the vivadi swara relationship in the R3–G3 region gives it a distinctive and somewhat tense scalar color. Alternate transliterations include Gangeyabhushani and gAngEyabhUshaNi. No specific time-of-day assignment, Hindustani equivalent, or confirmed composition list is supported by the provided snippets.

mood: Vagadheeswari has a bright yet composed melodic personality. Its use of R3 and G3 gives it a striking, incisive color, while the shuddha madhyamam and kaisiki nishadam soften the scale into something dignified rather than harsh. The raga can evoke a devotional and contemplative atmosphere, with a sense of intellectual clarity and quiet wonder. It suits reflective, prayerful, and mildly majestic expression rather than overtly romantic or tragic moods.
description: Vagadheeswari is a Carnatic melakarta raga and the 34th melakarta in the 72 melakarta system. It is a parent scale (janaka raga) and therefore a sampurna raga, containing all seven swaras in both ascent and descent. It is the 4th raga in the 6th chakra, Rutu, with mnemonic name Rutu-Bhu and mnemonic phrase 'sa ru gu ma pa dhi ni'. Its scale is S R3 G3 M1 P D2 N2 S / S N2 D2 P M1 G3 R3 S. The swaras used are shatsruthi rishabham, antara gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, chathusruthi dhaivatham and kaisiki nishadham, along with shadjam and panchamam. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, it is called Bhogachhāyānāţa. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Nasikabhooshani, the 70th melakarta. In Western terms, it corresponds to the Mixolydian sharp 2 scale/mode. Vagadheeswari has a few minor janya ragas, of which Magadhi and Mohanangi are occasionally heard; Chayanata is also mentioned as a janya raga. Through graha bhedam, its notes yield two other minor melakarta ragas: Naganandini and Bhavapriya.
gamaka_usage: As a sampurna melakarta with a straight scalar structure, Vagadheeswari can accommodate both plain-note handling and ornamented treatment. The raga identity is strongly shaped by the R3-G3 area, which must be handled with clarity so that the unusual bright opening tetrachord is unmistakable. R3 is not usually dwelt on with heavy oscillation in the manner of more gamaka-laden rakti ragas; instead it is often approached and released cleanly, sometimes with a light kampita or a subtle glide into G3. G3 can take measured kampita and serves as an important color-bearing swara, but excessive oscillation that blurs the R3-G3 distinction should be avoided. M1 and P are comparatively steady nyasa points and help stabilize the raga's otherwise sharp-edged purvanga. D2 and especially N2 in descent admit gentle kampita or slide-based treatment, with phrases such as N2-D2-P and P-M1-G3-R3 benefiting from smooth jaru movement. The raga tolerates plain-note sancharas well, and this plainness is in fact useful in establishing its melakarta outline; over-decoration can obscure its scalar identity. Characteristic handling therefore lies in balancing clean scalar movement with selective ornamentation: light to moderate kampita on G3 and N2, restrained treatment of R3, stable M1 and P, and graceful descending jarus across the upper notes. Compared with heavier rakti ragas, Vagadheeswari is best projected through lucid phrase architecture, slow-to-medium movement, and a dignified, devotional repose rather than dense gamaka saturation.
notable_features: Called Bhogachhāyānāţa in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. Mnemonic name: Rutu-Bhu. Mnemonic phrase: sa ru gu ma pa dhi ni. Graha bhedam on this scale yields Naganandini and Bhavapriya. It is the shuddha madhyamam counterpart of Nasikabhooshani. In Western scale terminology, it corresponds to the Mixolydian #2 mode. Though the source says it has a few minor janya ragas, Magadhi and Mohanangi are specifically noted as occasionally heard, and Chayanata is also mentioned as a janya raga.

mood: Shulini has a bright, forceful, and striking melodic personality. With its sharp R3–G3 combination and luminous N3, it can evoke a sense of brilliance, alertness, and uplift, often suggesting majesty, energy, and a slightly austere devotional intensity rather than softness or deep pathos. The raga can feel expansive and intellectually vivid, suiting compositions of praise, invocation, and dignified spiritual expression. Its mood may be experienced as bold and radiant, with an undercurrent of wonder and reverence.
description: Shulini is a Carnatic melakarta ragam, the 35th in the 72 melakarta system. It is also spelled Sulini, Shoolini, or Soolini. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, it is called Shailadesākshi or Shailadeshi. It is the 5th ragam in the 6th chakra, Rutu, with the mnemonic name Rutu-Ma and mnemonic phrase 'sa ru gu ma pa dhi nu'. Its scale is sampurna in both ascent and descent: S R3 G3 M1 P D2 N3 S / S N3 D2 P M1 G3 R3 S. The swaras used are shatsruthi rishabham, antara gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, chathusruthi dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham, along with shadjam and panchamam. As a melakarta, it contains all seven notes in both directions. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Kosalam, the 71st melakarta. Shulini has a couple of minor janya ragams associated with it. Through graha bhedam, its notes yield three other melakarta ragams: Shanmukhapriya, Dhenuka, and Chitrambari.
gamaka_usage: The source material does not give a codified gamaka lakshana for Shulini, but its handling can be described from its melakarta structure and tonal color. Since it is a straight sampurna melakarta without vakra movement or anya swaras, phrases are generally rendered with clear scalar integrity. The R3–G3 region is crucial: these two adjacent bright swaras should not be treated too blandly, as their close placement gives the raga much of its identity. R3 is typically approached with a light oscillatory touch or as a passing, energized note leading into G3, while G3 can take a slightly firmer kampita than R3. M1 and P function as comparatively stable nyasa points and can be held more plainly. In the upper tetrachord, D2 and especially N3 contribute to the raga’s brilliance; N3 may be given a gentle kampita or a glide into upper S, and phrases like D2 N3 >S and >S N3 D2 P should be shaped with brightness rather than heavy curvature. Descending phrases such as N3 D2 P and M1 G3 R3 S need careful intonation so that the sharp R3 is preserved distinctly and does not blur toward softer handling. Jaru between adjacent notes may be used tastefully, but over-ornamentation can obscure the raga’s direct, radiant contour. Overall, Shulini favors controlled kampita, light slides, and crisp scalar prayogas over dense gamaka-laden treatment, with emphasis on clarity, brilliance, and the energetic color of the R3–G3 and D2–N3 zones.
notable_features: 5th ragam in the 6th chakra Rutu; mnemonic name Rutu-Ma; mnemonic phrase 'sa ru gu ma pa dhi nu'. Called Shailadesākshi or Shailadeshi in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. It has a couple of minor janya ragams. Graha bhedam on Shulini yields the melakarta ragams Shanmukhapriya, Dhenuka, and Chitrambari. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Kosalam (melakarta 71).

mood: Chalanata has a bright, forceful, and majestic emotional color. Its sharp swara set, especially the presence of R3 and D3, gives it a striking, brilliant, and energetic feel that can suggest heroism, grandeur, and ceremonial splendor. At the same time, because it is closely associated with the Nattai family and used in compositions of devotional and concert significance, it can also evoke a devotional uplift and a sense of awe. The raga suits an atmosphere of invocation, radiance, and confident proclamation rather than introspective softness.
description: Chalanata is a Carnatic melakarta raga, the 36th in the 72 melakarta system. It is a janaka raga and, as a melakarta, is by definition sampurna, containing all seven swaras in both ascent and descent. It is the 6th raga in the 6th chakra, Rutu, with mnemonic name Rutu-Sha and mnemonic phrase 'sa ru gu ma pa dhu nu'. Its scale is S R3 G3 M1 P D3 N3 S / S N3 D3 P M1 G3 R3 S, employing shadjam, shatsruthi rishabham, antara gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, panchamam, shatsruthi dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham. It is the shuddha madhyamam equivalent of Rasikapriya, the 72nd melakarta. The raga is one of the few referred to by the same name in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school of Carnatic music. Chalanata has popular janya ragas associated with it, notably Nata (Nattai) and Ghambheeranata (Gambheeranattai), and Nata is said to overshadow Chalanata in concert usage. Being a vivadi melakarta because of the R3-G3 and D3-N3 proximity, it has comparatively fewer direct kritis, with composers often favoring its janya ragas for broader melodic treatment. Through graha bhedam, Chalanata yields the major melakarta raga Shubhapantuvarali.
gamaka_usage: Chalanata is generally handled with a bold, bright, and highly precise gamaka approach. Because it is a vivadi melakarta with the close note pairs R3-G3 and D3-N3, the swaras must be intoned very clearly; excessive smearing can blur the raga identity. S, M1, and P function as comparatively steady nyasa or anchoring points. R3 and D3 need especially careful treatment: they are usually given firm emphasis, often with controlled kampita rather than wide oscillation, so that they do not collapse into neighboring swaras. G3 and N3 may also take light kampita or spurita-like emphasis in brisk phrases, but their positions should remain sharply defined. Janta prayogas, assertive briga-oriented movement, and direct scalar runs suit the raga well. Slides and jarus may be used sparingly between adjacent swaras, but the overall handling should preserve the raga's crisp, radiant contour rather than a heavily curved or languorous texture. In comparison with its janya Nattai, Chalanata is approached more as a complete melakarta framework, with straighter sampurna movement and less dependence on characteristic vakra phrases; the gamakas support majesty and brilliance without obscuring the exact swara-sthanas.
notable_features: One of the few ragas referred to by the same name in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. It belongs to the 6th chakra Rutu and has mnemonic name Rutu-Sha. Its mnemonic phrase is 'sa ru gu ma pa dhu nu'. It is the shuddha madhyamam counterpart of Rasikapriya. Graha bhedam on Chalanata yields Shubhapantuvarali. Though it has notable janya ragas such as Nata/Nattai and Ghambheeranata/Gambheeranattai, Nata is more prominent in concert practice and tends to overshadow Chalanata. As a vivadi melakarta, it has relatively fewer direct kritis compared with more frequently elaborated melas, and musicians often explore its musical personality through its better-known janya ragas.

mood: Salagam projects a grave, inward, and austere emotional atmosphere. Its vivadi swara structure and prati madhyama color give it a tense yet meditative quality, often suggesting spiritual concentration rather than overt sweetness. The listener may experience a sense of stillness, devotional seriousness, and restrained poignancy, with an undercurrent of introspection. It suits contemplative, weighty expression and can evoke the feeling of early-morning inwardness or solemn prayer.
description: Salagam (pronounced Sālagam) is a Carnatic melakarta raga and the 37th melakarta in the 72-melakarta system. It is a sampurna janaka raga, containing all seven swaras in both ascent and descent: S R1 G1 M2 P D1 N1 S / S N1 D1 P M2 G1 R1 S. It belongs to the 7th chakra, Rishi, and is the first raga in that chakra. Salagam is the prati madhyama equivalent of Kanakangi, the 1st melakarta. As a melakarta, it serves as a parent scale for derived janya ragas, though it is not among the most frequently heard melakartas in mainstream concert practice. Its identity is shaped not merely by its scalar form but by the careful handling of its vivadi swaras and the serious melodic personality that emerges from them.
gamaka_usage: Salagam requires highly disciplined gamaka handling because of its vivadi structure, especially the close R1-G1 and D1-N1 groupings. These swaras should be intoned with great precision so that the raga does not collapse into an indistinct chromatic effect. R1 and G1 are typically rendered with controlled kampita or delicate oscillation rather than flat delivery; the oscillation must be narrow and well-centered, preserving the identity of each swara. Similarly, D1 and N1 benefit from careful, weighty treatment, often with restrained kampita and subtle linking motion. M2 is an important color-bearing swara and may be approached through a gentle glide from G1 or held with measured steadiness to highlight the prati madhyama flavor. P functions as a stable anchoring note and can serve as a point of repose amid the tension of the vivadi clusters. Jaru between adjacent swaras may be used sparingly to connect phrases, but excessive sweeping slides can blur the raga’s delicate intonational profile. In general, Salagam favors deliberate, weighty phrases, precise oscillations, and restrained ornamentation over fast scalar passages; compared with more common melakartas, its gamakas must be more carefully calibrated to preserve clarity and raga bhava.
notable_features: Salagam is a melakarta (parent) raga in Carnatic music and therefore a janaka/asraya raga. It is heptatonic in both ascent and descent and belongs to the 7th chakra, Rishi, as its first member. It is the prati madhyama equivalent of Kanakangi, the 1st melakarta; conversely, Kanakangi is the shuddha madhyama equivalent of Salagam. Since it contains the vivadi combinations R1-G1 and D1-N1, it uses vivadi swaras and demands especially careful intonation. Available sources note that Salagam has only a minor janya presence and is not widely represented by famous mainstream compositions in the cited material.

mood: Jalarnavam has a grave, inward, and slightly austere melodic personality, with a contemplative stillness arising from its shuddha rishabham, shuddha gandharam, prati madhyamam, and shuddha dhaivatham. The raga can evoke a sense of vastness and depth—apt for its name meaning 'ocean'—suggesting meditative quiet, devotional introspection, and a subtle wonder. Its color is not overtly bright or romantic; rather, it suits reflective, serious, and spiritually tinged expression.
description: Jalarnavam is a Carnatic melakarta raga, the 38th in the 72 melakarta system. It is the 2nd raga in the 7th chakra, Rishi, and its mnemonic name is Rishi-Sri; the mnemonic phrase is sa ra ga mi pa dha ni. The name Jalarnavam means 'the ocean'. It is also called Jaganmohanam in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school of Carnatic music. Its scale is sampurna in both ascent and descent: S R1 G1 M2 P D1 N2 S / S N2 D1 P M2 G1 R1 S, using shuddha rishabham, shuddha gandharam, prati madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and kaisiki nishadham. As a melakarta, it contains all seven swaras in both directions by definition. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Ratnangi, the 2nd melakarta. Jalarnavam has only a minor janya raga associated with it. In graha bhedam, it yields no other melakarta raga, like all six ragas in the Rishi chakra; the others mentioned are Salagam, Jhalavarali, Navaneetam, Pavani, and Raghupriya. These ragas are noted for having a gap of three notes between G1 and M2, a feature stated not to occur in any other melakarta by definition.
gamaka_usage: The available sources do not give a codified gamaka lakshana for Jalarnavam, but its vivadi melakarta structure strongly suggests careful, weighty handling rather than plain scalar execution. R1 and especially G1 need delicate treatment so the close lower tetrachord does not sound harsh or flattened; these swaras are best approached with controlled kampita or subtle nokku rather than being struck too bluntly. The leap-like span from G1 to M2 is a defining color of the raga and should be negotiated with sensitivity, often using a gentle connecting jaru or a measured emphasis on M2 as a bright but serious pivot. D1 and N2 in the upper tetrachord can take mild oscillation, particularly in descent, while S and P function as comparatively steady nyasa points. Because Jalarnavam is a vivadi melakarta, overuse of plain-note scalar runs can make it sound skeletal; the raga benefits from restrained oscillation, curved phrase endings, and linking slides that preserve its grave, oceanic depth. Compared with smoother non-vivadi ragas, Jalarnavam demands more precision in intonation and more nuanced gamaka on the sensitive swaras R1, G1, D1, and N2.
notable_features: Called Jaganmohanam in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. The name Jalarnavam means 'the ocean'. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Ratnangi. Graha bhedam on Jalarnavam does not yield any other melakarta raga. It belongs to the Rishi chakra, whose six ragas are stated to uniquely contain a three-note gap between G1 and M2. It has only a minor janya raga associated with it.

mood: Jhalavarali has a rare, austere, and slightly intense melodic personality shaped by its vivadi swaras and the striking leap between G1 and M2. It can evoke a feeling of inward devotion, gravity, and contemplative stillness, while also carrying an unusual, almost mystical brightness because of the prati madhyamam and kakali nishadam. The raga may suggest a mood of solemn prayer, yearning, and spiritual wonder rather than lightness or romance, making it suitable for reflective and serious musical expression.
description: Jhalavarali is a Carnatic melakarta raga, the 39th in the 72 melakarta system. It is pronounced Jhālavarāḻi and is said to mean 'A moon with the sun's heat'. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school of Carnatic music, it is called Dhālivarāḻi (also written Dhalivarali). The prefix 'Jhala' is stated to have been added to the raga name to suit the katapayadi formula. It is the 3rd raga in the 7th chakra, Rishi, with mnemonic name Rishi-Go and mnemonic phrase 'sa ra ga mi pa dha nu'. Its scale is S R1 G1 M2 P D1 N3 S / S N3 D1 P M2 G1 R1 S, employing shuddha rishabham, shuddha gandharam, prati madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham. As a melakarta, it is by definition a sampurna raga with all seven notes in both ascent and descent. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Ganamoorti, the 3rd melakarta. Jhalavarali has a few janya ragas, of which Varali is very popular. The raga belongs to a special group within the Rishi chakra whose graha bhedam does not yield any other melakarta raga. Like the other ragas of this chakra, it contains a distinctive gap of three notes between G1 and M2, a feature stated to be unique among melakartas.
gamaka_usage: The source material does not give a formal lakshana-style gamaka prescription for Jhalavarali, but its vivadi structure implies that gamaka handling must be careful, weighty, and identity-preserving rather than rendered as a plain scalar pattern. R1 and especially G1 should be treated with controlled kampita or delicate oscillation so that the lower tetrachord does not sound flat or generic. The unusual and highly characteristic jump from G1 to M2 must remain clearly profiled; this interval should not be blurred by excessive smoothing. M2 acts as a bright point of contrast and can be given emphasis as a nyasa or pivot tone after the tense lower swaras. P is generally a stable, comparatively plain resting note. D1 may take restrained oscillation, while N3 is an important upper-color note that can be approached with precision and emphasis in both ascent and descent. Jaru between adjacent notes may be used sparingly and with exact intonation, but overuse can weaken the raga's austere contour. Overall, the raga benefits from distinct articulation of swaras, measured kampita on the vivadi tones, and a serious, deliberate handling that preserves its rare and solemn melodic personality.
notable_features: It is the 3rd raga in the 7th chakra Rishi. Mnemonic name: Rishi-Go. Mnemonic phrase: sa ra ga mi pa dha nu. It is the prati madhyamam counterpart of Ganamoorti. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Dhālivarāḻi / Dhalivarali. The prefix 'Jhala' is said to have been added to suit the katapayadi formula. Graha bhedam on Jhalavarali does not produce any other melakarta raga. It is one of the six ragas in the Rishi chakra—along with Salagam, Jalarnavam, Navaneetam, Pavani, and Raghupriya—that have a gap of three notes between G1 and M2; this is stated to be unique among melakartas.

mood: Navaneetam has a quiet, inward, and somewhat rarefied emotional color. Its use of the vivadi combination R1–G1 along with prati madhyamam gives it a serious, contemplative, and slightly austere melodic personality rather than an overtly bright one. The raga can suggest devotional introspection, serene stillness, and a gentle touch of yearning or tenderness. It suits meditative, scholarly, and spiritually reflective settings more than festive exuberance, and can evoke the feeling of calm prayer, soft supplication, or a dignified inner solitude.
description: Navaneetam is a Carnatic melakarta raga, the 40th in the 72 melakarta system. It is pronounced navanītam. The name is glossed in the source as meaning 'the eternal new one', and Navaneetham is also explained as a Sanskrit word meaning fresh butter, from 'nava' meaning fresh/new and 'neetham' meaning butter. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, this raga is called Nabhomani. It is the 4th raga in the 7th chakra, Rishi; its mnemonic name is Rishi-Bhu and the mnemonic phrase is 'sa ra ga mi pa dhi ni'. Its scale is S R1 G1 M2 P D2 N2 S / S N2 D2 P M2 G1 R1 S, using shuddha rishabham, shuddha gandharam, prati madhyamam, chatushruti dhaivatam, and kaisiki nishadam. As a melakarta, it is by definition a sampurna raga with all seven notes in both ascent and descent. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Vanaspati, the 4th melakarta. The source states that Navaneetam does not yet have a janya raga associated with it. In graha bhedam, shifting its notes yields no other melakarta raga, like all six ragas in the Rishi chakra. The source specifically notes that only these ragas have a gap of three notes between G1 and M2, a feature not occurring in any other melakarta by definition.
gamaka_usage: As a melakarta with the vivadi swara pair R1 and G1, Navaneetam generally calls for careful, nuanced handling rather than plain scalar rendering. R1 and G1 are typically treated with delicate kampita or restrained oscillatory shading so that the vivadi character is heard clearly without sounding harsh. The R1–G1 region should not be attacked too bluntly; these notes are better approached with measured intonation and subtle inflection. M2, the prati madhyama, is an important color-bearing swara and can be sustained more steadily, often serving as a point of repose after the tense lower tetrachord. P functions as a firm anchoring note with comparatively plain treatment. D2 and N2 may be rendered with light kampita or smooth jaru-based approach in phrase movement, especially in descent such as N2–D2–P and >S–N2–D2–P. Because of the unusual three-note gap between G1 and M2, the transition across this span is a defining feature of the raga; phrases traversing G1 to M2 should be handled distinctly and with care, often as a clear leap or controlled glide rather than blurred intonation. As a non-vakra sampurna melakarta, the scale is linear, but effective rendition depends on phrase-sensitive gamaka, especially around R1 and G1, and on preserving the contrast between the oscillated lower swaras and the steadier M2–P region. Excessively brigha-heavy treatment can weaken the raga’s grave, contemplative identity.
notable_features: Called Nabhomani in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. It is the 4th raga in the 7th chakra Rishi, with mnemonic name Rishi-Bhu and mnemonic phrase 'sa ra ga mi pa dhi ni'. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Vanaspati (4th melakarta). The source states that it has no janya raga associated with it yet. Under graha bhedam, it yields no other melakarta raga, like the other ragas of the Rishi chakra: Salagam, Jalarnavam, Jhalavarali, Pavani, and Raghupriya. A distinctive theoretical feature is the three-note gap between G1 and M2, said to occur only in these six ragas and in no other melakarta by definition.

inventor: Venkatamakhin (melakarta scheme); also known as Kumbhini in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school
mood: Pavani carries a purified, austere, and luminous emotional color, fitting its meaning 'the purifier.' Its combination of R1 and G1 with prati madhyamam gives it a serious, inward, and slightly rarefied atmosphere rather than an overtly romantic or playful one. The raga can suggest spiritual cleansing, contemplation, and a sense of sacred intensity. To a listener, it may feel meditative and dignified, with an unusual brightness from N3 balanced by the gravity of the lower swaras. It suits reflective, devotional, and intellectually engaging musical settings.
description: Pavani is a Carnatic melakarta raga, the 41st in the 72 melakarta system. The name is pronounced pāvani and means 'the purifier.' In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, it is known as Kumbhini. It is the 5th raga in the 7th chakra, Rishi, with mnemonic name Rishi-Ma and mnemonic phrase 'sa ra ga mi pa dhi nu.' Its scale is sampurna, containing all seven swaras in both ascent and descent: S R1 G1 M2 P D2 N3 S / S N3 D2 P M2 G1 R1 S. The swaras used are shuddha rishabham, shuddha gandharam, prati madhyamam, chathusruthi dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham, along with shadjam and panchamam. Pavani is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Manavati, the 5th melakarta. A janya raga associated with it is Chandrajyoti, in which Tyagaraja composed a couple of songs. The raga's graha bhedam does not yield any other melakarta. This is explained by the characteristic three-note gap between G1 and M2, a feature shared by the Rishi chakra ragas; under melakarta rules, such shifts produce only invalid scales.
gamaka_usage: As a melakarta with the vivadi combination R1-G1 and the sharp contrast of M2, Pavani is best handled with careful, weighty gamaka treatment rather than plain scalar rendering. The lower swaras R1 and G1 need delicate but precise handling, typically with restrained kampita or subtle oscillation, so that the vivadi color is heard clearly without becoming harsh. These two swaras should not be rendered too flatly or too heavily shaken; their intonation must remain exact. The movement from G1 to M2 is especially important: the wide interval can be approached with a measured jaru or a clearly articulated leap, and M2 should emerge with brightness and firmness as a point of contrast after the dense lower tetrachord. P is generally a stable nyasa-like resting point, while D2 is usually treated more plainly, with moderate ornamentation. N3 can take a mild emphasis or light kampita when approaching the upper shadjam, and the N3-S ascent benefits from a smooth upward glide. Descending phrases through N3 D2 P and P M2 G1 R1 should preserve the raga's gravity and avoid overly decorative brigas that can blur the vivadi identity. In general, Pavani demands controlled gamakas, measured slides, and a dignified gait; excessive plain-note scalar singing makes it sound skeletal, while excessive ornamentation can obscure its rare melodic personality.
notable_features: Meaning of the name: 'the purifier.' Mnemonic name: Rishi-Ma. Mnemonic phrase: 'sa ra ga mi pa dhi nu.' It is the 5th raga in the 7th chakra Rishi. It uses the vivadi swara set R1 and G1. Pavani's graha bhedam does not produce any other valid melakarta. The explanation given is that the Rishi chakra ragas have a three-note gap between G1 and M2, and no other swara pair in melakarta rules has such a gap, so graha bhedam leads only to invalid scales. In the Dikshitar school, the raga is called Kumbhini. It is the prati madhyamam counterpart of Manavati. Available snippet evidence also associates the raga with auspicious early-morning rendering.

mood: Raghupriya has a striking and uncommon emotional color created by its vivadi swaras and prati madhyama. Its sound can feel austere, intense, and inward-looking, often suggesting a blend of devotional seriousness, poignancy, and wonder. The raga may evoke a contemplative atmosphere rather than easy sweetness, giving the listener a sense of spiritual tension resolving into calm. It suits reflective, meditative, and intellectually engaging musical treatment, where unusual tonal shades create both emotional depth and a sense of rare beauty.
description: Raghupriya is the 42nd melakarta raga in the Carnatic system and therefore a janaka (parent) raga. It belongs to the Rishi chakra and is the 6th raga in that chakra. As a melakarta, it contains all seven swaras in both ascent and descent and is a sampurna raga, with the scale S R1 G1 M2 P D3 N3 S / S N3 D3 P M2 G1 R1 S. Since it follows the straight ascending and descending order of notes, it is not vakra. The swara set includes the vivadi combination R1-G1, giving the raga a rare and distinctive tonal identity. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, this raga is known as Ravikriya. The name Raghupriya means 'the one dear to Raghu'. It is also the prati madhyama equivalent of Tanaroopi, the 6th melakarta. As a melakarta, it serves as a theoretical and compositional basis for derived ragas, though specific janyas are not identified in the provided snippets.
gamaka_usage: Because Raghupriya contains vivadi swaras, especially the close R1-G1 cluster, gamaka handling must be careful and precise. R1 and G1 are best treated with controlled kampita or subtle oscillation rather than broad, careless shaking, so that their identity remains clear and the raga does not blur into neighboring melas. The R1-G1 region especially demands clean intonation and measured handling, since over-sliding can obscure the vivadi character. M2 can take a gentle emphasis and often acts as an important point of color contrast against the lower vivadi region. P is generally a stable resting note, and D3-N3 may be rendered with measured glide or light oscillation depending on phrase context. Overall, the raga benefits from deliberate phrase architecture, restrained ornamentation, and clarity of swara placement; gamakas should highlight its unusual scalar personality without smudging the note identities.
notable_features: Raghupriya is a melakarta (janaka) raga with a complete seven-note scale in both ascent and descent. It is non-vakra in the given scale form and uses vivadi swaras, notably the R1-G1 combination, which gives it a rare and challenging melodic personality. It belongs to the Rishi chakra and is the 6th raga in that chakra. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Ravikriya. It is the prati madhyama equivalent of Tanaroopi (6th melakarta). According to the provided source, graha bhedam on Raghupriya does not yield another melakarta raga, like the other ragas in the Rishi chakra. No specific time association, Hindustani equivalent, Western equivalent, or confirmed compositions are established in the provided snippets.

mood: Gavambhodi evokes a grave, inward, and somewhat austere emotional atmosphere. The combination of shuddha rishabha, sadharana gandhara, prati madhyama, shuddha dhaivata, and shuddha nishada gives it a shaded, contemplative color rather than an extroverted brilliance. It can feel meditative and devotional, with a quiet undercurrent of yearning and pathos. The raga suits introspection, prayerful expression, and serious melodic exploration, creating an impression of stillness, restraint, and spiritual depth.
description: Gavambhodi is the 43rd melakarta raga in the Carnatic system. It is a sampurna melakarta, containing all seven swaras in both ascent and descent: S R1 G2 M2 P D1 N1 S / S N1 D1 P M2 G2 R1 S. It belongs to the 8th chakra, Veda. The raga is also known as Gavambodhi, and in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Geervani or Girvani. The name Gavambodhi has been glossed as meaning 'the teacher of the cows.' As a melakarta, it serves as a parent scale for derived janya ragas. Its swara material includes vivadi-associated positions, giving it a distinctive serious and somewhat austere melodic personality.
gamaka_usage: Gavambhodi should not be rendered as a bare scalar pattern despite being a sampurna melakarta. Its identity depends on careful handling of the vivadi-flavored swaras R1, G2, D1, and N1. R1 is delicate and is best given a controlled kampita or a slightly curved approach from S rather than a hard, plain intonation. G2 can also take gentle oscillation, often heard in relation to R1 and resolving toward M2; the R1–G2 region needs especially precise intonation because of the close spacing. M2 is comparatively steadier and can function as a point of repose, while P remains a firm nyasa-like anchor. In the upper tetrachord, D1 and N1 are usually treated with restraint, with subtle kampita and curved descent rather than flat, prolonged emphasis; phrases like N1 D1 P and D1 P are more convincing when shaped with slight jaru. Jaru and smooth connecting movements help preserve the raga's introspective color, especially in descent from N1 to D1 to P and from G2 to R1 to S. Excessively plain rendering can make the scale sound skeletal, while overdone brigas may blur the raga's serious character; measured oscillation, precise intonation, and restrained slides are therefore central. Compared with brighter prati-madhyama melas, Gavambhodi relies more on shaded gamaka treatment than on overt brilliance.
notable_features: Melakarta 43 in the 8th chakra Veda; a sampurna parent raga in Carnatic music. It is also known as Gavambodhi, and in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school as Geervani or Girvani. It uses the swara set R1 G2 M2 D1 N1 along with S and P, giving it a vivadi-flavored tonal color. Graha bhedam on Gavambhodi yields Hatakambari. As a melakarta, it has all seven notes in both arohana and avrohana and is non-vakra in the scale provided.

mood: Bhavapriya has a serious, inward, and devotional emotional color. Its combination of shuddha rishabham, sadharana gandharam, prati madhyamam, and shuddha dhaivatham gives it a slightly austere and meditative feel, often suggesting prayer, introspection, and gentle pathos rather than brightness or exuberance. The raga can evoke the mood of quiet worship, reflective solitude, and emotional surrender, making it suitable for compositions addressed to deities, especially Shiva. Listeners may experience it as contemplative, tender, and spiritually charged, with an undercurrent of yearning softened by calmness.
description: Bhavapriya is a Carnatic melakarta raga meaning 'the one dear to Bhava (Shiva)'. It is the 44th melakarta in the 72 melakarta system and is the 2nd raga in the 8th chakra, Vasu. Its mnemonic name is Vasu-Sri and its mnemonic phrase is 'sa ra gi mi pa dha ni'. The scale is sampurna in both ascent and descent: S R1 G2 M2 P D1 N2 S / S N2 D1 P M2 G2 R1 S. The swaras used are shuddha rishabham, sadharana gandharam, prati madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and kaisiki nishadham, along with shadjam and panchamam. As a melakarta, it contains all seven notes in both directions by definition. Bhavapriya is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Hanumatodi (Todi), the 8th melakarta. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, it is called Bhavani. It has a minor janya raga associated with it. Through graha bhedam, Bhavapriya yields two other minor melakarta ragas: Vagadheeswari and Naganandini.
gamaka_usage: As a melakarta with the R1-G2 and D1-N2 combinations and as the prati madhyama counterpart of Todi, Bhavapriya is best handled with nuanced gamaka-rich treatment rather than plain scalar rendering. R1 and G2 are important identity-bearing swaras and are typically given oscillated, weighty treatment rather than being sung as flat scale notes; the lower tetrachord especially needs repose and curved handling. M2 is a key color note in this raga and can be sustained with controlled emphasis, often serving as a point of repose that distinguishes the raga from its shuddha-madhyama counterpart. P is generally steady and acts as a stable anchor. D1 and N2 benefit from gentle kampita and curved approaches, especially in descent, and phrases such as N2-D1-P and M2-G2-R1 are naturally effective when rendered with jaru-like linkage rather than broken articulation. The raga's character emerges through measured oscillation, subtle slides, and a serious gait; overly plain, bright, or brisk scalar treatment can weaken its identity and reduce it to a mere note-sequence. In alapana and kriti rendering, the raga responds well to gamaka shaping that preserves its austere, devotional, and introspective flavor.
notable_features: Called Bhavani in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. Mnemonic name: Vasu-Sri. Mnemonic phrase: sa ra gi mi pa dha ni. It is the prati madhyama equivalent of Hanumatodi. Graha bhedam on Bhavapriya yields Vagadheeswari and Naganandini. The source notes that it has a minor janya raga associated with it, though the janya is not named. Its swara set corresponds to the harmonic minor scale in Western scalar comparison.

mood: Shubhapantuvarali has a deeply plaintive, intense, and emotionally charged character. It is widely associated with sorrow, yearning, and inward reflection, and can move a listener toward feelings of grief, supplication, and spiritual surrender. At the same time, its gravity can also create a meditative stillness after emotional release, making it suitable for earnest prayer, lament, and profound introspection. It often evokes the atmosphere of twilight-like inwardness or a solitary devotional appeal, where pain and devotion coexist.
description: Shubhapantuvarali is a Carnatic raga meaning 'the auspicious moon'. It is the 45th melakarta raga in the 72 melakarta system and the 3rd raga in the 8th chakra, Vasu. Its mnemonic name is Vasu-Go and its mnemonic phrase is 'sa ra gi mi pa dha nu'. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Shivapantuvarali. As a melakarta, it is by definition a sampurna raga, containing all seven notes in both ascent and descent. Its scale is S R1 G2 M2 P D1 N3 S / S N3 D1 P M2 G2 R1 S, employing shuddha rishabham, sadharana gandharam, prati madhyamam, panchamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Dhenuka, the 9th melakarta. In Hindustani music, its equivalent is Todi thaat. The raga is noted for its sad tonal quality and is usually used for sad songs by musicians. It also has a few minor janya ragas associated with it. Through graha bhedam, shifting its notes yields the melakarta raga Chalanata.
time_of_day: morning (often associated with the Hindustani Todi time, though also performed at other times in Carnatic practice)
gamaka_usage: Shubhapantuvarali depends heavily on gamaka for its identity and emotional force; plain scalar rendering weakens the raga considerably. The raga is generally treated with weighty, slow-moving ornamentation and curved phrases rather than dry note-by-note movement. R1 and G2 are especially sensitive and are usually rendered with pronounced kampita/oscillation, often approached in close relation so that the R1–G2 region carries much of the raga’s anguish and tension. D1 likewise takes a strong oscillated treatment, and N3 is often given an expressive, lingering kampita before resolving to >S or descending toward D1. M2 is a crucial color swara in this raga and should not be treated casually; it is often sustained with depth or reached through gentle jaru, helping establish the prati-madhyama brightness against the otherwise dark tonal canvas. P functions as a comparatively steady nyasa/resting point and can provide structural repose amid the intense oscillations on the other swaras. Jaru slides between N3-D1-P and M2-G2-R1 are important in evocative handling, and phrases gain power when the oscillations are broad and deliberate rather than brisk. In comparison with lighter ragas, Shubhapantuvarali requires more gravitas: the kampita on R1, G2, D1, and N3 should be substantial, while P remains relatively plain and stabilizing. The raga’s pathos emerges from sustained intonation, heavy gamaka, and careful handling of the R1-G2-M2 and D1-N3 zones.
notable_features: Pronounced śubhapantuvarāḻi. Meaning given as 'the auspicious moon'. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, the raga is known as Shivapantuvarali. It is the 3rd raga in the 8th chakra Vasu. Mnemonic name: Vasu-Go. Mnemonic phrase: 'sa ra gi mi pa dha nu'. It is the prati madhyamam counterpart of Dhenuka (melakarta 9). Graha bhedam on this raga yields Chalanata. The raga is especially associated with sad songs. A Tamil film song explicitly mentioned as set in this raga is 'Vaigaraiyil' from the soundtrack of Payanangal Mudivathillai. The Hindustani equivalent is identified as Todi/thaat Todi, and some sources associate that family with a morning performance time.

mood: Shadvidamargini has a serious, inward, and contemplative emotional color. Its combination of shuddha rishabham, sadharana gandharam, prati madhyamam, and kaisiki nishadham gives it a restrained and meditative feel, often suggesting devotion, introspection, and gentle pathos rather than overt brightness. It can evoke the atmosphere of quiet prayer, philosophical reflection, or a solemn temple setting, making it suitable for compositions of reverence and inner seeking.
description: Shadvidamargini is a Carnatic raga and the 46th melakarta ragam in the 72 melakarta system. The name is glossed as 'the one with the path to six forms of knowledge.' In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school of Carnatic music, it is called Stavarājam. It is the 4th ragam in the 8th chakra, Vasu, with the mnemonic name Vasu-Bhu and mnemonic phrase 'sa ra gi mi pa dhi ni.' As a melakarta ragam, it is by definition a sampurna ragam, containing all seven notes in both ascent and descent in linear order. Its scale uses shuddha rishabham, sadharana gandharam, prati madhyamam, chathusruthi dhaivatham, and kaisiki nishadham. Shadvidamargini is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Natakapriya, the 10th melakarta. It has a few minor janya ragams associated with it, and through graha bhedam its notes can yield Nasikabhooshani.
gamaka_usage: Though Shadvidamargini is a straight sampurna melakarta, it is not usually effective as a merely dry scalar pattern; its identity emerges through restrained, weighty gamaka treatment. R1 and G2 are important for establishing the raga’s grave color and are typically rendered with measured kampita rather than flat intonation, especially in phrases around S-R1-G2 and G2-R1-S. M2 is the key color-bearing swara of the raga and should be handled carefully, often with a gentle sustained emphasis or subtle oscillatory touch, avoiding overly bright or aggressive treatment. N2 in descent, particularly in phrases like >S-N2-D2 and N2-D2-P, benefits from soft kampita or a delicate slide toward D2, reinforcing the raga’s introspective quality. D2 is generally smoother in approach and is better linked to P or N2 through jaru-like movement than struck harshly. P functions as a stable nyasa point, while S is a natural point of repose. Because the raga is non-vakra and non-bhashanga, gamakas should support the linear flow of the scale and avoid phraseology suggestive of other prati-madhyama ragas. Excessive brigha-heavy handling can blur its identity; a measured, contemplative approach with clear intonation of R1-G2-M2 and the gentle use of N2 is more characteristic.
notable_features: 46th melakarta; 4th ragam in the 8th chakra Vasu; mnemonic name Vasu-Bhu; mnemonic phrase 'sa ra gi mi pa dhi ni'; called Stavarājam in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school; prati madhyamam counterpart of Natakapriya (10th melakarta); graha bhedam on its notes yields Nasikabhooshani; has a few minor janya ragams.

mood: Suvarnangi has a bright yet serious melodic personality, combining the austerity of shuddha rishabham and sadharana gandharam with the luminous color of prati madhyamam and kakali nishadham. The result is a raga that can feel devotional, inward, and quietly majestic rather than overtly romantic or playful. It evokes a sense of golden radiance suggested by its name, with a contemplative stillness and a subtle wonder that suits meditative, reverential, and dignified musical settings.
description: Suvarnangi is a Carnatic raga and the 47th melakarta in the 72 melakarta system. The name means 'the golden bodied one.' In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Souveeram or Sauviram, and the variant spelling Sowveeram is also attested. It is the 5th raga in the 8th chakra, Vasu, with mnemonic name Vasu-Ma and mnemonic phrase 'sa ra gi mi pa dhi nu.' Its scale is S R1 G2 M2 P D2 N3 S / S N3 D2 P M2 G2 R1 S, employing shuddha rishabham, sadharana gandharam, prati madhyamam, chathusruthi dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham. As a melakarta, it is by definition a sampoorna raga, containing all seven notes in both ascent and descent. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Kokilapriya, the 11th melakarta. Though not among the most frequently heard melakartas in mainstream concert practice, it is recognized for its distinctive scalar color and has a small set of janya ragas associated with it.
gamaka_usage: As a sampurna melakarta with a straight scalar structure, Suvarnangi can accommodate both scale-oriented and phrase-oriented treatment, but its identity is best preserved through careful gamaka on the softer swaras rather than excessive plain-note rendering. R1 and G2 are important for establishing the raga’s grave color and are typically not left completely flat; they respond well to gentle kampita and nuanced intonation, especially in phrases moving through S–R1–G2 and G2–R1–S. M2 is a key color-bearing swara and is often given a clear, slightly sustained emphasis rather than a heavy shake, helping distinguish the raga from nearby melakarta colors. P functions as a stable nyasa point. D2 is usually handled with measured emphasis and smooth linkage to N3 or P rather than aggressive oscillation. N3 may be touched with mild kampita or a delicate upper inflection, especially in descent, so that its brightness is retained without sounding stark. Smooth jaru connections across M2–P–D2–N3 and in descending contours such as >S–N3–D2–P and P–M2–G2–R1 are effective. Because the raga combines the tense lower tetrachord R1–G2 with the sharper prati madhyamam and kakali nishadham, precise intonation is crucial; over-plain scalar rendering can make it sound skeletal, while over-ornamentation can blur its clean melakarta outline. A dignified, balanced gamaka approach with controlled kampita on R1/G2/N3, steadier treatment of P, and clear emphasis on M2 best preserves Suvarnangi’s identity.
notable_features: 5th raga in the 8th chakra Vasu; mnemonic name Vasu-Ma; mnemonic phrase 'sa ra gi mi pa dhi nu.' Known as Souveeram or Sauviram in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school; the spelling Sowveeram is also seen. It is the prati madhyamam counterpart of Kokilapriya (melakarta 11). Graha bhedam on Suvarnangi does not produce any other melakarta raga. Janya ragas listed in available sources include Sowveeram, Abhiru, Rathikā, and Vijayashree.

mood: Divyamani has a bright yet serious melodic personality, combining the austerity of shuddha rishabham with the brilliance of prati madhyamam, shatsruthi dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham. The result is a raga that can feel devotional and elevated, with a sense of radiance and inward wonder. Its very name, meaning 'divine gem,' suggests a luminous, refined emotional color. It suits contemplative devotional expression as well as passages that evoke majesty, spiritual clarity, and a quietly wondrous atmosphere.
description: Divyamani is the 48th melakarta raga in the 72 melakarta system of Carnatic music. Its name means 'the divine gem.' In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, it is known as Jeevantika or Jeevantini. It is the 6th raga in the 8th chakra, Vasu, and its mnemonic name is Vasu-Sha, with mnemonic phrase 'sa ra gi mi pa dhu nu.' As a melakarta, it is a sampurna raga, containing all seven swaras in both ascent and descent with a symmetrical scale. Its swara set is S R1 G2 M2 P D3 N3 S / S N3 D3 P M2 G2 R1 S, employing shuddha rishabham, sadharana gandharam, prati madhyamam, shatsruthi dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Roopavati, the 12th melakarta. Though not a commonly heard major concert raga, it is recognized in theory and has a few janya ragas and a small but notable compositional presence. Available comparative references note that there is no direct Hindustani raga called Divyamani. Theoretically, graha bhedam on this scale does not yield another melakarta raga.
gamaka_usage: Available references do not give a full codified gamaka grammar for Divyamani, but the snippets do offer one important clue: the D3 is heard with a gamaka touch suggestive of N2 on it, and the raga can at first impression resemble Varali with a D3, while retaining G2 as its gandharam. In practice, this implies that Divyamani should not be rendered as a plain scalar scale alone. R1 and G2 need careful handling, with G2 carrying expressive oscillation and the R1-G2 region shaped delicately so the raga's identity is not flattened. M2 may be approached with measured emphasis to bring out the prati-madhyama color, while P remains a comparatively steady anchoring swara. D3 is a sensitive note in the raga and can take a nuanced kampita/ornamented treatment rather than a dry intonation; the reported shading toward N2 in its gamaka should be understood as an ornamental inflection, not as an actual anya swara. N3 can be used with brightness in the upper tetrachord, especially in D3-N3-S phrases. Since Divyamani is a sampurna melakarta with no vakra movement, straight phrases help establish the scale, but the raga gains character when the poorvanga is handled with gravity and the uttaranga with controlled brilliance. Excessively plain rendering can make it sound skeletal, while over-ornamentation may blur it toward neighboring prati-madhyama ragas; therefore a balanced use of kampita and subtle slides is important.
notable_features: 48th melakarta; 6th raga in the 8th chakra Vasu; mnemonic name Vasu-Sha; mnemonic phrase 'sa ra gi mi pa dhu nu'; known as Jeevantika or Jeevantini in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school; prati madhyamam equivalent of Roopavati (12th melakarta); graha bhedam on this scale does not yield any other melakarta raga; a comparative source notes that there is no Hindustani raga called Divyamani; some references list Deshamookhari, Dundubi, and Shuddha Gandhari as janya ragas derived from Divyamani; the D3 is noted in discussion as taking a gamaka shading suggestive of N2, giving the raga a fleeting resemblance to Varali with D3 while preserving its own identity through G2.

mood: Dhavalambari has a grave, inward, and somewhat austere melodic personality. The presence of shuddha rishabham, shuddha dhaivatham, and the vivadi shuddha nishadham against prati madhyamam gives it a restrained, meditative color rather than a bright or extroverted one. It can suggest devotional introspection, quiet solemnity, and a touch of yearning or pathos. Listeners often experience it as serious and contemplative, with a rarefied stillness; it suits prayerful, reflective settings more than festive or exuberant expression.
description: Dhavalambari is a melakarta rāgam in Carnatic music and is the 49th melakarta in the 72 melakarta system. It is the 1st rāgam in the 9th chakra, Brahma, with the mnemonic name Brahma-Pa and mnemonic phrase 'sa ra gu mi pa dha na'. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, it is called Dhavaḻāngam or Dhavaḻāngi, also seen in transliteration as Dhavaangam or Dhavaangi. Its scale is sampurna in both ascent and descent: S R1 G3 M2 P D1 N1 S / S N1 D1 P M2 G3 R1 S. The swaras used are shuddha rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and shuddha nishadham, along with shadjam and panchamam. As a melakarta, it contains all seven notes in both directions and has the same notes in ascent and descent. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Gayakapriya, the 13th melakarta. Dhavalambari is a comparatively rare melakarta in concert practice, but it has attracted compositions from Muthuswami Dikshitar, Koteeswara Iyer, and Dr. M. Balamuralikrishna. Theoretical analysis noted in the sources states that shifting its notes through graha bhedam does not yield any other melakarta rāgam.
gamaka_usage: Dhavalambari should not be rendered as a bare scalar prati-madhyama melakarta; its identity depends on careful handling of the vivadi material, especially N1. The distinguishing feature repeatedly noted in source material is the vivadi N1, which sets it apart from Pantuvarali-like scalar impressions. N1 therefore needs delicate, well-centered intonation and is best treated with restrained kampita or a soft approach from D1 rather than a harsh, exposed plain note. R1 and D1 also benefit from measured oscillation, giving the raga gravity and preventing a dry linear effect. G3 can be given a controlled emphasis, while M2 functions as a bright but steady pivot and should generally be handled with moderation rather than excessive shaking. P and S serve as stable nyasa points. Descending contours such as N1-D1-P and P-M2-G3-R1-S are important, often linked with gentle jaru; similarly, M2-G3-R1 and D1-N1->S need precise spacing so the vivadi color remains clear without sounding abrasive. Since the scale is straight and non-vakra, raga bhava comes less from unusual phrase architecture and more from intonation, weight distribution, and nuanced kampita on the sensitive swaras.
notable_features: 49th melakarta; first rāgam of the 9th chakra Brahma; mnemonic name Brahma-Pa; mnemonic phrase 'sa ra gu mi pa dha na'; called Dhavaḻāngam or Dhavaḻāngi in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, also transliterated as Dhavaangam or Dhavaangi; prati madhyamam equivalent of Gayakapriya (13th melakarta); a comparatively rare melakarta in performance; the vivadi N1 is a key distinguishing feature and helps separate its melodic color from Pantuvarali-like impressions; has a few minor janya ragams; graha bhedam on this scale yields no other melakarta rāgam.

mood: Namanarayani has a poised, contemplative, and devotional melodic personality. Its scale suggests a serious but not harsh emotional color, giving the listener a feeling of inward prayer, quiet dignity, and reflective calm. The presence of shuddha rishabham and shuddha dhaivatham lends gravity, while the prati madhyamam adds a subtle brightness and wonder. The raga can evoke a serene temple atmosphere, meditative devotion, and a sense of refined spiritual introspection rather than overt drama.
description: Namanarayani is a Carnatic melakarta raga and the 50th melakarta in the 72 melakarta system. It is known as Namadeshi in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school of Carnatic music. It is the 2nd raga in the 9th chakra, Brahma, with the mnemonic name Brahma-Sri and mnemonic phrase 'sa ra gu mi pa dha ni'. Its scale is sampurna in both ascent and descent: S R1 G3 M2 P D1 N2 S / S N2 D1 P M2 G3 R1 S. The swaras used are shuddha rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and kaisiki nishadham, along with shadjam and panchamam. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Vakulabharanam, the 14th melakarta. As a melakarta, it is by definition a parent scale and has a few minor janya ragas associated with it. Graha bhedam on this raga does not yield another melakarta raga. Though theoretically complete and symmetrical, it is a relatively rare melakarta in concert practice and is more often encountered in pedagogic, classificatory, or specialist compositional contexts than as a mainstream elaboration raga.
gamaka_usage: As a melakarta with a straight sampurna scale, Namanarayani permits direct scalar movement, but a merely plain-note rendering can make it sound skeletal or drift toward nearby prati-madhyama melas. Its identity is better brought out through measured classical gamaka. R1 and D1, being the more delicate swaras in the scale, are handled with care and usually with restrained kampita or a slightly weighted intonation rather than aggressive shaking. G3 and N2 can take tasteful kampita to supply melodic color and emotional depth. M2 is an important color-bearing swara and is often sustained with controlled emphasis, giving the raga its distinct prati-madhyama sheen; it should not be over-slid in a way that blurs its pitch. P and S function as stable nyasa points. In descent, smooth jaru-like connections such as N2-D1-P and M2-G3-R1 are natural and help preserve continuity, while ascent is generally clearer and more linear: S R1 G3 M2 P D1 N2 >S. Because the scale is symmetrical and uncommon, gamakas should clarify rather than obscure the swara-sthana-s: moderate kampita on R1, G3, N2, and occasionally D1; steady treatment of S and P; and controlled sustain on M2 are especially effective. No special bhashanga handling is involved, and over-ornamentation is best avoided.
notable_features: Called Namadeshi in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. It is the 2nd raga in the 9th chakra Brahma. Mnemonic name: Brahma-Sri. Mnemonic phrase: sa ra gu mi pa dha ni. It has a few minor janya ragas. Graha bhedam on this raga does not yield any other melakarta raga. It is the prati madhyamam counterpart of Vakulabharanam (melakarta 14). It is a relatively rare melakarta in practical concert usage despite its complete sampurna structure.

mood: Kamavardhani carries an intense, serious, and contemplative emotional color. It is deeply devotional, often evoking spiritual yearning, surrender, austerity, and inward prayer. The prati madhyamam lends the raga a luminous tension and mystical flavor, so the experience can feel at once pleading, majestic, and transcendent. Listeners often perceive it as meditative yet emotionally charged, suitable for profound introspection, solemn worship, and expansive alapana that suggests dawn-like stillness moving toward revelation.
description: Kamavardhani, also spelled Kamavardhini, is a Carnatic raga and the 51st melakarta in the 72 melakarta system. It is also referred to as Pantuvarali, and in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Kashiramakriya. It is a very popular raga with musicians and is often sung at the beginning of a concert. It is the 3rd raga in the 9th chakra, Brahma, with mnemonic name Brahma-Go and mnemonic phrase 'sa ra gu mi pa dha nu'. Its scale is sampurna, containing all seven swaras in both ascent and descent: shuddha rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham along with shadjam and panchamam. Kamavardhani differs from the 15th melakarta Mayamalavagowla only in the madhyamam, and is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Mayamalavagowla. It has a few minor janya ragas. In Hindustani music, its equivalent is given as the Poorvi thaat/Puriya Dhanashree. The raga's notes, when shifted using graha bhedam, yield Kanakangi, the 1st melakarta. It tends to sound similar to, and is often mistaken for, Purvikalyani. Additional sources describe it as a serious, contemplative raga with a distinctly mystical flavor and considerable scope for elaborate alapana.
gamaka_usage: Kamavardhani is not a plain scalar raga; its identity emerges through weighty, sustained, and highly expressive gamaka treatment. R1 and D1 are typically rendered with strong kampita/oscillatory handling and are rarely left dry in important phrases. G3 is a key nyasa and identity-bearing swara, often approached with curved movement from R1 and linked sensitively to M2. M2 is the defining pivot that separates the raga from Mayamalavagowla; it must be given clear prominence and intonational precision, often with a lingering emphasis or a smooth jaru into or out of adjacent swaras. N3 is also an important color note, usually handled with intensity rather than as a flat scalar step, especially in upper-register phrases leading to >S. P serves as a comparatively steady anchoring swara, offering repose amid the raga's tension-filled contours. Jaru phrases between adjacent notes and elongated, curved prayogas are central to its rakti; the raga responds especially well to expansive alapana because the swaras admit deep oscillation and sustained emotional shading. Care is needed to preserve the distinct Kamavardhani color and not let phrases drift toward Purvikalyani; the handling of M2 and the overall grave, austere gamaka profile are crucial in maintaining that distinction.
notable_features: Pronunciation and script variants are given in Telugu, Kannada, Tamil, and Devanagari forms. It is especially popular in concerts and is often presented early in a performance. Mnemonic name: Brahma-Go; mnemonic phrase: sa ra gu mi pa dha nu. The swaras used are shuddha rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham. It is the prati madhyamam counterpart of Mayamalavagowla. Through graha bhedam, its notes yield Kanakangi, the 1st melakarta. It is often mistaken for Purvikalyani. The text mentions that it has a few minor janya ragas, but does not list them. Additional sources mention Deepakam, Bhogavasanta, Gamakapriya, and Mandari as janya ragas. It is noted for its serious, contemplative, mystical character and for offering substantial scope for alapana.

mood: Ramapriya has a serious, inward, and devotional emotional color. Its combination of shuddha rishabham with prati madhyamam gives it a restrained intensity rather than overt brightness, creating a contemplative atmosphere suited to prayer, reflection, and dignified expression. The raga can suggest quiet devotion, spiritual yearning, and a gentle pathos, making it effective for meditative or reverential settings rather than exuberant celebration.
description: Ramapriya is a Carnatic melakarta raga, the 52nd in the 72 melakarta system. It is the 4th raga in the 9th chakra, Brahma, with mnemonic name Brahma-Bhu and mnemonic phrase 'sa ra gu mi pa dhi ni'. Its scale is sampurna in both ascent and descent: S R1 G3 M2 P D2 N2 S / S N2 D2 P M2 G3 R1 S. The swaras used are shuddha rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, chathusruthi dhaivatham, and kaisiki nishadham, along with shadjam and panchamam. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Ramamanohari. Ramapriya is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Chakravakam, the 16th melakarta. It has a few minor janya ragas associated with it. Theoretical analysis noted in the source states that graha bhedam on any of its six non-shadja swaras does not yield another melakarta raga, because the resulting scales do not conform to melakarta rules.
gamaka_usage: As a melakarta with a straight sampurna scale, Ramapriya permits clear scalar movement, but its identity is best preserved through measured gamaka rather than plain-note rendering throughout. R1 and N2 typically benefit from gentle kampita or nuanced intonation to avoid a dry scalar feel, while G3 and M2 can be given controlled oscillation or slight glide treatment to bring out the raga’s weight and prati-madhyama color. P is generally a stable resting tone, and D2 may be approached with mild slide from P or resolved carefully toward N2. Because the raga is not vakra and not bhashanga, excessive ornamental distortion or foreign-note shading should be avoided. The overall gamaka approach is dignified, moderate, and bhava-oriented, emphasizing clarity of the melakarta structure while preserving a contemplative, devotional tone.

notable_compositions.name: Sri Rajarajeshwari
notable_compositions.composer: Ponniah Pillai of the Tanjore Quartet

notable_features: In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, this raga is known as Ramamanohari. It belongs to the 9th chakra Brahma and is the 4th raga in that chakra. Mnemonic name: Brahma-Bhu; mnemonic phrase: sa ra gu mi pa dhi ni. It is the prati madhyamam counterpart of Chakravakam (melakarta 16). According to the source, graha bhedam performed on any of the six swaras from Ri to Ni does not produce another valid melakarta raga. The source also notes that Ramapriya has only a few minor janya ragas.

mood: Gamanashrama has a bright yet serious melodic personality, combining the austerity of shuddha rishabham with the brilliance of antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, and kakali nishadham. The result is a contemplative and somewhat majestic mood that can feel inward, devotional, and subtly tense in a refined way. It suits meditative, prayerful, and intellectually engaging musical treatment, while also carrying a sense of wonder and elevated emotional color. The raga can evoke the stillness of twilight reflection, the dignity of temple worship, and the quiet intensity of spiritual yearning.
description: Gamanashrama is a Carnatic raga and the 53rd melakarta in the 72 melakarta system. It is a sampurna melakarta, containing all seven swaras in both ascent and descent: S R1 G3 M2 P D2 N3 S / S N3 D2 P M2 G3 R1 S. It is the 5th raga in the 9th chakra, Brahma, with the mnemonic name Brahma-Ma and mnemonic phrase 'sa ra gu mi pa dhi nu'. The swaras used are shadjam, shuddha rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, panchamam, chathusruthi dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of the 17th melakarta Suryakantam. In Hindustani music, it is stated to be equivalent to Marva. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, this raga is called Gamakakriya. Gamanashrama has associated janya ragas, of which Hamsanandi and Poorvikalyani are noted as popular. Through graha bhedam, its notes yield two other minor melakarta ragas: Jhankaradhwani and Ratnangi.
gamaka_usage: Gamanashrama admits a full sampurna scalar outline, but its identity depends on careful handling of the tense R1-G3 relationship and the luminous color of M2 and N3. R1 is not a casual plain note; it is usually rendered with measured kampita or as a delicately touched note approached from S or resolved to G3, so that its shuddha-rishabha character is clearly heard without becoming harsh. G3 can take a firm kampita and often acts as an important color-bearing swara in conjunction with R1 and M2. M2 is a major point of raga identity and is often sustained or gently oscillated; phrases dwelling on G3-M2-P or descending through P-M2-G3-R1 help establish the melakarta clearly. P is generally steady and acts as a nyasa/supporting note. D2 is usually handled in smooth connection with N3 in ascent and with P in descent, often through jaru-like linkage in phrases such as P D2 N3 >S and >S N3 D2 P. N3 may be given a bright kampita or a poised upper approach into shadja. Descending slides such as N3-D2-P and M2-G3-R1 are especially useful. Because the raga can, if rendered too plainly, resemble a scalar or slightly altered Poorvikalyani/Marva-like contour, musicians rely on controlled kampita, subtle jaru, and clear emphasis on the jeeva-colored swaras R1, G3, M2, and N3 to bring out its distinct weight and seriousness. Excessively flat, unornamented treatment tends to underplay the raga’s character.
notable_features: Pronounced gamanāshrama. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar tradition it is called Gamakakriya. It is the prati madhyamam counterpart of Suryakantam (melakarta 17). Its graha bhedam yields Jhankaradhwani and Ratnangi. The source notes that some Dikshitar compositions listed here are also sung in Poorvikalyani. The Wikipedia page also mentions an Abhang, 'Gopi Gopala Lal', by Sant Surdas. A Western scalar comparison given in one source is 1-b2-3-#4-5-6-7, i.e. a Lydian flat-2 type scale.

mood: Vishwambari has a bright, striking and somewhat majestic emotional color. The combination of shuddha rishabham with antara gandharam and the sharp prati madhyamam gives it an intense, uncommon melodic personality that can feel awe-inspiring and expansive. It can suggest grandeur, spiritual power, and a sense of wonder, making it suitable for devotional or dignified presentation rather than soft intimacy. To a listener, it may evoke the feeling of standing before something vast, radiant, and slightly austere.
description: Vishwambari is the 54th melakarta raga in Carnatic music and therefore a janaka (parent) raga. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Vamshavati. It is the 6th raga in the 9th chakra, Brahma, with the mnemonic name Brahma-Sha and mnemonic phrase 'sa ra gu mi pa dhu nu'. As a melakarta, it is sampurna in both ascent and descent. The swaras employed are shuddha rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, shatsruthi dhaivatham and kakali nishadham, besides shadjam and panchamam. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Hatakambari, the 18th melakarta. Vishwambari has a few minor janya ragas associated with it. Through graha bhedam, its notes yield two other minor melakarta ragas: Shamalangi and Ganamoorti.
gamaka_usage: As a melakarta with a direct sampurna scale and vivadi content, Vishwambari is best handled with clear intonation and careful gamaka control. R1 should be rendered with precision and usually with a measured kampita rather than excessive oscillation, since its proximity relationships in the scale can easily blur the raga identity if handled loosely. G3 and N3 may take gentle oscillation or emphasis, while M2 often acts as a bright pivot note and can be sustained with moderate ornamentation. D3, being a vivadi-associated sharp dhaivata in this context, should be approached carefully and cleanly, often benefiting from controlled kampita or subtle glide from P rather than heavy shaking. P and S serve as stable resting points. Jaru phrases between P-D3-N3 and M2-G3-R1 can help outline the raga, but overuse of curved phrases may obscure its scalar clarity. Overall, the raga favors a disciplined, luminous treatment with restrained but deliberate gamakas.
notable_features: 54th melakarta; 6th raga in the 9th chakra Brahma; mnemonic name Brahma-Sha; mnemonic phrase 'sa ra gu mi pa dhu nu'; called Vamshavati in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school; also transliterated as Visvambari; prati madhyamam counterpart of Hatakambari (18th melakarta); graha bhedam yields Shamalangi and Ganamoorti; has a few minor janya ragas associated with it.

mood: Shamalangi has a serious, inward, and slightly austere melodic personality. The combination of prati madhyamam with shuddha dhaivatam and shuddha nishadam gives it a restrained, meditative color that can suggest devotion, quiet yearning, and gentle pathos rather than overt brightness. It can feel contemplative and introspective, suited to reflective moments, prayerful settings, or music that seeks depth over exuberance. The raga may also evoke a calm stillness with an undercurrent of emotional tension, making it effective for devotional and weighty musical expression.
description: Shamalangi or Shyamalangi (pronounced shāmaḻāngi) is a Carnatic raga and the 55th melakarta in the 72 melakarta system. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Shāmaḻam. It is the 1st raga in the 10th chakra, Disi, with mnemonic name Disi-Pa and mnemonic phrase 'sa ri gi mi pa dha na'. Its scale is S R2 G2 M2 P D1 N1 S / S N1 D1 P M2 G2 R2 S. The swaras used are chathusruthi rishabham, sadharana gandharam, prati madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham and shuddha nishadham, along with shadjam and panchamam. As a melakarta, it is by definition a sampoorna raga, containing all seven notes in both ascent and descent. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Jhankaradhwani, the 19th melakarta. A couple of minor janya ragas are associated with it. Through graha bhedam, its notes yield two minor melakarta ragas: Ganamoorti and Vishwambari.
time_of_day: No fixed time; in Carnatic practice ragas are generally not bound to a strict time-of-day system, and Shamalangi is chosen more for bhava than for hour.
