gamaka_usage: The source material does not give a codified lakshana of gamakas for Shamalangi, but its handling can be inferred from its melakarta identity and vivadi content. The raga should not be rendered as a bare scalar pattern. R2 and especially G2 are best given measured kampita or gentle oscillation so the poorvanga does not sound flat. M2 is a key color-bearing swara and must be intoned clearly and often approached with a smooth slide from G2 or resolved carefully to P. In the uttaranga, D1 and N1 require particularly careful treatment because of the vivadi flavor of the scale; they are usually more effective with restrained kampita, nokku, or short jaru connections rather than hard, plain attacks. P and S function well as comparatively steady nyasa points, helping stabilize the raga. Characteristic continuity is enhanced by linking phrases such as M2-P-D1-N1, N1-D1-P, and P-M2-G2-R2 with subtle jarus. Excessive briga-heavy or overly plain scalar singing can weaken the raga’s identity; the raga responds better to weight, precision of sruti, and controlled oscillation, especially around G2, D1, and N1. Compared with brighter prati-madhyama melas, Shamalangi benefits from a more restrained and introspective gamaka approach.
notable_features: 55th melakarta; first raga in the 10th chakra Disi; mnemonic name Disi-Pa; mnemonic phrase 'sa ri gi mi pa dha na'. Known as Shāmaḻam in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. It is the prati madhyamam counterpart of Jhankaradhwani (19th melakarta). Graha bhedam on this scale yields Ganamoorti and Vishwambari. It has a couple of minor janya ragas associated with it.

mood: Shanmukhapriya has a deeply devotional and emotionally charged character, often evoking reverence, yearning, and spiritual intensity. It is especially associated with bhakti, and listeners may experience it as prayerful, inward, and moving, sometimes rising into a sense of wonder or heroic uplift. The raga can suggest the image of ardent supplication before a deity, particularly Murugan or Shiva, while also carrying a radiant, majestic force in expansive treatment. Its prati madhyama color combined with the softer D1 and N2 gives it a searching, poignant hue, so the emotional experience can move between tender pleading, meditative absorption, and dramatic spiritual exaltation.
description: Shanmukhapriya is a melakarta ragam in Carnatic music and the 56th melakarta in the 72 melakarta system. It is the 2nd ragam in the 10th chakra, Disi, with mnemonic name Disi-Sri and mnemonic phrase 'sa ri gi mi pa dha ni'. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Chāmaram. Its scale is sampurna, containing all seven notes in both ascent and descent: S R2 G2 M2 P D1 N2 S / S N2 D1 P M2 G2 R2 S. The swaras used are chatushruti rishabham, sadharana gandharam, prati madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatam and kaisiki nishadam. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Natabhairavi, the 20th melakarta. It is said to have been borrowed into Hindustani music from Carnatic music. Many compositions on Lord Murugan and Lord Shiva are based on this raga. It has a few minor janya ragams associated with it. The raga is widely valued for its splendorous melodic appeal and for its strong capacity to evoke bhakti rasa in both composed and improvisational music.
gamaka_usage: As a melakarta with a symmetrical sampurna scale, Shanmukhapriya permits broad, elaborate treatment, but its identity depends strongly on gamaka-rich handling rather than plain scalar movement. G2 is a major jeeva swara and is rarely left bare; it is typically rendered with kampita and curved intonation in phrases such as R2-G2-M2 and M2-G2-R2-S. N2 also carries strong emotive weight and is often oscillated or approached with a glide from D1, especially in upper-register phrases like P-D1-N2-S and S-N2-D1-P. D1, because of its delicate position against M2 and N2, is usually treated with care—more shaded and caressed than struck harshly—and often appears in jaru-linked movement with P and N2. M2 is an important color-bearing note: it may be sustained, gently oscillated, or used as a pivot in phrases connecting G2 and P, helping bring out the raga’s prati-madhyama brightness. R2 is generally lighter than G2 in weight but participates in characteristic kampita-laden phrases leading into G2. P and S function as stable nyasa points and provide repose amid the more oscillated treatment of the softer swaras. Jaru phrases between adjacent notes, especially around G2-M2, P-D1-N2, and the descent N2-D1-P, are effective in bringing out the raga’s devotional and dramatic personality. Although straight scalar passages are possible because the raga is sampurna and non-vakra, a flat rendering can make it sound skeletal; the raga comes alive when G2, N2, and D1 are given nuanced oscillation and when M2 is used as a luminous anchor. Careful gamaka treatment also helps distinguish it from other prati-madhyama ragas with scalar similarity in isolated phrases.
notable_features: It is the 56th melakarta and the prati madhyamam counterpart of Natabhairavi (20). In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, the raga is known as Chāmaram, and the compositions Siddhi Vinayakam, Mahasuram Ketumaham, Sadashraye and Ekamreshanayakim contain that school-specific raga mudra. Mahasuram Ketumaham is disputed by some as being a disciple’s composition rather than Muthuswami Dikshitar’s. Through graha bhedam, Shanmukhapriya yields three other major melakarta ragams: Shoolini, Dhenuka and Chitrambari. It is said to have been borrowed into Hindustani music from Carnatic music. A janya scale mentioned in the source is Sumanesharanjani / Samudrapriya / Madhukauns with ascent S G2 M2 P N2 S and descent S N2 P M2 G2 S. The raga is especially associated with compositions on Lord Murugan and Lord Shiva and is often noted for its strong bhakti appeal.

mood: Simhendramadhyamam has a grand, regal, meditative, bold, and striking personality. It creates an atmosphere of deep inward reflection charged with emotional intensity: the listener may feel yearning, spiritual fervor, awe, and a darkly luminous majesty. Its contour can suggest pathos and devotion, yet it is not merely plaintive; there is also a powerful, kingly firmness and contemplative stillness in it. In expansive treatment it evokes the image of something majestic and mysterious unfolding slowly, with flashes of brilliance in the upper register and a grave, searching depth in the lower and middle octaves.
description: Simhendramadhyamam is a Carnatic melakarta raga and the 57th melakarta in the 72-melakarta system. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Sumadyuti. It is the 3rd raga in the 10th chakra, Disi, with mnemonic name Disi-Go and mnemonic phrase 'sa ri gi mi pa dha nu'. Its scale is S R2 G2 M2 P D1 N3 S / S N3 D1 P M2 G2 R2 S. The swaras used are chatushruti rishabham, sadharana gandharam, prati madhyamam, shuddha daivatam, and kakali nishadham, along with shadjam and panchamam. As a melakarta, it is by definition a sampurna raga with all seven notes in both ascent and descent. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Kiranavali (also known as Keeravani), the 21st melakarta. It is also equivalent to the Hungarian Minor or Gypsy Minor scale in Western music. The raga is said to have been borrowed into Hindustani music from Carnatic music. Simhendramadhyamam has a few minor janya ragas, including Vijaya Saraswati, a scale first used by Muthiah Bhagavatar, and Urmika. Through graha bhedam, its notes yield two other melakarta ragas: Mayamalavagowla and Rasikapriya. It is widely regarded as a weighty and rakti-bearing raga capable of elaborate alapana, tanam, and pallavi treatment, and is admired for its passionate appeal and strongly etched melodic identity.
gamaka_usage: Simhendramadhyamam is not handled as a plain scalar melakarta; its identity emerges through weighty, rakti-laden gamaka treatment. G2 and D1 are especially important for establishing the raga’s dark, intense color and are typically rendered with pronounced kampita/oscillation rather than as flat notes. R2 is usually not left bare for long; it is often approached in relation to G2 and can carry a measured oscillation, especially in phrases that dwell on the poorvanga. M2 is a major color-bearing swara and is often sustained with weight, sometimes reached through a glide from G2 or moving onward with force toward P. N3 is bright and tense, frequently emphasized in ascent toward the upper shadja and in descending phrases such as N3-D1-P; it should sound luminous rather than casual. P and S function as comparatively stable nyasa points. Jaru movements and curved phraseology are important around M2-P-D1, D1-N3-S, and S-N3-D1-P, and the upper-octave region can be given striking emphasis, with the tara sthayi Rishabha receiving notable weight in elaboration. The raga should be unfolded with gravitas and continuity of gamaka so that it remains distinct from Keeravani: the prati madhyamam must be clearly colored, and the handling of G2, M2, D1, and N3 should project a regal, meditative, and emotionally charged bhava rather than a dry heptatonic outline.
notable_features: Called Sumadyuti in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, and Dikshitar compositions in this raga are noted as carrying the raga mudra Sumadyuti. It is the 3rd raga in the 10th chakra Disi. Mnemonic name: Disi-Go; mnemonic phrase: sa ri gi mi pa dha nu. It is said to have been borrowed into Hindustani music from Carnatic music. Graha bhedam on Simhendramadhyamam yields Mayamalavagowla and Rasikapriya. Vijaya Saraswati is specifically noted as a scale first used by Muthiah Bhagavatar. The raga is often described as regal, meditative, bold, and striking, and is considered suitable for expansive, weighty concert treatment including RTP.

mood: Hemavati has a dignified, expansive and slightly intense melodic personality. Its prati madhyama color combined with the soft sadharana gandharam and kaisiki nishadam gives it a serious yet appealing emotional depth. It can feel devotional and majestic in kriti rendering, while in instrumental and nadaswaram treatment it often acquires a bold, resonant, heroic sweep. The raga suggests inward reflection, grandeur, and a sense of wonder, making it suitable for both meditative and powerfully expressive musical settings.
description: Hemavati is a melakarta ragam in Carnatic music and is the 58th melakarta in the 72 melakarta system. It is the 4th ragam in the 10th chakra, Disi, with the mnemonic name Disi-Bhu and mnemonic phrase 'sa ri gi mi pa dhi ni'. Its scale is sampurna in both ascent and descent: S R2 G2 M2 P D2 N2 S / S N2 D2 P M2 G2 R2 S. As a melakarta ragam, it contains all seven notes in both directions. Hemavati is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Kharaharapriya, the 22nd melakarta, and conversely its shuddha madhyamam equivalent is Kharaharapriya. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Simharavam or Deshi Simharavam. The raga is especially beloved of nadaswaram vidwans. It is also said to have been borrowed into Hindustani music from Carnatic music, especially by instrumentalists, where a raga using the same swara-set is also known as Hemavati. Hemavati has a few minor janya ragams associated with it.
gamaka_usage: As a sampurna melakarta with a straight scalar structure, Hemavati accommodates both scale-oriented and phrase-oriented treatment. The raga is typically enriched through expressive kampita on G2, N2 and often R2, while M2 is an important color-giving note that should be handled with clarity and weight. D2 can be approached with gentle oscillation or glide in melodic movement toward N2 or P. Panchamam is generally a stable resting point, and shadjam is held firm. In elaboration, smooth jaru connections across M2-P-D2-N2 and N2-D2-P are effective, while the raga's identity depends on preserving the distinct prati madhyama flavor without flattening it into Kharaharapriya-like handling. G2 and N2 should not be rendered too plain for long, since their nuanced oscillation helps establish the raga’s serious and resonant character. M2, being the defining prati-madhyama color, is often given sustained emphasis and approached cleanly from G2 or P; excessive ambiguity around M2 can blur the raga’s identity. R2 may be used with measured kampita in descent-oriented phrases such as M2 G2 R2 S, while D2 often works well in curved movement between P and N2 rather than as a stark isolated note. Panchamam and shadjam serve as firm nyasa points, giving balance to the otherwise rich, shaded middle notes. Since Hemavati is favored by nadaswaram performers, expansive, resonant gamaka treatment, broad jarus, and strong phrase projection suit the raga especially well.
notable_features: Hemavati is the 58th melakarta and the 4th raga in the 10th chakra Disi. Its mnemonic name is Disi-Bhu. Graha bhedam on Hemavati yields three other major melakarta ragams: Keeravani, Vakulabharanam and Kosalam. It is known as Simharavam or Deshi Simharavam in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. The raga is especially associated with nadaswaram performance. It is said to have entered Hindustani music from Carnatic music, particularly through instrumentalists; the Hindustani raga using the same swara-set is also called Hemavati and is regarded as a relatively recent migrant.

mood: Dharmavati has a poised, luminous and emotionally rich character. Its prati madhyama color gives it a modern, slightly yearning brightness, while the presence of sadharana gandharam adds depth and tenderness. The raga can evoke devotion, introspection and gentle pathos, making it suitable for contemplative, prayerful and emotionally expressive music. It suggests a serene but not detached atmosphere—more like inward reflection touched by longing and dignity.
description: Dharmavati is a Carnatic raga and the 59th melakarta in the 72 melakarta system. It is the 5th raga in the 10th chakra, Disi. Its mnemonic name is Disi-Ma and its mnemonic phrase is sa ri gi mi pa dhi nu. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Dhaamavati. As a melakarta, it is by definition a sampurna raga, containing all seven notes in both ascent and descent. Its scale is S R2 G2 M2 P D2 N3 S / S N3 D2 P M2 G2 R2 S, using chathusruthi rishabham, sadharana gandharam, prati madhyamam, chathusruthi dhaivatham and kakali nishadham. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Gourimanohari, the 23rd melakarta. Madhuvanti is stated to be the nearest Hindustani music scale to Dharmavati. The raga was introduced in Hindustani music in vocal style for the first time by the late Pandit Samaresh Chawdhury, a disciple of Pandit Ravi Shankar. Dharmavati has several janya ragas, of which Madhuvanti, Ranjani, Sri Tyagaraja and Vijayanagari are noted as popular in concerts.
time_of_day: In modern Carnatic practice, Dharmavati is not bound to a strict time-of-day convention and may be performed at any time.
gamaka_usage: Dharmavati admits both scalar movement and gamaka-rich handling, but its identity depends on preserving the Carnatic treatment of its swaras rather than rendering it as a plain scale. G2 (sadharana gandharam) is one of the key expressive notes and is typically given a soft kampita or approached through a glide from R2 or M2; it should not be rendered too flat for long. M2 is the defining color note of the raga and often receives emphasis as a nyasa or pivot swara, usually handled with a measured, polished touch rather than heavy oscillation. N3 is another important melodic color and can take a gentle kampita, especially in descending or cadential phrases. R2 and D2 may be lightly oscillated or linked through jaru in flowing phrases, while P generally functions as a stable resting point. Characteristic movement is smooth and linear—phrases such as S R2 G2 M2, M2 P D2 N3 S and S N3 D2 P M2 G2 R2 S bring out the melakarta form clearly. Because the scale is close to Hindustani Madhuvanti territory, performers take care to use Carnatic gamaka grammar and phrase architecture so that Dharmavati retains its own bhava and does not collapse into a merely scalar or Hindustani-sounding rendition.
notable_features: Dharmavati is the 59th melakarta and the 5th raga in the 10th chakra Disi. Mnemonic name: Disi-Ma; mnemonic phrase: sa ri gi mi pa dhi nu. It is known as Dhaamavati in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Gourimanohari (23rd melakarta). Through graha bhedam, Dharmavati yields two other melakarta ragas: Chakravakam and Sarasangi. Notable film composers including T. G. Lingappa, M. S. Viswanathan, Ilaiyaraja and A. R. Rahman have used the scale of Dharmavati in film music. Songs mentioned include 'kanasalli bandavanyare' (Shruti seridaga), 'Meendum Meedum Vaa' (Vikram), 'Andhela Ravamidhi' (Swarna Kamalam), 'Vaanaville' (Ramana), 'Nataraaja paadaalu' (Aalaapana), and 'Ottagathai Kattiko' from the 1993 Tamil film Gentleman.

mood: Neetimati has a bright, incisive and elevated melodic personality. With prati madhyamam, shatsruti dhaivatam and kakali nishadam in a complete heptatonic scale, it suggests brilliance, alertness and a sense of upward aspiration rather than repose-heavy softness. The raga can evoke a dignified devotional feeling, a noble and energetic inner confidence, and a mildly wondrous color because of its luminous upper tetrachord. It suits moods of earnest prayer, intellectual clarity, resolve, and stately musical exploration.
description: Neetimati is the 60th melakarta raga in Carnatic music and therefore a parent (janaka) raga in the 72 melakarta system. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Nishadham. It is the 6th raga in the 10th chakra, Disi, with mnemonic name Disi-Sha and mnemonic phrase 'sa ri gi mi pa dhu nu'. Its scale is sampurna in both ascent and descent: S R2 G2 M2 P D3 N3 S / S N3 D3 P M2 G2 R2 S. The swaras employed are chathusruthi rishabham, sadharana gandharam, prati madhyamam, shatsruthi dhaivatham and kakali nishadham, along with shadjam and panchamam. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Varunapriya, the 24th melakarta raga. Neetimati has a few janya ragas, of which Hamsanadam is specifically noted as a popular derivative. Its notes, when shifted through graha bhedam, yield the minor melakarta Gangeyabhooshani.
gamaka_usage: As a melakarta with a straight sampurna scale and no vakra movement indicated in the source, Neetimati is handled through clear scalar movement, but its identity depends on careful intonation of G2, M2, D3 and N3. The prati madhyamam (M2) should be rendered with firmness and brightness, often as a point of color rather than with excessive blur. G2 generally benefits from mild kampita or a gentle approach from R2 or M2 so that its sadharana character is preserved. D3 and N3, the sharper upper swaras, need especially precise handling; they may take light oscillation in manodharma, but over-oscillation or flattening can weaken the raga’s contour. P and S act as stable nyasa points. Characteristic scalar stretches such as M2-P-D3-N3->S and the descent >S-N3-D3-P-M2 help establish its luminous, elevated profile. Since no special vakra prayogas or anya-swara usages are indicated, the gamaka approach is best understood as balanced and controlled: enough kampita and connecting slides to retain Carnatic identity, but with clarity of swara-sthana and direct melodic outline remaining paramount.
notable_features: 60th melakarta; 6th raga of the 10th chakra Disi; mnemonic name Disi-Sha; mnemonic phrase 'sa ri gi mi pa dhu nu'. Called Nishadham in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. Prati madhyamam counterpart of Varunapriya (24th melakarta). Through graha bhedam, Neetimati yields the minor melakarta Gangeyabhooshani. It is also traditionally mentioned as a midday raga and is said to appease the gods and nullify inconsistencies in singing ragas at the wrong time.

mood: Kantamani has a serious, introspective and somewhat rarefied melodic color. The presence of prati madhyamam together with shuddha dhaivatam and shuddha nishadam gives it a tense yet meditative quality, often suggesting devotion, inward reflection, and a touch of poignancy. It can evoke the feeling of solemn worship, quiet wonder, or a contemplative late-evening atmosphere where emotion is restrained but deep. Its character is not overtly bright; instead it leans toward a dignified, subtle emotional world with devotional and slightly plaintive shades.
description: Kantamani is a melakarta ragam in Carnatic music and is the 61st melakarta in the 72 melakarta system. It is known as Kuntalam in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school of Carnatic music. It is the 1st ragam in the 11th chakra, Rudra, and its mnemonic name is Rudra-Pa; the mnemonic phrase is 'sa ri gu mi pa dha na'. Its scale is sampurna in both ascent and descent, as required of a melakarta. The swaras used are chathusruthi rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and shuddha nishadham, along with shadjam and panchamam. Like Kalyani, it employs R2, G3, M2 and P, but in the upper tetrachord it uses D1 and N1; its nishadam lies at the same sthana as the dhaivata of Kalyani. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Mararanjani, the 25th melakarta. In theoretical treatment, Kantamani yields Manavati through graha bhedam. It is a rare melakarta with only a few minor janya ragams associated with it.
gamaka_usage: Kantamani is not a raga that thrives on plain scalar rendering alone; its identity emerges through careful handling of its vivadi-flavored upper tetrachord. R2 and G3 may be given measured kampita and weight, while M2 is an important color note that can be sustained with repose. P remains a firm nyasa and stabilizing pivot. The crucial area is the ascent and descent around P-D1-N1->S and >S-N1-D1-P: D1 and especially N1 should be treated with precision and restraint, often with subtle kampita or delicate oscillatory shading rather than hard, flat intonation. Gentle jaru connections such as N1~D1~P and M2~G3~R2 help soften the austerity of the scale and preserve raga bhava. Because Kantamani shares R2 G3 M2 P with Kalyani but differs sharply in using D1 and N1, performers must clearly articulate the lower dhaivata-nishada region so that it does not drift toward Kalyani-like phrases. Overall, the gamaka approach is controlled, weighty, and exact, with restrained ornamentation that highlights the raga's serious, meditative and uncommon personality.
notable_features: Called Kuntalam in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. It is the 1st ragam of the 11th chakra Rudra, with mnemonic name Rudra-Pa and mnemonic phrase 'sa ri gu mi pa dha na'. Like Kalyani, it shares R2, G3, M2 and P, but differs in the upper tetrachord by employing D1 and N1; its nishadam is at the same sthana as the dhaivata of Kalyani. It is the prati madhyamam counterpart of Mararanjani (25th melakarta). Through graha bhedam, its notes yield Manavati. It has a couple of minor janya ragams. Because it contains D1 and N1 in a prati madhyama melakarta framework, it includes vivadi swara relationships.

mood: Rishabhapriya has a serious yet luminous prati-madhyama color, combining brightness with a restrained, introspective depth. Its scale suggests a contemplative and slightly yearning emotional space, capable of evoking devotion, quiet wonder, and gentle pathos rather than overt exuberance. The presence of shuddha dhaivata and kaisiki nishada gives it a soft inward pull, while the antara gandhara and prati madhyama lend brilliance and tension. The overall effect is meditative, dignified, and subtly emotive, suitable for reflective devotional expression and thoughtful melodic exploration.
description: Rishabhapriya is the 62nd melakarta ragam in Carnatic music and therefore a parent scale in the 72 melakarta system. It is the 2nd ragam in the 11th chakra, Rudra, with the mnemonic name Rudra-Sri and mnemonic phrase 'sa ri gu mi pa dha ni'. Its scale is sampurna in both ascent and descent: S R2 G3 M2 P D1 N2 S / S N2 D1 P M2 G3 R2 S. The swaras used are chathusruthi rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and kaisiki nishadham, along with shadjam and panchamam. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Charukesi, the 26th melakarta. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, it is called Ratipriya. A few minor janya ragams are associated with it. The raga also has theoretical importance: by graha bhedam from panchamam, it yields the melakarta Kokilapriya. Rishabhapriya and Kokilapriya are noted as the only melakarta ragas to contain a whole-tone scale segment; omitting Pa in Rishabhapriya yields the whole-tone collection. A janya raga named Gopriya is formed by omitting panchamam in both ascent and descent, and this scale is special because graha bhedam on it reproduces the same scale repeatedly due to equal interval spacing; in Western terminology this corresponds to a whole-tone scale.
gamaka_usage: As a melakarta with a straight sampurna scale, Rishabhapriya permits clear scalar movement, but a merely plain rendering can sound skeletal; its identity emerges through measured gamaka on the key swaras. R2 and especially G3 are often given gentle kampita or a nuanced oscillatory touch rather than being struck too flatly, helping establish the raga’s melodic personality early. M2 is an important color note and is usually approached smoothly from G3 or led into from P with a mild jaru, highlighting the prati-madhyama brightness without making the raga sound harsh. P generally functions as a stable nyasa point and is comparatively plain. D1 and N2 are crucial for bhava: D1 is delicate and should be handled with care, often with a soft oscillation or curved approach from P, while N2 benefits from a tender kampita and a subtle pull toward the upper shadja in ascent or toward D1 in descent. Characteristic movement is therefore not just linear SRGMPDN-S, but phrases where G3-M2 and D1-N2 are linked with smooth slides and controlled inflection. The raga should avoid over-heavy gamakas that obscure its melakarta clarity, yet it also should not be rendered in a dry scale-wise manner; balanced kampita on G3, D1, and N2, with graceful jaru around M2 and the upper tetrachord, best preserves its contemplative, slightly yearning character.
notable_features: 2nd ragam in the 11th chakra Rudra; mnemonic name Rudra-Sri. Mnemonic phrase: sa ri gu mi pa dha ni. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Charukesi (melakarta 26). In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is known as Ratipriya. Graha bhedam from panchamam yields Kokilapriya. Rishabhapriya and Kokilapriya are stated to be the only melakarta ragas to contain a whole-tone scale; omitting Pa in Rishabhapriya and Sa in Kokilapriya gives that collection. Its janya Gopriya, formed by omitting panchamam, is notable because graha bhedam on it reproduces the same scale repeatedly due to equal frequency spacing, corresponding to the Western whole-tone scale.

mood: Latangi has a bright yet serious emotional color, combining the brilliance of prati madhyama with the slightly tense pull of shuddha dhaivata. It can feel devotional and majestic, while also carrying an arresting, searching quality that gives it depth and intensity. The raga suits moments of inward focus, dignified praise, and expansive melodic exploration, often evoking a sense of wonder, spiritual aspiration, and controlled emotional power.
description: Latangi is a Carnatic raga and the 63rd melakarta ragam in the 72 melakarta system. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, it is called Geetapriya or Gitapriya. It is the 3rd ragam in the 11th chakra, Rudra, with the mnemonic name Rudra-Go and mnemonic phrase 'sa ri gu mi pa dha nu'. Its scale is sampurna in both ascent and descent, as required of a melakarta. The swaras used are chathusruthi rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham, along with shadjam and panchamam. Latangi is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Sarasangi, the 27th melakarta. It has a few minor janya ragams associated with it. The raga is also theoretically notable because, under graha bhedam, its notes yield two other melakarta ragams: Sooryakantam and Senavati.
gamaka_usage: Latangi should not be rendered as a bare scalar prati-madhyama melakarta; its identity emerges through classical Carnatic gamaka on the key swaras. R2 and G3 are typically enlivened with kampita and close, connected treatment rather than being struck as isolated plain notes. M2 is an important color-bearing swara and is often given sustained emphasis or approached with a smooth slide from G3; phrases around G3–M2 and M2–P help establish the raga’s brightness. D1 requires especially careful handling: because the raga combines a sharp M2 and N3 with the softer shuddha dhaivata, D1 is usually treated with weight and controlled oscillation or as part of a curved approach from P and toward N3, rather than overexposed flat intonation. N3 can take a graceful kampita and often serves as a luminous upper leading tone to the tara shadja. Jaru-based connections such as N3>S, P D1 N3, and descending N3 D1 P M2 are effective when shaped with restraint. Panchama and shadja are comparatively steadier resting points. In alapana, neraval, and kalpana swara, both linear and elaborative phrases are possible, but the raga’s character depends on preserving the tension-and-release between M2, D1, and N3 through nuanced oscillation and slides, avoiding an overly plain or mechanical scale rendition.
notable_features: Pronounced latāngi. Mnemonic name: Rudra-Go. Mnemonic phrase: sa ri gu mi pa dha nu. It is the 3rd raga in the 11th chakra. In the Dikshitar tradition it is known as Geetapriya or Gitapriya. It is the prati madhyama equivalent of Sarasangi (27th melakarta). Through graha bhedam, Latangi yields the melakarta ragas Sooryakantam and Senavati. It has a few minor janya ragas.

mood: Vachaspati has a poised, bright, and dignified emotional color. Its prati madhyama gives it a luminous, expansive quality, while the kaisiki nishada softens the brilliance and lends a reflective, devotional warmth. The raga can feel contemplative yet uplifting, suitable for prayerful, meditative, and intellectually serene expression. It evokes the feeling of inward clarity, reverence, and a calm sense of wonder rather than overt drama. Descriptions of the raga also point to a gentle romantic-devotional blend and a soothing, stress-relieving quality, so it can suggest both inward meditation and tender emotional warmth.
description: Vachaspati is a Carnatic melakarta raga meaning 'Lord of speech'. It is the 64th melakarta in the 72 melakarta system and the 4th raga in the 11th chakra, Rudra. Its mnemonic name is Rudra-Bhu and its mnemonic phrase is 'sa ri gu mi pa dhi ni'. It is a sampurna raga containing all seven swaras in both ascent and descent: S R2 G3 M2 P D2 N2 S / S N2 D2 P M2 G3 R2 S. The scale uses chathusruthi rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, chathusruthi dhaivatham, and kaisiki nishadham. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Harikambhoji, the 28th melakarta. According to the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, it is known as Bhushāvati. The raga was also borrowed into Hindustani music, where it is described as a relatively recent import from Carnatic music and was adapted around the mid-20th century by musicians such as Ravi Shankar and Ali Akbar Khan. Vachaspati is said to be close to Kalyani, differing only in the nishadham. Though not said to have many compositions overall, many composers have contributed individual works in it. It has many janya ragas, of which Bhooshavali and Saraswathi are noted as popular. Through graha bhedam, Vachaspati yields three other major melakarta ragas: Charukesi, Gourimanohari, and Natakapriya. In Western music, it corresponds to the Acoustic scale.
gamaka_usage: Vachaspati is not usually projected through excessively heavy or harsh gamaka; its identity emerges through measured, rakti-laden phraseology that balances the brightness of M2 with the softer color of N2. Shadja and panchama function as stable nyasa points. R2 and G3 are important carriers of melodic life and are commonly given kampita or gentle oscillatory treatment in phrases such as S R2 G3 and G3 R2 S. M2 is a key color note and should be intoned carefully and clearly, often approached smoothly from G3 or resolved to P; its handling must preserve the prati-madhyama sheen without making the raga sound like Kalyani. D2 can take a mild kampita or curved approach in ascent toward N2 or in descent back to P. N2 is especially crucial: it is usually rendered with a soft, delicate touch, often with slight oscillation or glide from D2, and must be clearly distinguished from N3 to avoid a Kalyani flavor. Jaru-based movement such as D2~N2~>S and >S N2 D2 P, as well as smooth linking of P M2 G3 R2, helps bring out the raga’s contemplative and devotional character. In descent, the R2 tends to retain importance as a resolving tone, and phrase-based handling is preferable to scalar plainness. Overall, gamakas in Vachaspati are graceful and controlled rather than dense, emphasizing melodic continuity, repose on S and P, and the characteristic contrast between the luminous M2 and the tender N2.
notable_features: Meaning: 'Lord of speech'. Pronounced Vāchaspati. It is the 4th raga in the 11th chakra Rudra. Mnemonic name: Rudra-Bhu. Mnemonic phrase: sa ri gu mi pa dhi ni. It is close to Kalyani (65th melakarta) and differs only in the nishadham. According to the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Bhushāvati. It was borrowed into Hindustani music, where it is treated as a relatively recent Carnatic import adapted around the mid-20th century. Graha bhedam on Vachaspati yields Charukesi, Gourimanohari, and Natakapriya. A very famous janya raga of Vachaspati is Saraswathi, which is often used in film music.

mood: Kalyani has a grand, luminous, and auspicious emotional character. It evokes a sense of well-being, expansiveness, and spiritual brightness, which is why it is prominently heard in South Indian weddings and often chosen near the beginning of concerts. To a listener, it can feel elevating and serene at once: majestic without harshness, devotional without excessive melancholy, and radiant in a way that suggests blessing, celebration, and inner poise. Its wide scope for elaboration also gives it a contemplative and wonder-filled quality, making it suitable for both stately musical exploration and emotionally uplifting expression.
description: Kalyani is the 65th melakarta ragam in Carnatic music and is one of the major ragams of the tradition, often grouped with Sankarabharanam, Todi, and Kharaharapriya as a principal raga for elaboration and exploration. It is also called Mechakalyani. The word 'Kalyani' means 'she who causes auspicious things,' and the raga is considered highly auspicious; it is prominently played in South Indian weddings and is often performed at the beginning of concerts. Kalyani is the prati madhyama equivalent of Sankarabharanam, the 29th melakarta, and its Western equivalent is the Lydian mode. In Hindustani music it was called Kalyan and is now more popularly called Yaman. It is the 5th ragam in the 11th chakra, Rudra, with mnemonic name Rudra-Ma and mnemonic phrase 'sa ri gu mi pa dhi (or di) nu'. The swaras used are shadjam, chatushūruti rishabam, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, panchamam, chatushruti dhaivatam, and kakali nishadam. It is a sampurna raga, containing all seven notes in both ascent and descent. The source notes that Kalyani was the first prati madhyama raga ever discovered and that it was obtained by the process of graha bhedam or modal shift of tonic of the ancient Shadja Grama. Kalyani has great scope for elaborate alapana. It is very prominent among the public and is significant in the melakarta chart as a raga sung with all higher notes. Historically, Yaman/Aiman is said not to be an ancient raga; it is first mentioned in literature in the late 16th century, by which time it was already very popular. The Sahasras contains 45 dhrupad song-texts for Kalyan and five for Iman-Kalyan. According to Venkatamakhin (1620), Kalyan was a favourite melody to the Arabs, and Pundarika included Yaman among his 'Persian' ragas. Kalyani is often described as the 'queen of ragas' because of its breadth, prestige, and ability to support everything from beginner pedagogical pieces to weighty kritis, ragam-tanam-pallavi, padams, and javalis.
gamaka_usage: Kalyani supports extensive and elaborate alapana and is a gamaka-rich rakti raga despite its symmetric sampurna scale. The raga should not be rendered as a plain Lydian scale; its identity comes from characteristic oscillation and curved handling of key swaras, especially G3, M2, N3, and D2. M2 is the defining color of the raga and is often approached with grace from G3 or touched in flowing phrases such as G3 M2 P and N3 D2 P M2 G3. R2 and G3 typically take kampita or gentle nokku-based treatment rather than being left dry; G3 in particular helps establish the raga’s classical weight. N3 is often given a delicate oscillated or leaning treatment in descent and in phrases resolving to Sa, while D2 is usually shaped in relation to N3 and Pa rather than struck too starkly. Jaru movement between adjacent notes and curved prayogas are important, especially around the upper tetrachord and in phrases descending from the tara sthayi. Sa and Pa function as relatively steady nyasa/anchor swaras, but an important lakshana caution is that one should not dwell excessively on Pa or alternate too frequently between Sa and Pa, as over-emphasis on these structural notes can dilute Kalyani’s distinctive personality. The raga’s grandeur emerges through sustained phrases, kampita on the melodic swaras, and elegant glides that highlight the prati madhyama sheen. Compared with scalar or lighter Kalyan-type renderings, Carnatic Kalyani generally demands more gamaka depth and more explicit melodic shaping around M2 and N3.
notable_features: Kalyani is the prati madhyama equivalent of Sankarabharanam (melakarta 29). It is the 5th ragam in the 11th chakra Rudra; mnemonic name: Rudra-Ma; mnemonic phrase: 'sa ri gu mi pa dhi (or di) nu'. It is said to be the first prati madhyama raga discovered. It was obtained by graha bhedam or modal shift of tonic of the ancient Shadja Grama. When its notes are shifted using graha bhedam, it yields five other major melakarta ragams: Hanumatodi, Sankarabharanam, Natabhairavi, Kharaharapriya, and Harikambhoji. It has many janya ragams, among which Hamir Kalyani, Mohanakalyani, Amritha Kalyani, Saranga, Nada Kalyani, Sunadavinodini, and Yamunakalyani are especially popular. It is often performed at the beginning of concerts because it is considered auspicious. The source also notes that the track 'Kalaivaniye' in the film Sindhu Bhairavi is sung in Kalyani without an avarohanam. It is widely regarded as one of the 'major' ragas of Carnatic music and is often called the 'queen of ragas' because of its versatility, prestige, and immense scope for manodharma.

mood: Chitrambari has a bright, sharp, and striking melodic personality owing to its prati madhyama and shatshruti dhaivata content. It can evoke a sense of brilliance, wonder, and intensity rather than softness, giving it an arresting and somewhat majestic emotional color. The raga may suggest awe, spiritual grandeur, and a bold, radiant energy, making it suitable for compositions with devotional depth as well as intellectual or dramatic musical exploration.
description: Chitrambari (pronounced chitrāmbari) is a Carnatic raga and the 66th melakarta in the 72 melakarta system. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Chaturangini. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Naganandini, the 30th melakarta. Chitrambari is the 6th raga in the 11th chakra, Rudra. Its mnemonic name is Rudra-Sha and its mnemonic phrase is 'sa ra gu mi pa dhu nu'. As a melakarta, it is by definition a sampurna raga, containing all seven swaras in both ascent and descent. Its scale uses chathushruti rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, shatshruti dhaivatam, and kakali nishadam. Chitrambari has a few janya ragas, of which Amritavarshini is especially popular. Theoretical analysis notes that by graha bhedam, Chitrambari yields three other melakarta ragas: Shanmukhapriya, Shoolini, and Dhenuka.
gamaka_usage: The available material does not give a direct lakshana-style prescription of gamakas for Chitrambari. From the scale structure and its melakarta status, the raga is best handled with clear, disciplined gamaka treatment rather than heavy, diffuse oscillation. R2 and G3 can take measured kampita and light nokku, while M2 should be rendered with a bright, well-defined intonation that preserves the prati-madhyama color. The upper tetrachord is especially important: D3 and N3, being close and highly brilliant in effect, need precise placement and controlled oscillation; they should not be over-shaken to the point of blurring into neighboring shades. Jaru phrases linking P-D3-N3->S and the reverse descent can be used carefully, but the straight sampurna outline must remain audible. P is generally a stable resting point, and direct scalar movement is acceptable because the raga is not vakra. Overall, Chitrambari benefits from crisp phrasing, exact intonation, and moderate ornamentation that highlights its vivid, somewhat rarefied melodic identity.
notable_features: Pronounced chitrāmbari. In the Dikshitar tradition it is known as Chaturangini. It is the prati madhyamam counterpart of Naganandini (melakarta 30). In graha bhedam, its notes yield the melakarta ragas Shanmukhapriya, Shoolini, and Dhenuka. It belongs to the 11th chakra Rudra and carries the mnemonic name Rudra-Sha with mnemonic phrase 'sa ra gu mi pa dhu nu'. It has a few janya ragas, among which Amritavarshini is noted as very popular.

mood: Sucharitra has a stark, austere, and somewhat severe melodic personality. The combination of R3, G3, M2 with the low D1 and N1 gives it a tense, unusual color that can feel dry, introspective, and emotionally restrained rather than lush or immediately pleasing. Its effect is serious and meditative, with a faintly plaintive undercurrent. The raga can evoke the feeling of solitary reflection, intellectual concentration, or a quiet inward gaze rather than overt romance or festivity. Because it is rarely heard and carries an uncommon tonal color, it often leaves an impression of gravity, restraint, and contemplative stillness.
description: Sucharitra is the 67th melakarta raga in Carnatic music and a parent (melakarta) scale in the 72-melakarta system. It is the 1st raga in the 12th chakra, Aditya, with mnemonic name Aditya-Pa and mnemonic phrase 'sa ru gu mi pa dha na'. Its scale is sampurna in both ascent and descent: S R3 G3 M2 P D1 N1 S / S N1 D1 P M2 G3 R3 S. The swaras used are shatsruthi rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and shuddha nishadham, along with shadjam and panchamam. It is considered an unattractive raga and is rarely performed. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, it is called Santāna manjari. Sucharitra is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Yagapriya, the 31st melakarta. As a melakarta, it is by definition a sampurna raga. The text also notes that it currently has no janya ragas associated with it. Under graha bhedam, shifting its notes does not yield any other melakarta raga.
gamaka_usage: Sucharitra is not generally treated as a heavily ornamented rakti raga; its identity comes from the unusual scalar color and careful handling of its vivadi-rich intervals. The R3-G3 region should be approached with precision, since these adjacent upper variants can easily sound harsh or merely scalar if overdone. G3 can take a measured kampita, while R3 is usually touched with restraint and clarity rather than broad oscillation. M2 functions as an important point of repose and a bright pivot in phrases, often steadier than the surrounding swaras. In the lower tetrachord of the upper half, D1 and N1 are sensitive notes and benefit from controlled kampita or subtle nokku-like emphasis, helping bring out the raga's grave and plaintive color. P and S serve as the principal stable anchors. Jaru movements such as N1-D1-P and P-M2-G3 can be used gently, but excessive sliding may blur the raga's already delicate identity. Overall, the gamaka approach is best kept restrained, exact, and dignified: enough oscillation to animate D1, N1, and G3, but with clear intonation and phrase definition so the raga does not collapse into a bare scale or lose its austere character.
notable_features: 67th melakarta; 1st raga of the 12th chakra Aditya; mnemonic name Aditya-Pa; mnemonic phrase 'sa ru gu mi pa dha na'; prati madhyamam equivalent of Yagapriya (31st melakarta); considered unattractive and rarely performed; called Santāna manjari in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school and in the asampurna/Venkatamakhi tradition; currently has no janya ragas associated with it; graha bhedam does not produce any other melakarta raga.

mood: Jyoti swarupini has a bright, radiant, and uplifting character, with a sense of clarity and spiritual luminosity suggested by its very name. As a melakarta with a complete, symmetrical scale, it tends to evoke calm devotion and a dignified, contemplative serenity rather than intense pathos or agitation. The raga can suggest the imagery of light, inner awakening, and auspiciousness, making it suitable for reflective and devotional expression.
description: Jyotiswarupini is the 68th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system of Carnatic music. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Ragavardhini, the 32nd melakarta. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is also referred to as Jyōtirāga, Joti, or Jyōti. It is the 2nd rāgam in the 12th chakra, Aditya, with mnemonic name Aditya-Sri and mnemonic phrase sa ru gu mi pa dha ni. As a melakarta, it is a sampoorna rāgam with all seven notes in both ascent and descent. The notes used are shatsruthi rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and kaisiki nishadham. The source notes that a few minor janya rāgams are associated with it, but does not list them. It is also noted that shifting its notes by graha bhedam does not yield any other melakarta rāgam.
gamaka_usage: The source does not give explicit gamaka rules for Jyotiswarupini. As a melakarta with the scale S R3 G3 M2 P D1 N2 S / S N2 D1 P M2 G3 R3 S, the raga is generally rendered with smooth, well-connected prayogas rather than heavy oscillation. R3 and G3 are typically handled with controlled kampita or gentle jaru transitions to preserve the bright, sharp color of the scale, while M2 and P are usually steadier anchor notes. D1 and N2 may take light oscillation or slide-based treatment in phrases, but the raga’s identity depends more on clean scalar movement than on elaborate gamaka-heavy treatment. Compared with janya ragas, its gamaka profile is expected to be more straightforward and less phrase-specific.
notable_features: Jyotiswarupini is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Ragavardhini (32nd melakarta). It belongs to the 12th chakra Aditya and carries the mnemonic phrase sa ru gu mi pa dha ni. The scale uses shatsruthi rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and kaisiki nishadham. The raga is a melakarta, so it is inherently sampoorna. The article states that graha bhedam on this raga does not produce any other melakarta raga. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is known by alternate names Jyōtirāga, Joti, and Jyōti.

mood: Dhatuvardhani has a bright, expansive, and dignified mood, with the clarity and symmetry of a full melakarta scale. Its shatsruti rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham give it a polished, slightly intense but orderly character that can feel uplifting and contemplative at once. It suits a listener seeking a composed, devotional, and somewhat majestic atmosphere rather than overt pathos or romance.
description: Dhatuvardhani (pronounced Dhātuvardhani) is the 69th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system of Carnatic music. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Dhautapanchamam. It is the 3rd rāgam in the 12th chakra, Aditya, with mnemonic name Aditya-Go and mnemonic phrase sa ru gu mi pa dha nu. Its scale is sampurna in both ascent and descent: S R3 G3 M2 P D1 N3 S / S N3 D1 P M2 G3 R3 S. The notes used are shatsruthi rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham. As a melakarta, it is a parent scale and the prati madhyamam equivalent of Gangeyabhushani, the 33rd melakarta. The text notes that a few minor janya ragams are associated with it, though no specific janyas are listed. In graha bhedam, shifting the shadjam yields Gayakapriya, a minor melakarta rāgam.
gamaka_usage: As a plain melakarta scale, Dhatuvardhani is generally rendered with a relatively direct, orderly treatment of swaras rather than heavy vakra ornamentation. The source does not specify special gamaka rules, but by melakarta convention R3, G3, D1, and N3 may be given light oscillation or phrase-based ornamentation depending on composition, while M2 and P are typically steadier anchor notes. Because the scale is symmetrical and non-bhashanga, the gamaka treatment is expected to remain within the scale without anya-swara inflections.
notable_features: Melakarta #69; 3rd chakra position in Aditya chakra; mnemonic name Aditya-Go; mnemonic phrase sa ru gu mi pa dha nu; called Dhautapanchamam in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school; prati madhyamam equivalent of Gangeyabhushani (#33); graha bhedam from Dhatuvardhani yields Gayakapriya; a few minor janya ragams are associated with it, but the source does not enumerate them.

mood: Nasikabhushani has a poised, complete, and balanced feel, with the bright color of prati madhyama giving it a clear, slightly luminous character. It tends to sound calm, dignified, and devotional rather than restless or dramatic. The full seven-note structure creates a sense of order and stability, so the raga often evokes serenity, composure, and an uplifting reflective space.
description: Nasikabhushani is the 70th melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system of Carnatic music. It is the 4th rāgam in the 12th chakra, Aditya, with mnemonic name Aditya-Bhu and mnemonic phrase sa ru gu mi pa dhi ni. Its scale is S R3 G3 M2 P D2 N2 S / S N2 D2 P M2 G3 R3 S, using shatsruthi rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, chathusruthi dhaivatham, and kaisiki nishadham. As a melakarta, it is a sampoorna rāgam with all seven notes in both ascent and descent. It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Vagadheeswari, the 34th melakarta. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is called Nāsāmaṇi. The raga has a few minor janya rāgams associated with it. Graha bhedam from the dhaivatham (D2) yields Shadvidamargini. It corresponds to the Hungarian major scale in Western music.
gamaka_usage: The source text does not give explicit gamaka prescriptions, but as a melakarta with a straightforward sampoorna structure, the swaras are generally rendered with smooth, connected prayogas rather than heavy vakra movement. R3, G3, D2, and N2 are typically handled with the characteristic Carnatic oscillatory treatment appropriate to their positions, while S and P are usually stable anchors and M2 is held clearly as the prati madhyama. The raga’s identity comes from clean scalar movement and the proper handling of the higher rishabha/gandhara and dhaivata/nishada rather than from unusual gamaka complexity.
notable_features: Melakarta #70; 4th rāgam in chakra 12 (Aditya); mnemonic phrase sa ru gu mi pa dhi ni; prati madhyama equivalent of Vagadheeswari (#34); called Nāsāmaṇi in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school; graha bhedam from D2 yields Shadvidamargini; associated with a few minor janya ragams; Western correspondence noted as Hungarian major scale.

mood: Kosalam has a bright, expansive, and orderly feel, with the clarity of a complete melakarta scale. Its prati madhyama coloring gives it a slightly elevated, luminous tension, while the straight sampurna ascent and descent create balance, composure, and a sense of spaciousness. The raga can evoke devotion and serenity, along with a subtle sense of wonder because of its high-positioned scale and distinctive tonal color. It suits contemplative, majestic, and devotional settings, with an uplifting character rather than deep pathos.
description: Kosalam (pronounced kōsalam) is the 71st melakarta rāgam in the 72-melakarta system of Carnatic music and is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Shoolini, the 35th melakarta. It is the 5th rāgam in the 12th chakra, Aditya, with mnemonic name Aditya-Ma and mnemonic phrase sa ru gu mi pa dhi nu. Its scale is sampoorna, with all seven notes in both ascent and descent: S R3 G3 M2 P D2 N3 S / S N3 D2 P M2 G3 R3 S. The notes are shatsruthi rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, chathusruthi dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham. The raga is also known as Kusumākaram in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. Kosalam has one janya rāgam associated with it, though none is named in the provided text. Its notes under graha bhedam yield three other melakarta rāgams: Keeravani, Hemavati, and Vakulabharanam. The text also mentions a few compositions set to this rāgam and notes that film songs such as 'Sundari Kannal' from Dalapathy and 'Paarthene' from Mookuthi Amman are based on Kosalam.
gamaka_usage: The source text does not explicitly describe gamakas for Kosalam. As a melakarta with a straight sampurna scale, the swaras are generally rendered with the standard Carnatic treatment for this note set: R3 and G3 are typically given gentle oscillation or connected by smooth jaru-like movement, M2 is held clearly as the prati madhyama anchor, and D2 and N3 are often approached with light gamaka or slides to preserve the raga's bright, expansive color. Because the scale is linear and non-vakra, gamaka usage is usually more about graceful continuity than heavy zig-zag ornamentation.
notable_features: Kosalam is the 71st melakarta and the prati madhyama counterpart of Shoolini (35th melakarta). It belongs to the 12th chakra Aditya and uses the mnemonic phrase sa ru gu mi pa dhi nu. Graha bhedam on Kosalam yields Keeravani, Hemavati, and Vakulabharanam. The text states that it has one janya rāgam associated with it, but does not name it. It is also called Kusumākaram in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. The page mentions film songs based on this raga: 'Sundari Kannal' from Dalapathy and 'Paarthene' from Mookuthi Amman.

mood: Rasikapriya has a bright, expansive, and climactic feel, with the polished brilliance of a late melakarta in its highest-practice स्वर forms. Its sound is bold, symmetrical, and intellectually satisfying, often evoking grandeur, completion, and uplift. Because it is the final melakarta, it can feel conclusive and पूर्ण, with a majestic, celebratory character rather than a soft or introspective one. The raga suits a listener who enjoys clarity, order, and a sense of musical culmination.
description: Rasikapriya is the 72nd and last melakarta rāgam in the Carnatic 72-melakarta system. It is the 6th rāgam in the 12th chakra, Aditya, with mnemonic name Aditya-Sha and mnemonic phrase sa ru gu mi pa dhu nu. Its scale is sampoorna, using shatsruthi rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, shatsruthi dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham: S R3 G3 M2 P D3 N3 S / S N3 D3 P M2 G3 R3 S. The raga is also known as Rasamanjari in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. It is the prati madhyama equivalent of Chalanata, the 36th melakarta. The source notes that graha bhedam on Rasikapriya yields Mayamalavagowla and Simhendramadhyamam. It has a few minor janya ragas associated with it, though the provided text does not enumerate them.
gamaka_usage: As a melakarta with the highest variants of R, G, D, and N, Rasikapriya is generally rendered with clear, well-defined intonation and controlled gamaka rather than heavy oscillation. R3, G3, D3, and N3 may take light kampita or subtle oscillatory treatment, but the oscillation should not blur their identity. M2 and P are usually held steady and serve as anchor notes. Jaru-like transitions between adjacent swaras can be used to preserve the scale’s linear, sampurna character. Because it is a parent scale, gamaka treatment should remain balanced and disciplined, keeping the swaras distinct.
notable_features: Last (72nd) melakarta; 6th in the 12th chakra Aditya; mnemonic name Aditya-Sha; mnemonic phrase sa ru gu mi pa dhu nu. It is the prati madhyama equivalent of Chalanata (36th melakarta). Graha bhedam from Rasikapriya yields Mayamalavagowla and Simhendramadhyamam. Also known as Rasamanjari in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school. The provided text mentions that it has a few minor janya ragas associated with it, but does not list them.

mood: Abheri has a soft, plaintive, and deeply expressive mood. It often feels tender and introspective, with a gentle melancholy that can evoke longing, devotion, and quiet emotional surrender. The raga can also sound soothing and serene, especially in its more traditional treatment, while the use of D1 in older phrases adds a more wistful, sorrow-tinged shade. It suits reflective, devotional, and emotionally nuanced settings, and its close resemblance to Bhimpalasi gives it a familiar, yearning fragrance.
description: Abheri is a janya raga of Kharaharapriya (22nd melakarta) and is commonly rendered in an audava-sampoorna form. The commonly cited scale is S G2 M1 P N2 S / S N2 D2 P M1 G2 R2 S, but the raga has important historical and stylistic variants. Older sources and pre-1960 classifications often treat it as a Natabhairavi janya with Shuddha Dhaivatam (D1) in the descent, and some traditional versions still preserve this older form. The text notes that the original Abheri had D1 rather than D2 in avarohanam, and that the present form was influenced by Hindustani Bhimpalasi. There are at least two D1-bearing versions mentioned: Thyagaraja’s version with N2 in the ascent (S G2 M1 P N2 S / S N2 D1 P M1 G2 R2 S), and Dikshitar’s version with a more restricted ascent and vakra prayoga (S M1 G2 M1 P P S S / S N2 D1 P M1 G2 R2 S). The raga is said to sound close to Hindustani Bhimpalasi/Bheempalas and Dhanashree, and the current version is also compared to Bageshri because of the soft rendering of Gandhara. Abheri is a bhashanga raga because of the occasional use of D1, though some sources alternatively analyze the D2-bearing form as a janya of Kharaharapriya and the D1-bearing form as a janya of Natabhairavi. It has graha bhedam relationships yielding Mohanakalyani, Kedaragaula, and Arabhi when shifted from the Kharaharapriya-based scale.
gamaka_usage: Gandhara is treated with delicate oscillation and soft emphasis; in the Bhimpalasi-like modern form it is often rendered gently, which is part of the raga’s plaintive color. R2 and M1 are the most stable swaras and can be prolonged without gamaka. G2 typically takes light to moderate kampita and often resolves toward R2; phrases such as S G R G M are characteristic in related treatment and help preserve the raga’s identity. D2 in the common form and D1 in older phrases must be handled carefully, with D1 giving a more melancholic shade. N2 is important in the ascent and descent, and the phrase P N2 S is characteristic in the Thyagaraja version. The text specifically notes that in Dikshitar’s variant, S M1 G2 M1 is a vakra prayoga and that S G2 M1 and P N2 S are not allowed there, indicating stricter phraseology. Overall, the raga depends more on nuanced phrase-shape and gentle oscillation than on heavy gamaka; the subtle treatment of G2 and the dhaivata distinction are central to its identity.
notable_features: Abheri has a disputed historical identity: older sources classify it as a Natabhairavi janya with D1, while the modern common form is treated as a Kharaharapriya janya with D2 and occasional D1 as anya swara. The raga has at least two named D1-based variants in the text: a Thyagaraja version and a Dikshitar version with stricter prayogas. It is related by graha bhedam to Mohanakalyani, Kedaragaula, and Arabhi (using the Kharaharapriya-based scale). The text also notes close resemblance to Karnataka Devagandhari and Bhimpalasi, and that the subtle Gandhara treatment can make it feel like Bageshri. The raga is associated with the original version of Nagumomu and with the older form of Veenabheri and Kandha Vandharul.

mood: Abhogi has a calm, restrained, and gently uplifting mood. Its pentatonic structure gives it a clean, uncluttered feel, often creating a serene and introspective atmosphere rather than intense drama. Because it is frequently used for medium to fast-tempo compositions, it can also feel graceful and lightly animated, suitable for devotional expression, reflective beauty, and a composed, inward-looking emotional space. In Hindustani usage it is associated with a late-night ambience, while in Carnatic music it is often heard as a raga that balances simplicity with elegance.
description: Abhogi is a Carnatic janya raga derived from Kharaharapriya (22nd melakarta), though it can also be derived from Gourimanohari by omitting both panchamam and nishadam. It is a symmetric audava-audava pentatonic scale with five notes in both ascent and descent, and it omits Pa and Ni entirely. The scale is S R2 G2 M1 D2 S : S D2 M1 G2 R2 S. The raga is also adapted into Hindustani music, where it is known as Abhogi Kanada or simply Abhogi and is classified under Kafi thaat. In Hindustani theory it is treated as a Kanada-type raga, with a characteristic vakra phrase G M R S used straight in the Carnatic version as a notable difference. Graha bhedam on Abhogi yields Valaji, and according to P. Moutal, Kalavati is a transposition of Abhogi. The raga is used for compositions in medium to fast tempo and has been employed by many composers in both classical and film music. Its close scale relations include Sriranjani and Jayamanohari, which share similar note sets but differ by the presence or absence of N2 and by descending treatment.
gamaka_usage: Abhogi is generally rendered with restrained gamaka, keeping the raga clean and uncluttered. G2 is the most characteristic swara and is often approached from M1 with a gentle slide or light oscillation to preserve the Kanada flavour; in Hindustani-influenced treatment it may carry a more pronounced and lingering oscillation. R2 and M1 are comparatively stable anchor notes and are often held plainly or with only mild kampita. D2 is usually clean and functional, serving as a simple upper support note rather than a heavily ornamented pitch. The descent commonly emphasizes the straight phrase D2 M1 G2 R2 S, and the Kanada-like phrase G2 M1 R2 S is important, though in Carnatic usage it is rendered more directly and less heavily vakra than in Hindustani Abhogi Kanada. Overall, gamakas are subtle rather than expansive, with slides and light kampita on G2 being the main expressive device.
notable_features: Abhogi is an audava-audava janya raga with no panchamam or nishadam. It is considered a janya of Kharaharapriya (22nd melakarta), though it can also be derived from Gourimanohari by dropping Pa and Ni. Graha bhedam from Abhogi yields Valaji, and P. Moutal notes Kalavati as a transposition. In Hindustani music it is known as Abhogi Kanada and assigned to Kafi thaat. A key stylistic point is the Kanada phrase G2 M1 R2 S, which is treated straight in Carnatic usage. The raga is popular for medium-to-fast compositions and is used in both classical and film music.

mood: Ahiri has a deeply compassionate, plaintive, and introspective mood. It is associated foremost with karuna rasa, so it tends to evoke tenderness, empathy, and a gentle sense of sorrow rather than agitation. Because it is also considered an early-morning scale, it can feel quiet, reflective, and spiritually inward, like a prayerful dawn atmosphere. The raga is difficult to master, but when rendered well it feels rewarding and emotionally rich, with subtle shades that can move the listener toward devotion and calm contemplation.
description: Ahiri is an ancient Carnatic janya rāgam associated primarily with the 14th melakarta Vakulabharanam, though it has also been associated with the 8th melakarta Hanumatodi. It is a sampurna rāgam in the sense that it uses all seven swaras, but it is classified as a janya because of its vakra (zig-zag) prayogas and bhashanga usage. The source notes that it is mentioned in Sangita Makarandha and Sangita samayasara, indicating its antiquity. Several arohana-avarohaṇa forms are given due to foreign-note usage: S R1 M1 G3 M1 P D1 N2 S, and also S R1 S G3 M1 P D1 N2 S, with avarohaṇa S N2 D1 P M1 G3 R1 S. It is described as synonymous with karuna rasa and as an early-morning scale. The raga is noted to have subtle srutis, and its notes Ri, Ga, Dha and Ni are treated as differing from the parent scale. It is a difficult raga to master but rewarding in effect. The text also implies a strong bhashanga character and a close relationship to the parent melakarta scale through altered note usage.
gamaka_usage: Ahiri is rendered with very subtle srutis and delicate ornamentation rather than heavy, overt gamakas. The text specifically notes that Ri, Ga, Dha and Ni are different from the parent scale and that the raga is considered to have a lot of subtle srutis, so these swaras are typically approached with nuanced oscillation and microtonal shading. The vakra prayogas require careful jaru-like movement through the zig-zag phrases, especially in the ascent where S–R1–M1–G3–M1–P is characteristic. G3 and N2 are not treated as plain, rigid notes; they are usually colored gently, while S and P tend to remain comparatively stable as structural anchors. Because the raga is difficult to master, precise intonation and restrained gamaka are essential; over-ornamentation would obscure its plaintive character. It is also described as a gamaka-vārika raktirāgam with many subtle shrutis, so the gamaka treatment is expressive but controlled, with emphasis on delicate kampita and smooth transitions rather than forceful oscillation.

notable_compositions.name: Adhiviravil En Munne
notable_compositions.composer: H H Maharanyam Sri Sri Muralidhara Swamiji

notable_compositions.name: Ay Chand Teri Chandni Ki Kasam

notable_features: Ancient rāgam mentioned in Sangita Makarandha and Sangita samayasara. Classified as a janya rāgam because of vakra swaras and bhashanga prayogas, despite being sampurna in note count. Associated with the 14th melakarta Vakulabharanam, though also linked in some traditions to the 8th melakarta Hanumatodi. The raga is explicitly described as synonymous with karuna rasa and as an early-morning scale. It is said to use foreign notes relative to the parent scale, with Ri, Ga, Dha and Ni treated as differing from the parent. The text also notes that it has a lot of subtle srutis and is difficult to master but rewarding. It is described as a gamaka-vārika raktirāgam and is used in kṛtis, operas, dramas, and dance forms.

mood: Amritavarshini has a bright, uplifting, and refreshing mood, with a clear pentatonic openness that feels buoyant and expansive. Its sound often suggests a gentle shower of nectar or rain, creating an atmosphere of hope, delight, and auspiciousness. Because of its association with rain and the famous belief that it can bring showers, it can evoke wonder and a sense of the miraculous, while also carrying a devotional sweetness in concert and temple settings.
description: Amritavarshini is a popular janya rāgam in Carnatic music, created in the early nineteenth century by Muthuswami Dikshitar. It is an audava (pentatonic) symmetric scale using only five notes: shadjam, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, panchamam, and kakali nishadam. The rāgam is popularly associated with rain; its name comes from Sanskrit amrita (nectar) and varshini (one who causes a shower or rain). A well-known tradition says that Dikshitar brought rain at Ettayapuram, Tamil Nadu, by singing his composition Aanandaamrutakarshini amrutavarshini. It is generally considered a janya rāgam of Chitrambari, the 66th melakarta, though it can also be derived by omitting rishabham and dhaivatam from other melakartas such as Kalyani, Gamanashrama, or Vishwambari. The text also notes that another scale with the same name exists but is less practiced, associated with the 39th melakarta Jhalavarali. Because of its symmetry and pentatonic structure, the rāgam lends itself to extensive elaboration and is used in both classical and film music. It has a distinct, pleasing, and accessible melodic identity, and is especially known for its bright, rain-laden, nectar-like character.
gamaka_usage: The rāgam is built on a very stable pentatonic framework, so the swaras are generally rendered with clean, well-defined intonation rather than heavy oscillation. S, P, and the upper shadjam are typically held steady. G3 and N3 may take light, graceful gamaka or gentle slides to give the rāgam its sweetness, while M2 is also treated clearly and prominently as a structural anchor. Because the scale omits R and D, phrases tend to move directly between S–G3 and P–N3 with smooth jaru-like transitions, producing a flowing, airy feel. The overall treatment is more transparent and less ornament-dense than in vakra or bhashanga ragas, and the gamakas should preserve the rāgam’s bright, crystalline character rather than obscure the pentatonic outline.

notable_compositions.name: Devi ni en pon veena nadam

notable_features: Created in the early nineteenth century by Muthuswami Dikshitar. Believed to bring rain; the famous anecdote is that Dikshitar sang Aanandaamrutakarshini amrutavarshini at Ettayapuram and rain fell. It is a symmetric audava-audava rāgam with no rishabham or dhaivatam. Considered a janya of Chitrambari (66th melakarta), though derivable from other melakartas by omitting R and D. Graha bhedam from Amritavarshini yields Karnataka Shuddha Saveri. Scale-similar ragas mentioned are Hamsadhvani and Gambhiranata. Another less-practiced scale with the same name is associated with Jhalavarali (39th melakarta).

mood: Amritha Kalyani has a bright, uplifting, and auspicious character, with a serene devotional sweetness. Its pentatonic ascent gives it a clean, open, floating quality, while the fuller descent adds warmth and completeness. The raga tends to evoke calm joy, prayerful devotion, and a gentle sense of grace, making it suitable for reflective or celebratory settings rather than intense pathos or drama.
description: Amritha Kalyani is a Carnatic janya raga created by composer Mahesh Mahadev. It is derived from the 65th melakarta, Mechakalyani, and is an audava-sampurna raga: the ascent is pentatonic and omits R and D, while the descent is sampurna with all notes of the parent scale except that the source text specifically presents the scale as S G3 M2 P N3 S' / S' N3 D2 P M2 G3 R2 S. It is described as equivalent to the Western Lydian mode, though with the second and sixth notes omitted in the ascending form. The raga is also referred to as Amrith Kalyan in Hindustani music. The source notes that it is asymmetric and that the ascending scale lacks rishabham and dhaivatam. Compositions mentioned in this raga include 'Achyutam Keshavam' by Mahesh Mahadev and 'Ragapravaham' by Ganesh Kumaresh. It is a modern derivative of Kalyani and shares the parent raga's bright, expansive, and auspicious color while remaining distinct through its reduced ascent.
gamaka_usage: The source does not give explicit gamaka rules, but the scale suggests a smooth, lyrical treatment. The ascent is best rendered plainly and lightly, with emphasis on the clean pentatonic contour S–G3–M2–P–N3–S. In descent, the raga can take fuller Carnatic ornamentation on G3, R2, D2, and N3, with gentle slides into M2 and P to preserve the Kalyani-like brightness. Because the ascent omits R and D, those notes should not be stressed there; they belong to the descending phrase and should be introduced naturally with jaru-like movement rather than abrupt attack. Compared with plain Kalyani, the raga’s gamaka treatment is more restrained in ascent and more complete in descent. G3 and N3 may be given mild kampita or delicate oscillation, while M2 and P are often approached with smooth jaru. R2 and D2 are best used as connecting descent notes rather than as heavily gamaka-laden anchors, preserving the raga's airy, graceful character.
notable_features: Created by Mahesh Mahadev. Janya of Mechakalyani (65th melakarta). Audava-sampurna structure. Asymmetric scale with omitted R and D in ascent. Equivalent to the Western Lydian mode, but with the second and sixth notes omitted in the ascending form. Also named Amrith Kalyan in Hindustani music.

inventor: Syama Sastri is said to have made this raga popular and given it its present form
mood: Anandabhairavi is a very old, melodious raga that conveys happiness, tenderness, and a gentle devotional warmth. Its very name suggests joy, and the scale tends to create a soft, pleasing, rakti-filled atmosphere rather than brilliance or aggression. Listeners often feel comforted, affectionate, and emotionally soothed, as if hearing a graceful, intimate expression of devotion or feminine grace. It suits reflective, auspicious, and emotionally rich settings, with a happy yet restrained character.
description: Anandabhairavi or Ananda Bhairavi is an ancient and highly melodious Carnatic raga, also used in Indian traditional and regional musics. It is a janya raga of the 20th melakarta Natabhairavi. Though it is a sampoorna raga with all seven swaras, it is not a melakarta because of its vakra prayoga and its use of anya swaras in phrases. It is classified as a bhashanga and rakti raga. The source notes three anya swaras used only in prayogas: G3, D1, and N3, while the arohana-avarohaṇa itself uses the parent-scale notes. The raga is strongly associated with Syama Sastri, who is said to have popularized it and given it its present form. It is also noted for unique phraseology in manodharma and compositions, and for being distinct from Reetigowla because one should not linger long on nishadam. The raga appears prominently in major compositions by Syama Sastri, Tyagaraja, Muthuswami Dikshitar, Papanasam Sivan, Swathi Thirunal, Annamacharya, Purandara Dasa, and many others. In the Kamalamba Navavarna set of Muthuswami Dikshitar, Anandabhairavi is the raga of the second kriti, and the set is highlighted as one of the classic showcases of the raga. The text also mentions a defining modern illustration, 'Yaadhum Oore' from Sandham: Symphony Meets Classical Tamil, and a Kuchipudi-related anecdote involving Tyagaraja and the song Madhura Nagarilo set in this raga. The raga is also described as ancient, with origins in South Indian folk traditions, and as one that spreads a feeling of joy and fulfillment.
gamaka_usage: Anandabhairavi uses characteristic vakra and phrase-based gamakas rather than a plain scalar treatment. The source highlights recurring prayogas such as 'sa ga ga ma', 'sa pa', and 'sa ga ma pa'. G2 is central and often handled with gentle oscillation and curved movement; R2 is approached and left with smooth jaru-like motion; M1 and P are generally stable anchor notes. D2 is used in the scale, but the anya D1 appears only in phrases like 'ga ma pa da', so dhaivatam treatment must be carefully differentiated between the scale note and the foreign shade. G3 appears in 'ma pa ma ga ga ma' as an ornamental phrase note, and N3 appears subtly in 'sa da ni sa'. The musician is specifically not allowed to stay long on nishadam, so N2 should be touched lightly and released quickly. Overall, the raga depends on delicate kampita and phrase contours, with restrained, graceful oscillation rather than heavy gamaka. Compared with Reetigowla, its nishada handling is more fleeting and less lingering. It is also described as thriving on melodic oscillations and majestic glides.
notable_features: Janya of Natabhairavi (20th melakarta). Sampoorna but not melakarta because of vakra prayoga and anya swaras. Classified as bhashanga and rakti raga. The source explicitly names three anya swaras: G3, D1, and N3, used only in prayogas. Popular patterns include 'sa ga ga ma', 'sa pa', and 'sa ga ma pa'. The raga should not linger on nishadam. Syama Sastri is credited with making it popular and shaping its present form. It is considered one of the favorite ragas of Syama Sastri. The Kamalamba Navavarna kriti 'Kamalamba Samrakshathu' is in Anandabhairavi. The text also notes that Tyagaraja and Muthuswami Dikshitar do not use any anya swaras in their compositions, though the raga itself permits them in prayoga. A modern defining example cited is 'Yaadhum Oore' by Raleigh Rajan. The raga is said to have a life-changing anecdote connected with Tyagaraja and a Kuchipudi Bhagavata recital. It is considered melodious, old, and happiness-inducing. It is also described as having origins in South Indian folk traditions and as a raga that spreads joy and fulfillment.

mood: Andolika has a gentle, lilting, and soothing character, with a soft devotional warmth rather than intense drama. Its pentatonic ascent and compact descent create a calm, graceful emotional space that feels serene and lightly contemplative. The raga can evoke tenderness, quiet joy, nostalgic sweetness, and a devotional inwardness, making it suitable for reflective singing and elegant melodic elaboration.
description: Andolika is a Carnatic janya raga, also written as Andholika, derived from the 22nd melakarta Kharaharapriya. It is an asymmetric audava-audava raga with five notes in both ascent and descent. Its arohana is S R2 M1 P N2 S and avarohana is S N2 D2 M1 R2 S. The descent is panchama-varjam, replacing panchamam with chatushruti dhaivatam in the avarohana. The text notes that because gandharam is omitted, the scale can also be viewed as a janya of the 28th melakarta Harikambhoji, though the Kharaharapriya association is more commonly preferred because of historical ordering. The raga is related by scale similarity to Madhyamavati and Kedaragaula. It has been used for several varnams and kritis, and its structure gives it a clean, compact melodic identity. Web snippets describe it as gentle, meditative, timeless, nostalgic, and soaked in devotion, and it is especially associated with Tyagaraja's Raga Sudha Rasa, which helped establish its identity in concert practice.
gamaka_usage: Andolika is generally rendered with restrained, graceful gamaka treatment rather than heavy oscillation. R2 and N2 may take light kampita or gentle stress, but they should not be over-ornamented. M1 and P function as relatively stable anchor notes, giving the raga its clean pentatonic outline. D2 in the descent is a key characteristic note and is usually articulated clearly, often approached by a smooth jaru from N2 or used in a soft glide into M1. The phrases should preserve the raga's lilting, meditative quality; excessive gamaka on every note can obscure its identity. Compared with more heavily gamaka-laden ragas, Andolika benefits from crisp phrase boundaries and elegant, flowing prayogas such as S R2 M1 P N2 S and S N2 D2 M1 R2 S.
notable_features: Audava-audava asymmetric janya of Kharaharapriya (22nd melakarta). The avarohana is panchama-varjam, using D2 instead of P in descent. The text explicitly notes an alternate theoretical association with Harikambhoji (28th melakarta) because gandharam is omitted. Scale-similarity relations are given with Madhyamavati and Kedaragaula.
