Web snippets also note that Tyagaraja's Raga Sudha Rasa was important in popularizing the raga, and that Andolika is sometimes discussed as a South Indian raga adopted into Hindustani music under the name Raag Andolika. The listed compositions are all set to Adi talam, though tala is not attached to each item in the source text individually.

mood: Arabhi is an ancient, highly auspicious raga with a bright, energetic, and valour-filled character. It tends to evoke a sense of uplift, confidence, and ceremonial grandeur rather than deep pathos. The raga feels brisk and open, suited to spirited singing, festive occasions, and music that inspires courage and forward motion. Its relatively plain-note treatment gives it a direct, clean, and assertive emotional profile, while its devotional associations also lend it a sacred, prayerful atmosphere.
description: Arabhi or Aarabhi is a janya raga derived from Shankarabharanam (29th melakarta). It is described as a combination of the pentatonic scale Shuddha Saveri and the sampurna scale Shankarabharanam. The raga dates back to 7 AD and was originally called pazhanthakkam in ancient Tamil music. It is considered a very auspicious raga and is associated with Veera rasa. Arabhi is one of the five Ghana ragams and is especially brilliant when thanam is played on the veena. Its scale is audava-sampurna: the arohana has five notes, while the avarohana is complete. The raga is upanga and uses only N3, unlike Devagandhari which can use N2 and is bhashanga. Arabhi is closely related to Devagandhari, but differs in the treatment of ga, ri, and phraseology; in particular, phrases like pa ma da sa are avoided in Arabhi. It is noted for its energy and for brigas more than elaborate gamaka-laden exposition. The famous Pancharatna Kriti Sadhinchane by Saint Tyagaraja is set in Arabhi, and the Onam song Maveli Naadu Vaneedum Kaalam is also popularly taught in this raga.
gamaka_usage: Arabhi is said to use very little gamaka and frequency variation, relying mainly on flat, steady notes. The gandharam is kept very close to madhyamam, so the phrase ma ga ri tends to sound almost like ma ma ri. Likewise, nishadam stays very close to shadjam, so sa ni da can sound like sa sa da. Rishabham is not fluctuated, and the raga favors plain, direct intonation over oscillation. Compared with Devagandhari, Arabhi avoids the asaivu on ri and does not use the phrase pa ma da sa. It is more suited to brigas and crisp phrase movement than to heavy gamaka treatment. The swaras R2, M1, P, D2, and N3 are generally rendered cleanly, with G3 treated as a near-M1 note rather than a strongly oscillated tone.
notable_features: Ancient raga dating to 7 AD; originally called pazhanthakkam in ancient Tamil music. One of the five Ghana ragams and especially effective in thanam on veena. Upanga raga using only N3. Closely related to Devagandhari, but Arabhi avoids Devagandhari-specific phraseology and gamaka style. The text explicitly notes that Thyagaraja uses phrases like 'sa sa da' in Sadhinchane, though 'sa ni da' also occurs. Arabhi is described as very energetic and more conducive to brigas than gamakas.

mood: Asaveri has a plaintive, devotional, and gently meditative mood. Its pentatonic ascent gives it a simple, austere, and prayerful character, while the vakra descent and foreign notes add a touch of yearning and emotional depth. The raga can evoke quiet surrender, introspection, and a soft sense of pathos, making it suitable for devotional and reflective settings rather than brilliance or exuberance.
description: Asaveri is an ancient Carnatic rāgam mentioned in the Sangita Ratnakara. It is a janya rāgam derived from the 8th melakarta, Hanumatodi. The raga is asymmetric and bhashanga in nature: its ascending scale omits gandharam and nishadam, while the descending scale is vakra and includes foreign notes relative to the parent scale. It is classified as audava-vakra-sampurna, with a pentatonic ascent and a fuller, zigzag descent. The scale is closely related in feel to Komal Asavari and Asa Todi of Hindustani music. The text also notes that the Hindustani Asavari resembles Natabhairavi in Carnatic music. Asaveri is a well-established concert and compositional raga with many kritis and varnams, especially by Tyagaraja, Muthuswami Dikshitar, and other major composers.
gamaka_usage: The raga’s gamaka treatment is shaped by its bhashanga and vakra nature. R1 is a key anchor note and is typically handled with clear emphasis and kampita/ornamental stress. M1 and P are generally steadier structural tones, often sustained or connected by smooth jaru. D1 is important in both ascent and descent and is usually given a firm, slightly ornamented treatment. In the descent, N2 is not treated as a plain scalar note but as a characteristic foreign color tone, often approached and left with delicate oscillation or slide. The anya swaras R2 and G2 in the avarohana are especially expressive and should be rendered with careful gamaka, not as rigidly flat notes; they help create the raga’s distinctive pathos. Because the scale is vakra, straight scalar rendering is less effective than curved, phrase-based prayogas. Compared with closely related Todi-type ragas, Asaveri’s descent is lighter and more plaintive, with the foreign notes giving it a unique veena-like color.
notable_features: Asaveri is a bhaashaanga ragam because its avarohanam contains chatushruti rishabham, a foreign swara relative to the parent Todi scale. The text explicitly identifies the anya swaras as Chaturshruti Rishabham (R2) / Shuddha gandharam (G1) and Chaturshruti dhaivatam (D2) / Shuddha nishadam (N1), with the veena rendering them from G1 and N1. It is an ancient raga mentioned in Sangita Ratnakara. The raga is structurally audava-vakra-sampurna. It resembles Komal Asavari and Asa Todi of Hindustani music. The source lists many popular kritis and two varnams in this raga.

mood: Atana has a bright, forceful, and highly theatrical character. It is described as very catchy and capable of giving a strong stage flavor when a concert feels dull, so it tends to wake up the audience and create a sense of energy, confidence, and dramatic uplift. Its emotional world is bold rather than tender, often evoking courage, public celebration, and a spirited, dramatic atmosphere suited to drama music and performance settings.
description: Atana, also spelled Athana or Adana, is a janya raga derived from the 29th melakarta, Sankarabharanam (Dhirasankarabharanam). It is a well-known Carnatic raga with a strong association with drama music and stage performance, valued for its catchy, attention-grabbing appeal. The raga is described as organically developed and not bound by a rigid linear ascent and descent; instead, it relies on characteristic interwoven phrases in improvisation. Its standard scale is arohana S R2 M1 P N3 S and avarohana S N3 D2 P M1 P G3 R2 S. The source notes that the raga uses chatushruti rishabham, antara gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, chatushruti dhaivatam, and kakali nishadam, while also being bhashanga with anya swaras G2 and N2. A rare feature mentioned is the use of kakali nishada in descent. The raga is associated with veeram (courage) and is especially effective in dramatic and concert settings where a lively, attention-grabbing mood is desired. It is unrelated to the Hindustani raga Adana despite the similar name.
gamaka_usage: Atana is treated with characteristic phrase-based gamakas rather than a plain scalar rendering. The raga’s identity depends on interwoven prayogas, with strong emphasis on kampita and oscillatory treatment in the melodic phrases, especially around R2, G3, D2, and N3. M1 and P are often handled as stable anchor notes, with P frequently repeated or touched in phrase loops. The descent is particularly important for its vakra movement and phrase turns, and the rare descental use of kakali nishada (N3) is a defining color. As a bhashanga raga, the anya swaras G2 and N2 may appear as delicate phrase-inflections rather than as structural scale tones, and they should not be overemphasized. The raga should sound lively, clipped, and idiomatic, not like a straight Shankarabharanam derivative.
notable_features: Very common in drama music; considered highly catchy and stage-effective. Gives a strong veera/courageous flavor. Bhashanga raga with anya swaras G2 and N2. Rarely uses kakali nishada in descent. Not strictly linear in arohana-avaroha; phrase-based and organically developed. Unrelated to Hindustani Adana. No compositions were explicitly listed in the provided text.

mood: Bahudari has a bright, lively, and delightful emotional character, yet it remains graceful and uncluttered because of the omission of rishabham. The raga feels open and direct, with a sweet devotional warmth that makes it especially effective for bhakti-oriented lyrics. At the same time, its clean scalar outline and pleasing melodic turns create a sense of freshness and gentle wonder, giving it a serene but uplifting atmosphere suitable for concert exposition and lyrical beauty.
description: Bahudari is a derived Carnatic rāgam (janya rāgam) from the 28th melakarta, Harikambhoji. It is an asymmetric shadava-audava scale, meaning it has six notes in ascent and five notes in descent. The raga omits rishabham entirely and uses the swaras S, G3, M1, P, D2, and N2. Its structure is ārohaṇa S G3 M1 P D2 N2 S and avarohaṇa S N2 P M1 G3 S. The name is often understood as suggesting "many paths," reflecting its versatile melodic possibilities. Bahudari is described as melodious, bright, and pleasing, and it is widely used in concerts for its attractive and devotional character. It is a janya of Harikambhoji and therefore not a melakarta. The provided sources do not mention a formal inventor beyond the note that it is created by Tyagaraja Swamy. The raga is well represented in composition literature across composers and languages, especially in devotional and concert repertoire.
gamaka_usage: Bahudari is generally rendered with smooth, lyrical gamaka treatment rather than heavy oscillation. G3 is the most characteristic swara and is often given a gentle kampita or a soft slide into M1, helping preserve the raga’s sweetness. D2 and N2 may also be touched with subtle kampita or jaru-like movement, especially in phrases leading to the upper shadjam and in the descent. P and M1 are usually steadier anchor notes. The raga’s identity depends strongly on the clean omission of R, so phrases should avoid any rishabha-like inflection. Characteristic movements such as S–G3–M1 and S–N2–P–M1–G3–S should be sung with a flowing, melodic touch. Compared with more heavily gamaka-laden ragas, Bahudari should sound light, graceful, and restrained.
notable_features: Bahudari is a shadava-audava janya raga of Harikambhoji (28th melakarta). It is asymmetric and omits rishabham. The scale is S G3 M1 P D2 N2 S / S N2 P M1 G3 S. The name is sometimes interpreted as meaning "many paths." It is concert-friendly and especially associated with the celebrated composition "Brovabharama." No bhashanga usage, vivadi swaras, or vakra prayogas are mentioned in the source. The Wikipedia page lists it as a well-known janya raga with many compositions.

mood: Bhairavi is a deeply expansive and highly expressive raga with a broad emotional range. It can convey devotion, tenderness, pathos, and serene nobility, often evoking a mature, weighty feeling rather than light ornamentation. The raga is especially effective for long elaboration and can move the listener through introspective calm, devotional surrender, and poignant emotional depth. It is often suited to the later hours of the day, when its rich phrases feel reflective and dignified.
description: Bhairavi is an ancient and very popular janya raga in Carnatic music, said to have been prevalent for about 1500 years. Though structurally sampoorna, it is not a melakarta because it is a bhashanga raga that uses two dhaivathams: chatushruti dhaivatham (D2) in the ascent and shuddha dhaivatham (D1) in the descent and in phrases such as P D N D P. It is derived from the 20th melakarta Natabhairavi. The raga has two commonly cited scalar forms: S R2 G2 M1 P D2 N2 S / S N2 D1 P M1 G2 R2 S, and a vakra variant S G2 R2 G2 M1 P D2 N2 S / S N2 D1 P M1 G2 R2 S. Bhairavi is one of the most important concert ragas, with enormous scope for improvisation in all three sthayis, especially the upper madhya and tara sthayis. It is well suited to thanam, kanakku, sarvalaghu swaras, and ragam-thanam-pallavi. Syama Sastri's Kamakshi Swarajathi is noted for using every note of the raga as a graha swaram. The raga is distinguished from Kharaharapriya by the phrase S G R G and by gamaka treatment on G ending in R, and from Mukhari by the use of P D2 N2 S and S G2 R2 G2 M1. It is also related by similarity of murchana and anya-swara usage to Manji, Mukhari, and Huseni. Hindustani Bhairavi is very different from the Carnatic raga and corresponds more closely to Carnatic Thodi in aroha-avaroha terms.
gamaka_usage: Gamaka treatment is central to Bhairavi’s identity. R2 and M1 can be prolonged without gamaka, but G2 is typically handled with oscillation and slides, often in conjunction with R2. In ascent, G2 is held together with R2 and may be rendered with short or wide oscillation, as in S G R G M; the gamaka on G should resolve into R rather than resting on G. In descent, phrases like M P G R are used, with G2 sliding down from M1 and oscillating. The nishada is an important jeeva swara and may be rendered with varying degrees of gamaka depending on whether D2 or D1 is in use. D2 is characteristic in the ascent phrase P D2 N2 S, while D1 is used in descent and in P D N D P. The deft handling of G2, N2, D2, and D1 gives the raga its distinctive flavour. Compared with Kharaharapriya, Bhairavi’s S G R G phrase and the tendency for gamaka to end on R are essential; unlike some allied ragas, G should not be allowed to settle plainly on itself in the same way.
notable_features: Bhairavi is one of the most popular concert ragas and has near-infinite compositional repertoire. It can be elaborated beautifully in all three sthayis, especially upper madhya and tara sthayis. It is a bhashanga raga with both D2 and D1 in different directions/phrases. The raga lacks vakra sancharas in its common form, making both brighas and slower phrases effective. It is especially suitable for thanam, kanakku, sarvalaghu swaras, and RTP. Syama Sastri’s Kamakshi Swarajathi is highlighted as using every note as a graha swaram. The raga is distinguished from Kharaharapriya and Mukhari by characteristic phrases and gamaka handling. It is one of the oldest ragas, and the text notes that there are hundreds of compositions in it.

mood: Bhimsen has a devotional, serene, and uplifting character. Its pentatonic ascent gives it a direct, open, and uncluttered feel, while the fuller descent adds a sense of completion and repose. The raga can evoke reverence, calm confidence, and a gentle heroic dignity, making it suitable for devotional and contemplative expression rather than intense pathos or agitation.
description: Bhimsen is a rāgam in both Carnatic and Hindustani music, created by composer Mahesh Mahadev and named after Bharat Ratna Pandit Bhimsen Joshi. It is identified as a janya raga of the 11th melakarta, Kokilapriya, in the 72-melakarta system. The source describes it as an asampoorna/asymmetric scale with no rishabham or dhaivatam in the आरोhaṇa, making it audava-sampurna: pentatonic ascending and sampurna-like descending. The scale uses S, G2, M1, P, N3 in ascent and adds R1 and D2 in descent. Mahesh Mahadev introduced the raga to Indian classical music by composing two Hindustani bandishes. The raga is thus a modern creation with cross-genre relevance, and its naming explicitly honors Bhimsen Joshi.
gamaka_usage: The source does not specify detailed gamaka prayoga, but from the scale structure the raga would typically treat S, P, and M1 as relatively stable anchor notes, with G2 and N3 carrying gentle oscillation or slides to shape the phrase. In the descent, R1 and D2 are introduced only in avarohaṇa and are likely handled with careful jaru-like movement rather than heavy emphasis, since the raga is asymmetric and the ascent omits them entirely. The raga’s identity depends on preserving the pentatonic ascent cleanly and avoiding over-ornamentation that would blur the audava character. Compared with closely related pentatonic-sampurna janyas, the descent should sound fuller and more expansive, but still controlled.
notable_features: Janya of melakarta 11 (Kokilapriya). Audava-sampurna structure: ascent omits R and D, descent includes R1 and D2. The source explicitly says Mahesh Mahadev created the raga and introduced it through two Hindustani bandishes. The raga is named after Bhimsen Joshi, with the name explained as combining 'BHIM' and 'SEN' from Bhimsen Joshi and Miaya Tansen. It is described as an asampoorna/asymmetric raga.

mood: Bhupalam has an auspicious, serene, and devotional character. It is commonly associated with the calm freshness of morning and evokes a sense of purity, prayerfulness, and gentle uplift. The raga can feel simple yet dignified, suitable for chanting, ritual contexts, and folk-like expressions, creating an atmosphere of peace, reverence, and quiet optimism.
description: Bhupalam (pronounced bhūpalam) is a pentatonic janya rāgam in Carnatic music, also written as Bhoopalam. It is an audava rāgam with a symmetric scale that omits madhyamam and nishādham in both ascent and descent. The scale is considered auspicious and is traditionally rendered as a morning rāgam. In Tamil music it is called Puranirmai pann, and some thevarams are set to this scale. It is also used for chanting slokas, folk songs, Kathakali music, and other rituals. The raga is considered a janya of Shubhapantuvarali, the 45th melakarta, though the text notes it can also be derived from five other melakartas by dropping both madhyamam and nishādham. Its Hindustani equivalent is Bhupal Todi. Related theoretical notes include graha bhedam yielding Gambhiranata and Hamsanadam, and scale-similarity comparisons with Revagupti, Bhauli, and Karnataka Shuddha Saveri.
gamaka_usage: Bhupalam is generally rendered with a clean, restrained gamaka profile because of its pentatonic structure and devotional, open character. R1 and D1 are typically approached with gentle oscillation or short slides rather than heavy kampita, while G2 is the most characteristic note and is often given a measured kampita or jaru-based touch to establish the raga's identity. P is usually held steady as an anchor. The absence of M and N contributes to the raga's uncluttered, spacious feel, so excessive ornamentation is avoided. Compared with similar ragas, the treatment of G2 is especially important: Bhupalam uses sadharana gandharam, unlike Revagupti's brighter G3, so the gandharam should retain its softer Carnatic shading.
notable_features: Bhupalam is an auspicious audava-audava raga with no madhyamam or nishādham. It is used for slokas, folk songs, Kathakali, and ritual contexts, and is identified in Tamil tradition as Puranirmai pann. Graha bhedam on Bhupalam yields Gambhiranata and Hamsanadam. The raga is said to be derivable from Shubhapantuvarali (45th melakarta), though the text also notes derivation from five other melakartas by omitting M and N. Scale-similarity notes: Revagupti differs only by G3 instead of G2; Bhauli adds N3 in descent; Karnataka Shuddha Saveri replaces G2 with M1. The text also mentions that Samajendra by Swati Tirunal is actually in Bowli, which was historically called Bhupalam in the Travancore region.

mood: Bilahari has a bright, open, and uplifting character, with the pentatonic ascent giving it a clean, expansive lift and the full descent adding richness and completeness. It tends to feel cheerful, auspicious, and devotional rather than heavy or plaintive. The raga can suggest freshness, clarity, and a sense of graceful movement, making it suitable for compositions that project optimism, reverence, and lively elegance.
description: Bilahari is a janya rāgam derived from the 29th melakarta Sankarabharanam. It is an asymmetric audava-sampurna rāgam: the ascent is pentatonic and omits madhyamam and nishādham, while the descent is sampurna in the sense of using the full descending framework of Sankarabharanam. The scale is described as a combination of Mohanam in the ascent and Sankarabharanam in the descent. The rāgam uses shadjam, chathusruthi rishabham, antara gandharam, panchamam, and chathusruthi dhaivatham in the ascent, and kakali nishadham and shuddha madhyamam in the descent. It is also classified as bhashanga because it uses kaishiki nishadham (N2) as an external note in the descending scale. The source notes that the notes are used in every song, emphasizing the standard prayoga of the scale. Bilahari is a well-known concert raga with a large repertoire of compositions across composers and languages. It is closely related by scale similarity to Mohanam, Shankarabharanam, Mohanakalyani, Garudadhvani, and Desakshi. The Wikipedia content does not mention a specific inventor or formalizer.
gamaka_usage: Bilahari is generally rendered with a clean, bright, and relatively direct gamaka style rather than heavy oscillation. In the आरोहण (arohana), S, R2, G3, P, and D2 are usually articulated plainly and clearly, with the pentatonic ascent avoiding madhyamam and nishadam. The characteristic movement is often crisp and well-defined, especially in phrases like R2-G3 and D2-S. In the avarohana, M1 and N3 are introduced smoothly, and the descent may allow gentle kampita on G3 and R2 to preserve the Sankarabharanam flavor. The anya N2, when used, is typically a delicate color note rather than a sustained target, and it should be handled sparingly so the raga does not lose its identity. Compared with more heavily gamaka-driven ragas, Bilahari favors clear phraseology, light jaru between key notes, and graceful landing on S, P, and G3.
notable_features: Bilahari is a janya of Sankarabharanam (29th melakarta) and is described as a combination of Mohanam and Sankarabharanam. It is audava-sampurna and bhashanga due to the external kaishiki nishadham (N2) in descent. The arohana omits madhyamam and nishadam, while the avarohana includes M1 and N3, with N2 as anya swara. The text explicitly says the notes are used in every song. Related scales listed are Mohanam, Shankarabharanam, Mohanakalyani, Garudadhvani, and Desakshi. No specific graha bhedam or prati madhyama equivalent is stated in the provided content.

mood: Bowli is a gentle, serene morning raga with a calm, devotional, and slightly plaintive character. Its sparse audava ascent and fuller descent create a feeling of quiet unfolding, like dawn light gradually spreading across a still landscape. The raga tends to evoke peace, inwardness, reverence, meditation, and spirituality rather than dramatic emotion, making it suitable for devotional and contemplative settings.
description: Bowli is a Carnatic janya raga derived from the 15th melakarta, Mayamalavagowla. It is an asymmetric audava-shadava raga, with five notes in the ascent and six in the descent. The scale uses shuddha rishabham, antara gandharam, panchamam, and shuddha dhaivatam in the arohana, with kakali nishadham added in the avarohana. It is known as a morning raga and is associated with meditation, spirituality, calmness, and a pious, deeply contemplative atmosphere. The raga is also spelled Bauli or Bhauli. Related scale-similarity notes mention Revagupti, which matches Bowli’s ascent but omits nishadam entirely, and Mechabowli, which matches the ascent while adding madhyamam only in the descent. Bowli is used in a number of devotional and classical compositions across composers and languages.
gamaka_usage: Bowli is generally rendered with restrained, delicate gamaka treatment rather than heavy ornamentation. R1 and G3 are often given gentle kampita or subtle oscillation, especially in phrases that establish the raga’s devotional color. P is usually held steady as an anchor note. D1 is approached and released smoothly, often with a light jaru-like connection from P to D1 and back in descent. N3 appears only in the avarohana and is treated carefully as a descending color note, usually with a soft glide into D1 rather than a forceful oscillation. The raga’s compact ascent favors phrase-based singing over brigha-heavy elaboration; characteristic prayogas should preserve its serene, meditative quality. Compared with closely related pentatonic/hexatonic morning ragas, Bowli’s identity depends on the gentle use of N3 in descent and the calm handling of the Mayamalavagowla-derived R1-G3 contour.
notable_features: Bowli is a janya of Mayamalavagowla and is classified as audava-shadava. The source explicitly notes its morning association and its meditative, spiritual character. It is closely related by scale similarity to Revagupti (same ascent, no nishadam) and Mechabowli (same ascent, with madhyamam added only in descent).

mood: Darbar has a dignified, composed, and devotional mood with a regal bearing, yet it also carries a plaintive softness because of its vakra descent and the lingering treatment of gandharam and nishadam. It can feel solemn and prayerful, suitable for medium to fast tempo compositions that still retain gravity. The raga often evokes the atmosphere of a courtly setting, but in Carnatic usage its emotional center is more devotional and reflective than merely majestic.
description: Darbar (pronounced darbār) is a comparatively recent raga in Carnatic music, possibly entering the tradition in the earlier part of the 18th century. It is a vakra ragam derived from the 22nd melakarta Kharaharapriya. The scale is characterized by a straightforward ascent and a zig-zag descent, with nishadam and gandharam treated as long notes in the avarohana. The raga is widely used for compositions in medium to fast tempo and has been employed by many composers, including Tyagaraja, Muthuswami Dikshitar, Swathi Thirunal, Veena Kuppayyar, Patnam Subramania Iyer, Subbarama Dikshitar, Mysore Vasudevachar, Koteeswara Iyer, Subbaraya Sastri, Andal, Ambujam Krishna, Purandara Dasa, Walajapet Venkataramana Bhagavathar, Gowrishankar Sthapthi, KS Krishnamurthi, Thirupathi Narayanaswamy, Ramaswami Sivan, Neelakanta Sivan, Bhadrachala Ramadasu, Krishnaswaamy Ayyaa, Ghanam Krishna Iyer, Pattabhiramayya, Unnayi Warrier, Melattur Veerabadhrayya, and Tarangambadi Panchanada Iyer. The raga resembles Nayaki. It is also used in film music. The source text does not mention a specific inventor or formalizer.
gamaka_usage: Darbar is handled with a strong vakra prayoga in the descent, where G2 is explicitly repeated and elongated: Ṡ N2 D2 P M1 R2 G2 G2 R2 S. Nishadam and gandharam are to be rendered long in the avarohana, suggesting sustained kampita/oscillatory treatment and careful emphasis rather than quick, flat execution. R2 and M1 are important anchor notes and are usually approached smoothly with jaru-like movement in phrases. The ascent is comparatively plain and direct, while the descent carries the raga’s identity through the repeated gandharam and the lingering nishadam. Compared with closely related ragas, Darbar’s descent is more ornate and weighty, and the repeated G2 is a key signature that should not be glossed over.
notable_features: Derived from the 22nd melakarta Kharaharapriya. It is a vakra raga with the characteristic avarohana Ṡ N2 D2 P M1 R2 G2 G2 R2 S. Nishadam and gandharam are specifically noted as long in the descent. Darbar is a comparatively recent raga, likely from the early 18th century. It is suitable for medium to fast tempo compositions. The raga resembles Nayaki. The text also notes that Nayaganai Nindra was originally in Darbar but is now sung in Mohanam. Darbar has been used in film music as well.

mood: Darbari Kanada is a grave, deeply expressive raga with a slow-burning emotional pull. It evokes sadness, longing, and a touch of romance, yet its dignity and breadth also create a majestic, contemplative atmosphere. The listener may feel as if they are in a royal court at night, surrounded by solemnity, emotional depth, and restrained beauty. Its lower-octave emphasis gives it a dark, weighty, introspective character rather than a bright or playful one.
description: Darbari Kanada, also called Raga Darbari, is a janya ragam derived from the 20th melakarta Natabhairavi and belongs to the Kanada family. The text notes that it was believed to have been borrowed into Hindustani classical music by Miyan Tansen and that Mughal emperor Akbar was fond of it, which is reflected in the name Darbari, meaning 'court.' In Carnatic music it is described as an extremely melodious and emotionally powerful ubhaya vakra sampurna rāgam. Its scale uses chatusruti rishabham, sadharana gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatham, and kaisiki nishadham. The raga is associated with sadness, longing, and romance, and is considered capable of producing extreme emotional impact. It is also known as Shuddha Kanada or pure Kanada, and is sometimes written as Durbari or Durbarikanada. The source also notes that in Hindustani music it is a grave raga performed deep into the night, with improvisation centered in the lower octave and with slow, elaborate meend and andolan preferred over light ornamentation.
gamaka_usage: The raga relies heavily on slow, weighty gamakas rather than quick ornamentation. In the Hindustani description, komal Ga and komal Dha are rendered with slow andolan, and the same gravity is carried into the Carnatic treatment through sustained, oscillated handling of G2 and D1. The Carnatic text implies a vakra, meandering treatment of phrases, with the characteristic return to S after G2 in ascent and the P-G2-R2-S descent. The note G2 is especially important and is typically not left plain; it is approached and released with a curved, expressive oscillation. R2 and M1 are the notes most naturally held steady, while the rest of the scale gains its identity through meend-like slides and gentle oscillations. Light murki or khatka-style ornamentation is avoided in favor of broad, grave gamaka.
notable_features: Derived from the 20th melakarta Natabhairavi. Classified as an ubhaya vakra sampurna rāgam. Strongly associated with the Kanada family and sometimes called Shuddha Kanada. In Hindustani music it is linked to the Asavari thaat, has vadi Re and samvadi Pa, and is traditionally rendered in the lower and middle octaves with deep night-time gravitas. The source emphasizes its emotional power and the importance of andolan on Ga and Dha. It is also noted as a difficult raga to master in Hindustani tradition. The text mentions that it is called raaga Kaanada in Yakshagana and that it is often written as Durbari or Durbarikanada.

mood: Devagandhari has a prayerful, supplicatory mood with a heroic undertone. It feels devotional and earnest rather than ornate, evoking a seeker’s longing for the divine, a sense of humble appeal, and a quiet inner strength. The raga suits morning hours and can suggest a calm, dignified atmosphere with a restrained but uplifting emotional arc.
description: Devagandhari is a rare, little-known, ancient janya raga in Carnatic music, derived from Shankarabharanam (melakarta 29). It is an audava-vakra-sampurna raga: the आरोhaṇa uses five notes, while the avarohaṇa is complete and zig-zag in motion. The basic scale given is S R2 M1 P D2 S / S N3 D2 N2 D2 P M1 G3 R2 S. The raga is sometimes bhashanga because it may include the kaishika nishadam (N2) as an anya swara. It is closely related to Arabhi in scale outline, but differs in treatment: Devagandhari is rendered with gamakas and vilambita kala prayogas, and is characterized by elongated G3 (deergha gandharam). Certain prayogas use N2, especially phrases like S N3 D N2 and D P. The text notes that historically the raga had three forms, and the less ornamented type is described here. It is also present in the Sikh tradition and appears in the Guru Granth Sahib, where it is the 21st raga in the sequence, spanning pages 527 to 537. In Sikh usage it is associated with prayerful supplication and a heroic effect, and was used primarily by Guru Arjan; 47 hymns are said to be composed in it, including three by Guru Tegh Bahadar and six by Guru Ram Das.
gamaka_usage: Devagandhari is sung with prominent gamakas and vilambita kala prayogas, so notes are often stretched and ornamented rather than rendered plainly. G3 is specifically described as deergha (elongated), making it a key expressive note. The raga may use the anya N2 in certain prayogas, especially S N3 D N2 and D P, so the nishada treatment is flexible and phrase-dependent. R2 and M1 are the more stable notes in the scale outline, while the overall identity depends on graceful oscillation and slow, weighty phrasing. Compared with Arabhi, the same basic note set is handled with more ornamentation and a more devotional, expansive gait.
notable_features: Not to be confused with Karnataka Devagandhari, which is a janya of Kharaharapriya. In Sikh tradition, Devagandhari is the 21st raga in Guru Granth Sahib and is associated with 10 pages (527–537). It is described there as having a prayerful supplication and heroic effect. The raga is said to have three historical forms; the less ornamented form is the one described in the source. It belongs to the Bilawal thaat in Sikh classification, though some relate it to Bilawal with Asavari ang. The source also gives Sikh-style structural labels: Jaati Audava–sampurna, Timing second pehar, Aroh Sa Re Ma Pa Dha Sa, Avroh Sa Ni Dha Pa, Ma Pa, Dha Ni Dha Pa, Ma Ga Re Sa, Pakar Dha Ni Dha Pa, Ma Ga, Sa Re Ma, Ga Sa Re Ga Sa, Vadi Ma, Samvadi Sa.

mood: Gambhiranata has a serene, expansive, and dignified mood. Its pentatonic symmetry gives it a calm, uplifting, and temple-like character, often evoking devotion, grandeur, and a sense of spaciousness rather than intense pathos or agitation. It suits contemplative listening, processional settings, and moments that feel auspicious, stately, and spiritually elevated.
description: Gambhiranata is a Carnatic janya rāga and also appears in Yakshagana, where it is called Naati. It is an audava (pentatonic) symmetric rāgam with the scale S G3 M1 P N3 S / S N3 P M1 G3 S, omitting both rishabham and dhaivatam. The notes used are shadjam, antara gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, panchamam, and kakali nishadam. It is considered a janya of Chalanata, the 36th melakarta rāgam, though it can also be derived from other melakartas by dropping R and D. The raga is also known as Shuddha Nata. Its pentatonic symmetry and openness make it suitable for elaboration, and the mallari tune played on nadaswaram in temple processions is commonly set in this scale. Related theoretical notes include graha bhedam yielding Bhupalam, and the presence of a prati madhyamam counterpart in Amritavarshini. It is also related by scale similarity to Nata, which shares the ascending structure but has a different descent.
gamaka_usage: The raga is generally rendered with a clean, open pentatonic treatment. Since the scale is symmetric and omits R and D, the main melodic weight falls on G3, M1, P, and N3. G3 is typically given a gentle kampita or light oscillation in phrases, while M1 and P are usually held steady and flat. N3 is often approached with a smooth jaru from P and may receive a mild oscillation in descent. Because the raga is structurally simple, excessive gamaka can blur its identity; phrases should remain crisp and spacious, with clear emphasis on the characteristic S-G3-M1-P-N3-S movement. Compared with more gamaka-heavy ragas, Gambhiranata favors restrained ornamentation and well-shaped phrases over heavy oscillation.
notable_features: Yakshagana name: Naati. Considered a janya of Chalanata (36th melakarta), but derivable from 8 other melakartas by omitting R and D. Graha bhedam on Gambhiranata yields Bhupalam. Amritavarshini is noted as the prati madhyamam equivalent, and Hamsadhvani is mentioned in the related-scale comparison table. The raga is especially suitable for elaboration and is used for temple-procession mallari tunes on nadaswaram. It is also called Shuddha Nata.

mood: Garudadhvani has a bright, uplifting, and cleanly articulated character. Its ascent is complete and expansive, while the descent is concise and pentatonic, giving it a sense of forward motion, clarity, and elegance. The raga can feel devotional and auspicious, yet also light and energetic, especially when rendered in faster tempo where it can suggest a Western-like sheen. It tends to evoke confidence, freshness, and a graceful, prayerful joy rather than deep pathos or intense drama.
description: Garudadhvani or Garudadhwani is a janya rāgam derived from the 29th melakarta, Shankarabharanam. It is an asymmetric scale and a combination of the sampurna scale Shankarabharanam and the pentatonic scale Mohanam. Its structure omits madhyamam and nishādham in the descending scale, making it a sampurna-audava rāgam. The raga is noted for its clean, straightforward melodic movement and for having shades of Western music when played at fast pace. It is related to Mohanam by sharing the same descending scale, and Bilahari is mentioned as a related raga with the ascent and descent interchanged. The raga is also described as acquiring extra sheen when rendered with more plain notes and relatively fewer gamakas. No inventor, chakra, or time-of-day specification is given in the source.
gamaka_usage: Garudadhvani is generally rendered with restrained gamaka treatment. The source specifically notes that it acquires extra sheen when it takes more plain notes and relatively less gamakas, so the raga’s identity is often best preserved through clean, direct swara placement rather than heavy oscillation. Rishabam (R2), gāndhāram (G3), madhyamam (M1), dhaivatam (D2), and nishādham (N3) in the ascent are typically articulated clearly, while the descent is especially uncluttered because it omits M1 and N3. The descent S-D2-P-G3-R2-S should be smooth and concise, with minimal ornamentation. Compared with gamaka-rich ragas, Garudadhvani favors plain notes, light touches, and graceful transitions over kampita-heavy treatment.
notable_features: Janya of Shankarabharanam (29th melakarta). Asymmetric sampurna-audava structure. Descending scale omits M1 and N3, using only S D2 P G3 R2 S. The raga is described as a combination of Shankarabharanam and Mohanam. It acquires extra sheen when rendered with plain notes and relatively less gamaka. Related scale comparison: Mohanam matches Garudadhvani’s descent; Bilahari has the ascent and descent interchanged. There is no Hindustani raga called Garudadhvani.

mood: Gaula has an auspicious, ancient, and dignified mood, often associated with the early part of a concert. It feels solemn yet uplifting, with a devotional and serene quality that can evoke reverence, calmness, and a sense of traditional gravity. The raga’s contour gives it a stately, grounded character rather than overt sweetness, making it suitable for opening a performance with an atmosphere of sanctity and composure.
description: Gaula (or Gowla, pronounced gauḷa) is an ancient Carnatic janya raga derived from the 15th melakarta Mayamalavagowla. It is described as an auspicious raga, usually sung in the early part of a concert, and is also considered a popular ghana raga. Its structure is asymmetric: the ascent is audava (pentatonic) and omits gandharam and dhaivatam, while the descent is vakra-shadava with six notes and zig-zag movement. The scale given is S R1 M1 P N3 S in ascent and S N3 P M1 R1 G3 M1 R1 S in descent. The text notes that it is an ancient raga mentioned in Sangita Ratnakara, Sangita Makarandha, and Sangita Samayasara. It is strongly associated with the Gaula rishabham (ekasruti rishabham) and includes antara gandharam only in the descent. The raga has a substantial repertoire of compositions, especially by Tyagaraja, Muthuswami Dikshitar, Swathi Thirunal, Mysore Vasudevachar, Gopalakrishna Bharathi, Ambi Dikshithar, Thiruvottriyur Thyagaraja, and Oothukkadu Venkata Kavi.
gamaka_usage: Gaula is rendered with characteristic Carnatic gamakas, especially on R1 and G3 in the descent. R1 is a key note and is typically given a firm kampita/oscillatory treatment, reflecting its identity as Gaula rishabham. G3 appears only in the avarohana and is approached in a vakra manner, usually with a gentle slide or oscillation rather than a plain, flat touch. M1 and P are comparatively steadier anchor notes, while N3 in the ascent is also held clearly as part of the pentatonic climb. The raga’s zig-zag descent requires careful jaru-like movement through S–N3–P–M1–R1–G3–M1–R1–S, and the repeated return to M1 and R1 is a defining prayoga. Dhaivatam is avoided entirely, and gandharam is not used in ascent. Compared with closely related Mayamalavagowla-type phrases, Gaula’s treatment is more weighty and gamaka-rich, with emphasis on its ghana character rather than scalar plainness.
notable_features: Ancient raga mentioned in Sangita Ratnakara, Sangita Makarandha, and Sangita Samayasara. It is a janya of Mayamalavagowla (melakarta 15). Classified as audava-vakra-shadava. Considered auspicious and a ghana raga, commonly placed in the early part of concerts. The scale omits dhaivatam entirely and omits gandharam in ascent, but uses antara gandharam in descent. The text explicitly lists it as a popular raga with many compositions. No graha bhedam or prati madhyama equivalent is mentioned in the provided content.

mood: Hamsadhvani has a bright, uplifting, and auspicious character. It feels open, clean, and celebratory, often evoking a sense of freshness, invocation, and devotional joy. Because it is commonly sung at the beginning of performances and is strongly associated with Lord Ganesha, it creates an atmosphere of welcome, confidence, and spiritual optimism rather than deep pathos or intensity. Its pentatonic clarity gives it a light, sparkling quality that can suggest the graceful cry of a swan, as its name implies.
description: Hamsadhvani means "the cry of the swan." It is a Carnatic audava (pentatonic) janya raga of Shankarabharanam (29th melakarta), though by prayoga it is sometimes treated as a janya of Kalyani (65th). The raga omits madhyamam and dhaivatham entirely, using only S, R2, G3, P, and N3. It is widely used in both Carnatic and Hindustani music, having been borrowed into Hindustani tradition from Carnatic music. The raga is credited to Ramaswami Dikshitar (1735–1817), father of Muthuswami Dikshitar, and was later popularized in Hindustani music by Aman Ali Khan of the Bhendibazaar gharana and further through Amir Khan. It is especially favored for invocatory pieces and many compositions in praise of Lord Ganesha. In Hindustani music it is associated with Bilaval thaat, and its vadi-samvadi are Sa and Pa. The raga is also noted for graha bhedam relationships: shifting the tonic yields Nagasvaravali. It has scale similarities with Amritavarshini, Gambhiranata, Mohanam, and Niroshta, each differing by one note substitution. In performance practice it is a highly accessible, elegant, and concert-opening raga with broad use in classical and film music.
gamaka_usage: Hamsadhvani is generally rendered with a clean, crisp treatment rather than heavy oscillation. Since the scale is pentatonic and omits madhyamam and dhaivatham, the raga depends more on phrase shape and note emphasis than on dense gamaka. R2 and G3 are typically given gentle kampita or light oscillation in phrases, while P and N3 are often held more steadily to preserve the raga's bright, open character. Jaru-like slides between R2–G3 and G3–P are important, and the descent N3–P–G3–R2–S should sound smooth and flowing. Compared with more gamaka-heavy ragas, Hamsadhvani should remain relatively plain and buoyant; over-oscillation can blur its identity. The characteristic phraseology and the clear landing on P and N3 are more important than elaborate ornamentation.
notable_features: Audava-audava pentatonic raga with no madhyamam or dhaivatham. It is commonly used at the beginning of performances. In Hindustani music it is linked to Bilaval thaat and has vadi Sa, samvadi Pa. Graha bhedam on Hamsadhvani yields Nagasvaravali. Scale-similarity relations are noted with Amritavarshini (prati madhyamam in place of R), Gambhiranata (shuddha madhyamam in place of R), Mohanam (D2 in place of N3), and Niroshta (D2 in place of P).

mood: Hamsanadam has a bright, serene, and uplifting mood. Its pentatonic symmetry gives it a clean, open, and airy feel, often evoking calm joy, devotional uplift, and a sense of graceful wonder. The raga can feel soothing and expansive rather than heavy or sorrowful, making it suitable for contemplative as well as celebratory expression.
description: Hamsanadam is a Carnatic rāgam, pronounced hamsanādam, and is currently sung as a symmetric pentatonic audava scale. It is a janya rāgam derived from the 60th melakarta, Neetimati. In its modern form it omits gandharam and dhaivatam, using only S, R2, M2, P, and N3 in both ascent and descent. An earlier form of the scale included D3 in both directions, making it a shadava structure with vakra descent: arohana S R2 M2 P D3 N3 S and avarohana S N3 D3 N3 P M2 R2 S. The raga is notable for graha bhedam relationships: shifting the tonic yields Gambhiranata, Neela, and Bhupalam. It has several popular compositions and is a well-known concert raga despite its relatively compact scale.
gamaka_usage: The source does not specify detailed gamaka rules, but in practice the raga’s identity rests on clean handling of the five core swaras. R2 and N3 are typically given light oscillation or gentle kampita, while M2 and P are often held more steadily to preserve the raga’s bright, transparent character. In the older form, D3 appears as a passing/zig-zag note in vakra descent and would be treated with a slide-like jaru between P and N3 rather than as a heavily oscillated resting note. Because the scale is compact and symmetric, excessive gamaka can blur its identity; phrases should remain crisp and well-shaped.
notable_features: Derived from the 60th melakarta Neetimati. Modern usage is audava (5-note) and symmetric; earlier usage included D3, making it shadava with vakra descent. Graha bhedam on Hamsanadam yields three pentatonic ragas: Gambhiranata, Neela, and Bhupalam. The raga has a small but popular concert repertoire.

mood: Hamsanandi has a serene, luminous, and expansive mood. Its omission of panchamam gives it a floating, slightly ethereal quality, often felt as calm devotion with a gentle sense of wonder. The raga suits reflective listening and evening contemplation, evoking a peaceful, graceful atmosphere rather than intense pathos or drama. It can feel soothing and uplifting at once, with enough openness for alapana and swara elaboration.
description: Hamsanandi is a hexatonic (shadava) janya rāgam in Carnatic music, derived from the 53rd melakarta, Gamanashrama. It is symmetric and omits panchamam entirely, sharing with Shree ranjani the feature of having only the invariant panchamam missing from the parent scale. Its structure is shadava-shadava, with identical six-note ascent and descent: S R1 G3 M2 D2 N3 >S and >S N3 D2 M2 G3 R1 S. The scale uses shadjam, shuddha rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, chathusruthi dhaivatham, and kakali nishadam. It is an evening rāgam and is noted for ample scope for alapana and swara prasthara. The text also notes that it has been used by many composers and in film music. In Hindustani classical music, Sohni raga of the Marwa thaat resembles Hamsanandi. Through graha bhedam, Hamsanandi yields Anandanatani by shifting the tonic to niṣāda, and this relationship is used in a Carnatic tillana by Bhagavatīśvara.
gamaka_usage: Hamsanandi is generally rendered with smooth, lyrical gamakas rather than heavy oscillation. R1 is often approached cleanly and may receive a light kampita or gentle slide into G3. G3 and N3 are important color notes and are typically given nuanced kampita or delicate jaru-inflected transitions to preserve the raga’s brightness and floating quality. M2 and D2 are usually held with clarity and stability, acting as bright pillars in the scale. The absence of panchamam creates characteristic flowing movements between M2–D2 and D2–N3, and phrases should avoid any accidental P. Compared with closely related panchama-less ragas, Hamsanandi tends to sound more expansive and luminous when the upper notes are allowed to glide smoothly rather than being over-ornamented.
notable_features: A janya rāgam of Gamanashrama (53rd melakarta). It is a symmetric shadava-shadava scale with no panchamam. The text explicitly compares it to Shree ranjani in that only the invariant panchamam is missing from the parent scale. It is suitable for alapana and swara prasthara. Through graha bhedam, it yields Anandanatani by shifting the tonic to niṣāda. Sunadavinodini is noted as a similar-sounding rāgam, but it lacks shuddha rishabham. Hamsanandi is also said to resemble Hindustani Sohni (Marwa thaat).

mood: Hindolam has a beautiful, soothing, and deeply calming mood. Its pentatonic symmetry gives it a poised, balanced character that feels meditative and inward-looking, often evoking serenity, devotion, and quiet joy. Listeners may experience a sense of stillness, gentle uplift, and contemplative sweetness, making it especially effective for reflective or devotional settings and for improvisation in concerts.
description: Hindolam is a symmetric audava rāgam in Carnatic music, using only five notes in both ascent and descent and omitting rishabham and panchamam. Its scale is S G2 M1 D1 N2 S / S N2 D1 M1 G2 S. The raga uses sadharana gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatam, and kaisiki nishadam. It is not a melakarta because it lacks all seven swaras. The commonly accepted parent is Natabhairavi (20th melakarta), though some associate it with Hanumatodi (8th melakarta), since it can be derived from either by dropping R and P. Hindolam is widely regarded as beautiful and soothing, and its symmetry makes it highly suitable for improvisation, which is why it is popular in concerts. The text also notes that shades of Hindolam and Mohanam can sometimes be traced in Chinese and East Asian music because of the pentatonic structure. Its Hindustani equivalent is Malkauns (or Malkosh), and it is explicitly not the same as Hindustani Hindol.
gamaka_usage: Hindolam is generally rendered with restrained, graceful gamakas rather than heavy ornamentation. G2, D1, and N2 are the main notes that take gentle oscillation or soft kampita, often approached and left with smooth jaru-like slides to preserve the raga’s meditative color. M1 is comparatively steadier and acts as an anchor in phrases. Because R and P are absent, prayogas should avoid any accidental suggestion of those swaras; the raga’s identity depends on clean, flowing movement between G2–M1–D1–N2. The gamaka treatment is usually rounded and elegant, with emphasis on smooth transitions and subtle oscillation rather than forceful brigas. Compared with more heavily gamaka-driven ragas, Hindolam sounds cleaner, calmer, and more serene.
notable_features: Audava-audava pentatonic raga; symmetric ascent and descent; commonly accepted as a janya of Natabhairavi (20th melakarta), though some attribute it to Hanumatodi (8th melakarta). It can be derived from either by omitting R and P. It is popular for improvisation because of its symmetry. Graha bhedam on Hindolam yields four other major pentatonic ragas: Mohanam, Shuddha Saveri, Udayaravichandrika (Shuddha Dhanyasi), and Madhyamavati. The text also notes scale-shade similarities with Chinese and East Asian music. Hindolam is not the same as Hindustani Hindol; its Hindustani equivalent is Malkauns/Malkosh.

mood: Jana Ranjani is a bright, pleasing, and audience-friendly raga whose very name suggests delight and enjoyment among listeners. Its zig-zag ascent and compact five-note descent create a lively yet controlled emotional space: cheerful, engaging, and mildly uplifting rather than deeply plaintive. It can evoke a sense of graceful movement, festive warmth, and easy accessibility, making it feel welcoming to both performer and audience.
description: Jana Ranjani is a janya raga of Shankarabharanam, the 29th melakarta. The name is explained as meaning a raga that induces enjoyment among rasikas: 'Jana' meaning people and 'Ranjani' meaning that which pleases or delights. It is classified as a vakra sampoorna-audava ragam: the ascent uses all notes but in a zig-zag manner, while the descent is pentatonic. Its scale is S R2 G3 M1 P D2 P N3 S in ascent and S D2 P M1 R2 S in descent. The raga is noted as being very similar to Poornachandrika, and the two are considered twin ragas, difficult to distinguish unless rendered with precise sangatis and pidis. It is a well-known concert and teaching raga because its structure is compact, attractive, and easy for listeners to enjoy, while still demanding careful phrase control from the performer.
gamaka_usage: The source does not give explicit gamaka rules, but the raga’s identity depends strongly on characteristic phrases rather than plain scalar movement. R2, G3, M1, D2, and N3 are typically handled with gentle oscillation and phrase-based slides, especially in the zig-zag ascent S R2 G3 M1 P D2 P N3 S. The repeated P in the ascent suggests a jaru-like turn around panchamam, and the descent is usually rendered with clean, compact phrases emphasizing D2–P–M1–R2–S. Compared with Poornachandrika, Janaranjani relies heavily on sangatis and pidis, so gamaka and phrase choice are crucial for differentiation. The notes are not treated as rigidly plain; instead, the raga comes alive through delicate kampita on R2 and G3, smooth glides into M1 and D2, and restrained, graceful oscillations that preserve its bright but composed character.
notable_features: Jana Ranjani is a janya of Shankarabharanam (melakarta 29). It is vakra in the ascent and audava in the descent. The raga is described as highly pleasing to listeners, and its name reflects that quality. It is closely related to Poornachandrika and is considered one of a pair of twin ragas with it; accurate sangatis and pidis are needed to distinguish them. The provided text does not mention graha bhedam, but the scale and structure are clearly defined.

mood: Kalyana Vasantham has a bright, serene, and gently uplifting character. Its pentatonic ascent gives it a clean, open, and graceful feel, while the fuller descent adds a sense of completion and repose. The raga often evokes calm devotion, tender optimism, and a soft festive glow rather than intense pathos or drama. Because it combines the flavor of Chandrakauns with the structure of Keeravani, it can feel both meditative and emotionally rounded, suitable for reflective, devotional, and aesthetically pleasing moods.
description: Kalyana Vasantham (also written as kalyanavasantham) is a janya rāgam derived from the 21st melakarta, Keeravani. It is an asymmetric audava-sampurna rāgam: the आरोhaṇa is pentatonic and omits rishabham and panchamam, while the avarohaṇa is sampurna in effect with panchamam and chatushruti rishabham appearing in descent. The scale is described as a combination of the Hindustani pentatonic Chandrakauns and the sampurna Keeravani scale. Its structure is S G2 M1 D1 N3 S / S N3 D1 P M1 G2 R2 S. The raga is noted for its scale similarity to Chandrakauns in ascent. The provided text lists several compositions in this raga, including tanavarnams and kritis by major composers. No explicit time-of-day, inventor, or chakra is given in the source.
gamaka_usage: The source does not give detailed gamaka rules, but the scale implies a smooth, phrase-based treatment. G2, M1, D1, and N3 are the core melodic notes in ascent and are typically rendered with gentle oscillation and slides rather than hard attacks, while R2 and P appear in descent and should be handled carefully to preserve the raga’s Keeravani-derived color. Because the ascent omits R and P, phrases should avoid forcing those notes upward; instead, use flowing jaru-like movement through G2–M1–D1–N3. The descent can accommodate fuller kampita on G2 and R2, with P acting as a stabilizing bridge. Compared with similar pentatonic ragas, the raga’s identity depends on preserving the bright, clean ascent and the more complete descent.

notable_compositions.name: Unnai Paadaadha
notable_compositions.composer: HH Maharanyam Sri Sri Muralidhara Swamiji

notable_features: Janya of Keeravani (21st melakarta). Audava-sampurna structure: ascent omits R and P, descent includes P and R2. The raga is said to combine the pentatonic Chandrakauns with Keeravani. Scale similarity noted with Chandrakauns: S G2 M1 D1 N3 S / S N3 D1 M1 G2 S. The source lists it as having several tanavarnams and kritis, but no additional theoretical details such as graha bhedam or prati madhyama equivalent are provided. Also referred to as Varamu in some sources.

mood: Kalyanavasantam has a serene, soft, and devotional character. The pentatonic ascent gives it a light, uncluttered lift, while the fuller descent adds a gentle sense of completion and repose. It can evoke calm joy, quiet devotion, and a slightly wistful sweetness, making it suitable for reflective or prayerful moods rather than intense drama. The raga feels graceful and soothing, with an airy brightness tempered by inwardness. In some renderings it can also carry a deep, nostalgic, and absorbing melancholy, lending the music a tender, heart-touching quality.
description: Kalyanavasantam is a janya rāgam derived from the 21st melakarta, Keeravani. It is an asymmetric audava-sampurna scale: the ascent is pentatonic and omits rishabham and panchamam, while the descent is sampurna in effect with panchamam and chatushruti rishabham included. The raga combines the pentatonic Hindustani Chandrakauns with the full Keeravani scale. Its structure is ārohaṇa S G2 M1 D1 N3 S and avarohaṇa S N3 D1 P M1 G2 R2 S. The notes used are shadjam, sadharana gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, shuddha dhaivatam and kakali nishadham in ascent, with panchamam and chatushruti rishabham appearing in descent. The raga is also written as kalyanavasantham. The source text does not mention a specific inventor, time theory, or special historical origin beyond its derivation and scale relationship. It is a raga that has been popularized in modern concert practice and is often appreciated for its poignant, introspective beauty.
gamaka_usage: The source text does not specify gamaka treatment, but from the scale structure the raga is likely rendered with gentle, flowing gamakas rather than heavy oscillation. G2 and N3 are typically the most expressive notes and may be given light kampita or graceful slides, while M1 and P in the descent are usually held cleanly as anchor notes. R2 appears only in descent and is generally approached by a smooth jaru from G2. D1 is a key note in both directions and is often treated with subtle oscillation or touch-based emphasis. Since the ascent omits R and P, phrases should avoid forcing those notes upward; the raga’s identity depends on the pentatonic ascent and the fuller, more complete descent. In practice, the raga often sounds most effective when the phrases are sung with delicate meends and restrained kampita, preserving its plaintive, serene character rather than using heavy gamaka density.

notable_compositions.name: Unnai Paadaadha
notable_compositions.composer: HH Maharanyam Sri Sri Muralidhara Swamiji

notable_features: Kalyanavasantam is an audava-sampurna janya of Keeravani (melakarta 21). It is structurally related to Chandrakauns in the ascent, sharing the same notes, while the descent restores panchamam and rishabham from Keeravani. The raga is asymmetric and specifically noted to omit rishabham and panchamam in the आरोहण. The source explicitly identifies the scale similarity with Chandrakauns. No graha bhedam, vivadi, or pedagogical details are stated in the provided text.

mood: Kamas is generally felt as a gentle, graceful, and pleasing raga with a soft lyrical charm. It evokes tenderness, devotion, and aesthetic beauty, often creating an intimate and refined atmosphere rather than dramatic intensity. The raga can suggest quiet romance, serene contemplation, and a devotional sweetness that makes it suitable for expressive, ornamented melodic lines.
description: Kamas is a Carnatic janya raga associated with grace, sweetness, and lyrical appeal. It is commonly treated as a derivative of Harikambhoji (melakarta 28), and its phraseology gives it a distinctive, attractive character. The raga is well known for its elegant melodic movement and for supporting compositions that combine devotion with beauty of expression. The name is also rendered as Kāmas, and the Wikipedia disambiguation page points to the raga entry as Kamas (raga). The available source material here does not provide historical origin details, but the raga is clearly recognized in the Carnatic tradition and is used in compositions such as svarajatis and varnams.
gamaka_usage: Kamas is shaped more by phrase and ornament than by a plain scalar outline. The raga typically uses gentle kampita and delicate oscillation on G3 and N2, with smooth jaru-like movement into and out of M1 and P. R2 is usually handled lightly as a connecting note rather than a heavily stressed resting tone. D2 and N2 are often approached with graceful slides in descent, and the characteristic vakra movement around M1-G3-M1 helps define the raga identity. Compared with more straightforward Harikambhoji-derived ragas, Kamas depends on nuanced prayogas and soft gamaka treatment to preserve its sweet, romantic, and devotional flavor.
notable_features: Kamas is a janya raga of Harikambhoji (melakarta 28). Its scale is vakra in practice, with a characteristic phrase-based identity rather than a strictly linear scale. It is associated with graceful, devotional, and romantic expression, and is used in forms such as svarajatis and varnams. The available snippets specifically mention a swarajathi, 'Sambhasivaayana', and a varnam, 'Samiyai Alaittuvadi', in raga Kamas.

mood: Kambhoji is a grand, expansive, and deeply expressive raga with a regal yet intimate emotional range. It can project devotion and serenity, but it is equally capable of tenderness, pathos, and graceful romantic feeling. The raga often evokes a dignified, mature atmosphere—majestic in concert settings, yet warm and human in its emotional pull. Because of its bhashanga usage and broad melodic canvas, it can move from stately repose to affectionate lyricism, making it suitable for both devotional and lyrical compositions.
description: Kambhoji or Kambodhi is a highly popular and ancient derived raga in Carnatic music, classified under the 28th melakarta Harikambhoji. It is said to have existed since the 7th century, and some traditions also associate its scale with Cambodia. It is a shadava raga in the ascent and sampurna in the descent. A key feature is the use of N3 in the phrase N3 P D2 S, which makes it a bhashanga raga. Historically, Kambhoji is referenced in ancient and medieval Indian musical literature under related names and classifications, including as Thakkesi in Cilappatikaram and in treatises such as Brihaddesi, Sangita Makaranda, and Chaturdandi Prakashika. It has long been treated as one of the primary and most important ragas in Carnatic music, with a very large repertoire of compositions across composers and genres, including geetam, kriti, varnam, padam, javali, and even film music. The raga is also associated with older classificatory schemes where it appears as a female raga/ragini, and that traditional association is often linked to its suitability for shringara, hasya, and karuna.
gamaka_usage: Kambhoji is traditionally rendered with rich, weighty gamakas and broad oscillations. R2 and G3 are typically handled with kampita and graceful slides, giving the raga its characteristic majesty. M1 is usually sustained or gently touched rather than heavily oscillated. D2 and N2 in the descent are often approached with jaru-like phrases, and the bhashanga N3 in the phrase N3 P D2 S is treated as a delicate, phrase-specific inflection rather than a stable scale note. The raga depends strongly on prayoga and phrase identity rather than plain scalar rendering; flat, dry intonation would weaken its identity. Compared with closely related Harikambhoji, Kambhoji is more ornate, more expansive, and more phrase-driven, with stronger emphasis on oscillatory movement and long, flowing melodic curves.
notable_features: Derived from the 28th melakarta Harikambhoji. It is a bhashanga raga because of the use of N3 in the phrase N3 P D2 S. It is classified as shadava in the ascent and sampurna in the descent. Historically, it appears in ancient and medieval sources under related names and classifications, including Thakkesi in Cilappatikaram and references in Brihaddesi, Sangita Makaranda, Chaturdandi Prakashika, and other treatises. The raga is one of the primary ragas in Carnatic music and has a very large composition corpus. The text also notes a possible derivation of the scale from Cambodia. It is associated with female-raga/ragini classification in older theory, linking it to shringara, hasya, and karuna. Harikambhoji is its parent melakarta; Kambhoji is one of its most famous janyas.

mood: Kapi carries a deeply devotional and emotionally tender atmosphere. It is often felt as a raga of prayer, yearning, and gentle pathos, with an undercurrent of warmth and happiness. The listener may experience a soft, meandering emotional landscape that moves between surrender, sadness, and serene devotion, making it suitable for reflective and intimate settings.
description: Kapi is a popular rāga in Carnatic music and is a janya rāgam of Kharaharapriya. It is characterized by a meandering vakra scale and is typically performed at slow and medium speeds. The raga is described as capable of inducing devotion, pathos, sadness, sentiment, and happiness. Its structure is audava-vakra sampoorna: the आरोhaṇa is pentatonic, while the avarohaṇa uses seven notes in a non-linear, vakra manner. The raga is bhashanga because of the use of Kakali Nishadam (N3) and Anthara Gandharam (G3), and the text also notes a mild presence of shuddha daivatham (D1) in some usages, especially in devotional contexts such as 'Jagadhodharana'. The traditional Kāpi differs from the Hindustani raag and thaat Kafi; the Hindustani equivalent is Pilu. The text also notes that Muthuswami Dikshitar's Kāpi is quite different from the traditional Kāpi and is often cited as karNATaka Kāpi. Venkatamakhin's moorchana for Kāpi is given as S R2 G2 M1 P D2 N2 S / N2 D2 P M1 G2 G2 R2 S.
gamaka_usage: Kapi is rendered with gentle, expressive gamakas rather than rigid scalar delivery. The nishadas are especially important: N2 and N3 are both used, with the vakra phrase N2-D2-N2 being a distinctive prayoga. N2 is typically oscillated and approached with a curved, meandering motion, while D2 is used as part of the vakra descent rather than as a starkly held note. R2 and G2 are also treated with light kampita and smooth jaru-like transitions, supporting the raga’s flowing character. M1 and P are comparatively steadier anchor notes. The text indicates a mild presence of D1 in devotional renderings, and G3 may appear as an anya swara, so these foreign notes are handled delicately and sparingly. Compared with closely related ragas, Kapi’s identity depends less on a plain scale and more on its characteristic oscillations, vakra descent, and the expressive treatment of nishada.

notable_compositions.name: Kanna Vaa Manivanna Vaa (First two verses)
Nee mattume en nenjil nirkirai
notable_compositions.composer: Perumal Murugan and K Arun Prakash

notable_features: Kapi is an audava-vakra sampoorna janya of Kharaharapriya. It is bhashanga due to the use of N3 and G3, and the text also mentions a mild foreign D1 in some contexts. The raga is associated with devotion and pathos, and is often sung slowly or at medium tempo. The traditional Kāpi differs from Hindustani Kafi; its Hindustani equivalent is Pilu. The text explicitly notes that Muthuswami Dikshitar’s Kāpi is quite different from the traditional Kāpi and is often called karNATaka Kāpi. Venkatamakhin’s moorchana is given as S R2 G2 M1 P D2 N2 S / N2 D2 P M1 G2 G2 R2 S. The source also states that in the song 'Jagadhodharana' by Purandara Dasa, D1 occurs as an anya swara.

mood: This is a serene, compact pentatonic raga with a clean and uncluttered emotional profile. The absence of gandharam and nishadam gives it a bright, open, and almost austere simplicity, creating a feeling of calm devotion, innocence, and gentle uplift. It can sound meditative and composed rather than deeply plaintive, making it suitable for reflective or devotional settings and for creative elaboration without emotional heaviness.
description: Karnataka Shuddha Saveri is an audava rāgam (pentatonic scale) and a janya rāgam. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school it is known as Shuddha Sāveri, and it is explicitly distinguished from the more widely known Shuddha Saveri of the Tyagaraja school, which is called Devakriya in the Dikshitar tradition. The scale is symmetric and omits gandharam and nishādam. Its structure is ārohaṇa S R1 M1 P D1 >S and avarohaṇa >S D1 P M1 R1 S. It is considered a janya of Kanakangi, the 1st melakarta, though the text notes that it can also be derived from eight other melakarta rāgams by dropping both gandharam and nishādam. The rāgam lends itself to creative elaboration and exploration because of its use of shuddha notes. A graha bhedam on this scale yields Amritavarshini. It is also related by scale similarity to Saveri, whose ascending scale matches this rāgam while its descent is sampurna. The text also notes that Malahari resembles Saveri, and that the Dikshitar-school Shuddha Saveri has R1 and D1 in place of the more common R2 and D2.
gamaka_usage: The source text does not give explicit gamaka rules for this rāgam, but its shuddha-note pentatonic structure implies a relatively plain, stable treatment compared with heavily gamaka-laden ragas. R1, M1, and D1 are the defining tones and are typically rendered with gentle oscillation or light janta/jaru inflection as needed for phrase shaping, while S and P are usually held steady. Because gandharam and nishadam are absent, phrases tend to move cleanly between R1–M1–P–D1 without ornamental detours. The raga’s character comes more from the purity and spacing of the notes than from intense kampita, so excessive oscillation would blur its identity.
notable_features: This rāgam is a janya of Kanakangi (melakarta 1). It is a symmetric audava-audava scale with no gandharam or nishadam. The text states that it can also be derived from eight other melakarta rāgams by omitting G and N. Graha bhedam on Karnataka Shuddha Saveri yields Amritavarshini. It is called Shuddha Sāveri in the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, and is distinct from the more common Shuddha Saveri/Devakriya of the Tyagaraja school. The source emphasizes that it is suitable for creative elaboration because of its shuddha notes.

mood: Kathanakuthuhalam has a bright, lively, and engaging character, with a sense of curiosity and uplift that makes it feel animated and story-like. The scale’s unusual ordering gives it a playful, sparkling energy that can suggest wonder, delight, and dramatic movement rather than deep pathos. It suits compositions that aim to captivate the listener, and its concert use often brings a fresh, attractive, and spirited atmosphere.
description: Kathanakuthuhalam, also spelled kathanakuthoohalam or kathanakuthuhala, is a Carnatic raga credited to Patnam Subramania Iyer, who is said to have invented it. The raga is known from the famous kriti Raghuvamsha Sudhambudhi by Patnam Subramania Iyer. Its structure is asymmetric and vakra, with the arohana S R2 M1 D2 N3 G3 P S and avarohana S N3 D2 P M1 G3 R2 S. It is commonly treated as a janya of Melakarta 29, Shankarabharanam. The scale uses chatushruti rishabham, shuddha madhyamam, chatushruti dhaivatam, kakali nishadam, antara gandharam, and panchamam. The raga has attracted a notable body of compositions, including kritis, varnams, thillanas, and a jathiswaram. It is a modern raga in the sense of being formally introduced by a named composer rather than being an ancient traditional scale.
gamaka_usage: The raga’s vakra structure suggests that gamakas and phrase-based treatment are essential to its identity rather than plain scalar rendering. R2 and M1 are generally handled with relative steadiness compared with the more expressive notes G3, D2, and N3, which are shaped with slides and oscillatory movement to bring out the raga’s lively contour. The zig-zag ascent S R2 M1 D2 N3 G3 P requires careful janta/jaru phrasing so that the unusual turn through N3–G3–P is clearly articulated. In descent, the line S N3 D2 P M1 G3 R2 S should be rendered with smooth glides, especially around G3 to R2, to preserve the raga’s characteristic color. Compared with more straightforward sampurna ragas, the identity here depends strongly on phrase order and ornamented transitions.
notable_features: The raga is credited to Patnam Subramania Iyer as its inventor. It is explicitly given with the scale S R2 M1 D2 N3 G3 P S / S N3 D2 P M1 G3 R2 S. The source lists a substantial set of compositions in this raga, including a famous kriti, several varnams, thillanas, and a jathiswaram. It is commonly described as a janya of Melakarta 29 (Shankarabharanam). The raga’s unusual vakra ascent makes it pedagogically useful for phrase recognition and for demonstrating how a raga identity can be defined by movement rather than by a simple linear scale.

mood: Kedāragowla carries a calm, devotional, and auspicious morning feeling. Its pentatonic ascent gives a clean, open, and prayerful lift, while the fuller descent adds grace, warmth, and emotional depth. The raga tends to evoke stillness, tenderness, and dignified devotion, with a gentle freshness rather than intense drama or pathos.
description: Kedāragowla is a janya rāgam derived from the 28th melakarta Harikambhoji. It is sometimes spelled Kedaragaula or Kedaragowla. The raga is an asymmetric audava-sampurna structure: the ascent omits gandharam and dhaivatam, while the descent is complete with G3 and D2. Its scale is often described as combining the pentatonic feel of Madhyamavati in the ascent with the fuller Harikambhoji descent. The rāgam is traditionally associated with the morning and is valued for its serene, auspicious, and devotional character. It has a substantial repertoire across major composers and is used in kritis, varnams, javalis, padams, tillanas, and darus. The source also notes related scale-feel with Madhyamavati and Yadhukula kambhoji.
gamaka_usage: Kedāragowla is shaped more by characteristic phrases than by heavy oscillation. The ascent is relatively plain and stable, with S, R2, M1, P, and N2 often rendered cleanly to preserve the raga's open, pentatonic lift. In the descent, G3 is an important color note and is usually approached gently from M1, often with a soft slide rather than a hard attack. N2 and D2 may carry mild kampita or delicate oscillation, but the gamaka weight is generally restrained compared with more heavily ornamented ragas. R2 and M1 function as anchor swaras and are typically held with clarity. Smooth jaru-like movement in the avarohana helps define the raga's grace, and the descent carries much of its expressive identity.

notable_compositions.name: Tillana
notable_compositions.composer: Natyacharya Guru Late Sri S Aravindan

notable_features: Kedāragowla is a janya of Harikambhoji (28). It is an audava-sampurna scale: ascent uses S R2 M1 P N2 S, while descent uses S N2 D2 P M1 G3 R2 S. The source explicitly notes that it combines the pentatonic scale Madhyamavati with the sampurna Harikambhoji scale. It is a morning rāgam. Scale similarity is noted with Madhyamavati (same ascent) and Yadhukula kambhoji (related scale-feel).

mood: Kedaram has a serene, uplifting, and devotional mood. Its bright yet gentle contour gives a feeling of calm grace, auspiciousness, and quiet joy rather than intense pathos or drama. The raga often evokes temple imagery, reverence, and a composed, dignified beauty, making it suitable for devotional and reflective settings.
description: Kedaram is a derived (janya) rāgam from Shankarabharanam, the 29th melakarta. It is an asymmetric scale classified as a vakra audava-shadava rāgam: the ascent has five notes with zig-zag movement, while the descent has six notes. Its scale omits dhaivatam entirely. The arohana is S M1 G3 M1 P N3 S and the avarohana is S N3 P M1 G3 R2 S. The raga uses shadjam, chatusruti rishabham, antara gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, panchamam, and kakali nishadam. The source notes that Kedaram is similar to Natbehag of Hindustani classical music, while the Hindustani Kedar is different and belongs to Kalyan thaat. Kedaram has been used by many composers and is a well-established concert and devotional raga.
gamaka_usage: Kedaram is shaped more by characteristic prayogas than by heavy scalar rendering. The vakra ascent S–M1–G3–M1–P–N3–S makes the M1–G3–M1 turn especially important; M1 is often treated as a central, gently oscillated note, with a smooth return from G3 back to M1. G3 is usually approached with a soft slide or kampita-like touch rather than a stark, plain hold. R2 in the descent is typically introduced smoothly from G3, and the descent S–N3–P–M1–G3–R2–S favors rounded, connected phrases. P and N3 function as relatively stable anchor notes, while D is completely absent and should not be implied in gamaka treatment. The raga’s identity depends on its phrase contours and the delicate handling of M1 and G3, rather than on broad oscillation across all swaras.

notable_compositions.name: Rare Ramanulamta
notable_compositions.composer: Sri Bhakta Gnanananda Teertha (Sri Ogirala Veera Raghava Sarma)

notable_features: Kedaram is a vakra audava-shadava janya of Shankarabharanam (29th melakarta). It is asymmetric and omits dhaivatam completely. The scale is S M1 G3 M1 P N3 S / S N3 P M1 G3 R2 S. The raga is related to Natbehag in Hindustani music, but not to Hindustani Kedar. The source does not mention graha bhedam, prati madhyama equivalent, or specific pedagogical role.

mood: Madhyamavati has a calm, auspicious, and uplifting character. It is strongly associated with devotional closure, so it often leaves the listener with a sense of peace, completion, and blessing. The raga feels spacious and balanced because of its even note spacing and pentatonic simplicity, making it suitable for elaboration without tension. It evokes images of serenity, prayer, and a dignified festive calm, and is especially apt for the concluding moments of a concert or for music meant to feel propitious and reassuring.
description: Madhyamavati is a janya rāga of the 22nd melakarta Kharaharapriya, though the text notes that it can also be derived from Charukesi, Natabhairavi, or Harikambhoji by omitting both gandharam and dhaivatam. It is an audava (pentatonic) rāga with a symmetric ascent and descent, using only S, R2, M1, P, and N2.
