The scale is described as especially auspicious, and in Carnatic concert practice it is commonly used to conclude performances, either as the final song or as the rāga in which the ending of the last song is rendered. The raga is also noted for being suitable for elaboration and exploration because of its even spacing of notes. Its notes correspond to the first three notes of the cycle of fifths (S, P, R2) and fourths (S, M1, N2).
gamaka_usage: The source does not give detailed gamaka prescriptions, but the raga’s pentatonic symmetry and even spacing suggest smooth, restrained ornamentation rather than heavy oscillation. R2 is typically treated as a gently ornamented note, M1 and P are usually held steady as structural pillars, and N2 may receive light kampita or a soft slide into upper S. Because the raga lacks gandharam and dhaivatam, phrases should remain clean and direct, with jaru-like transitions between the available notes rather than dense gamaka clusters. Compared with closely related ragas such as Manirangu or Kedaragaula, Madhyamavati is more straightforward and less colored by extra descending notes.

notable_compositions.name: Vidachi sukhimpave
notable_compositions.composer: Saint Gnanananda Teertha (Ogirala Veera Raghava Sarma)

notable_compositions.name: Devi parvati
notable_compositions.composer: Saint Gnanananda Teertha (Ogirala Veera Raghava Sarma)

notable_features: Madhyamavati is a janya of Kharaharapriya (22nd melakarta), and the text notes that it can also be derived from Charukesi, Natabhairavi, or Harikambhoji by dropping gandharam and dhaivatam. It is a symmetric pentatonic rāga with no G or D. It is considered highly auspicious and is traditionally used to end Carnatic concerts. The raga is said to be very suitable for elaboration because of its even spacing. Graha bhedam on Madhyamavati yields four major pentatonic rāgas: Mohanam, Hindolam, Shuddha Saveri, and Udayaravichandrika (Shuddha Dhanyasi). Related scale-similarity notes mention Kedaragaula, Manirangu, Revati, Brindavana Sāranga, Andolika, and Nagagandhari.

mood: Mahathi has a bright, compact, and uplifting character, with a clean and symmetrical four-note contour that feels direct and uncluttered. Its omission of rishabham, madhyamam, and dhaivatam gives it a crisp, open sound, often creating a sense of freshness, poise, and lightness. The raga can evoke devotional serenity as well as a striking, novel musical curiosity because of its unusual tetratonic structure.
description: Mahathi (also written Mahati) is a Carnatic raga created by M. Balamuralikrishna. It is a symmetric tetratonic scale (chaturswara rāgam) with no rishabham, madhyamam, or dhaivatam in either ascent or descent. The scale is given as S G3 P N2 S and S N2 P G3 S. The source text classifies it as a janya of the 34th melakarta Vagadhishvari; it also notes alternative classifications as a janya of 14th Vakulabharanam or 28th Harikambhoji in some systems. The raga has no equivalent in Hindustani music and no direct Western equivalent is given. Two compositions are mentioned in this raga: 'Mahaneeya Madhura' by M. Balamuralikrishna, and 'Adhisaya Ragam' from the film Apoorva Ragangal, composed by M. S. Viswanathan and sung by K. J. Yesudas. The raga is notable for being a rare, self-contained four-note creation and for its pedagogical and compositional interest as a modern raga innovation.
gamaka_usage: Because Mahathi is a compact tetratonic raga, its gamaka treatment is generally restrained and highly focused. G3 is the principal melodic anchor and is typically handled with gentle oscillation or nuanced kampita/janta-style emphasis rather than heavy ornamentation. N2 may also receive light oscillation or a smooth slide into and out of P, while S and P are usually kept comparatively steady as structural pillars. The raga’s identity depends on clear, clean phrase shapes such as S-G3-P-N2-S and the reverse, so excessive gamaka on the omitted swaras is not relevant; the absence of R, M, and D makes the phrases sound stark and distinctive. Jaru-like glides between G3-P and P-N2 help preserve its flowing character, but the overall treatment should remain simple and uncluttered.
notable_features: A modern raga created by M. Balamuralikrishna; tetratonic/chaturswara structure; symmetric arohana-avarohaṇa; classified in the source as a janya of melakarta 34 Vagadhishvari, with alternate source classification as janya of 14 Vakulabharanam or 28 Harikambhoji; no Hindustani or Western equivalent stated; useful as a rare example of a four-note Carnatic raga.

mood: Mahati has a serene, compact, and strikingly modern melodic personality. With only four notes, it feels clear, open, and uncluttered, creating a calm and contemplative atmosphere rather than emotional complexity. The absence of rishabham, madhyamam, and dhaivatam gives it a bright, floating quality, while the use of antara gandharam and kaishiki nishadam lends a gentle sweetness and a devotional, reflective shade. It can evoke wonder at its unusual structure, and its symmetry makes it feel balanced and self-contained.
description: Mahati, also written as Mahathi, is a tetratonic rāga in Carnatic music created by M. Balamuralikrishna. It is classified as a janya of the 14th melakarta Vakulabharanam, the 28th melakarta Harikāmbhōji, or the 34th melakarta Vagadhishvari, depending on the theoretical parent framework cited. The rāga is symmetric and omits rishabham, madhyamam, and dhaivatam in both ascent and descent. Its scale is S G3 P N2 S / S N2 P G3 S, using shadjam, antara gandharam, panchamam, and kaishiki nishadham only. It is described as a chaturswara rāgam. The source states that there is no equivalent raga in Hindustani music and no equivalent scale in Western music. The rāga is notable as a modern creation by Balamuralikrishna and as an example of a highly concise, mathematically neat Carnatic scale.
gamaka_usage: Because Mahati is a very compact tetratonic scale, gamaka treatment is necessarily restrained and selective. S is the anchor note and is typically held steady. G3 and N2 may take light oscillation or gentle kampita to give the rāga its expressive identity, but the phrases should remain clean and uncluttered. P is generally rendered plainly as a stable resting note. The characteristic movement is the direct, symmetric glide between G3–P–N2 and back, with smooth jaru-like transitions rather than heavy ornamentation. Excessive gamaka would blur the rāga’s sparse identity, so the notes are best approached with clarity and precision.
notable_features: Mahati is a four-note (chaturswara/tetratonic) rāga created by M. Balamuralikrishna. It is symmetric and omits R, M, and D entirely. The source gives three possible parent melakarta associations: 14th Vakulabharanam, 28th Harikāmbhōji, and 34th Vagadhishvari. There is no Hindustani equivalent and no Western equivalent stated. The only compositions mentioned are Mahaneeya Madhura and the film song Adhisaya Ragam. The rāga is also written as Mahathi.

mood: Malahari has a gentle, morning-like serenity that feels calm, fresh, and lightly devotional. Its character suggests the quiet of early daybreak and the soothing atmosphere of the rainy season, making it evoke peace, innocence, and a soft sense of reverence. Because it is often used for beginners’ geethas, it also carries a simple, clear, and pedagogically friendly mood that feels approachable and unforced.
description: Malahari is a Carnatic janya raga traditionally associated with the 15th melakarta Mayamalavagowla, though the text notes that because it omits nishadha it can also be derived theoretically from Gayakapriya (13th) or Vakulabharanam (14th). It is an asymmetric audava-shadava raga, with five notes in ascent and six in descent. The arohana is S R1 M1 P D1 S and the avarohana is S D1 P M1 G3 R1 S. It is described as a morning raga that brings calmness and is associated with the rainy season. In Carnatic pedagogy it is especially important as a beginner raga for geethas, typically taught after swara exercises in Mayamalavagowla. Many geethas in this raga were composed by Purandara Dasa. The scale is closely related to Karnataka Shuddha Saveri in ascent, differing by the presence of gandhara in descent. The raga is a common teaching raga and a classic example of an asymmetric janya with a simple, devotional, and soothing profile.
gamaka_usage: The source does not give explicit gamaka rules, but the scale suggests a relatively plain, beginner-friendly treatment. R1, M1, and D1 are the core structural notes and are typically rendered with light, controlled gamaka rather than heavy oscillation. G3 appears only in avarohana and functions as the characteristic descent note, usually approached by a smooth jaru from M1 into G3 and then to R1. Since the raga is used for elementary geethas, phrases are generally kept clear and stable, with less ornate kampita than in heavier ragas. The absence of nishadha and the simple audava-shadava structure favor clean phrase-based singing over elaborate oscillatory treatment.
notable_features: Audava-shadava asymmetric scale; traditionally a janya of Mayamalavagowla (15), though theoretically derivable from Gayakapriya (13) or Vakulabharanam (14) because it omits nishadha. Used extensively for beginner geethas in Carnatic training. Associated with morning and rainy-season mood. Scale similarity: Karnataka Shuddha Saveri matches the ascending scale of Malahari, but without the descending G3. Many geethas in this raga are by Purandara Dasa.

mood: Malayamarutam has a pleasing, gentle, and refreshing morning character. It tends to evoke calmness, light devotion, and an easy, uplifting serenity rather than deep pathos or intense drama. The raga suits the early hours of the day, creating an atmosphere of freshness, clarity, and quiet optimism, and it is often associated with morning programmes and functions.
description: Malayamarutam is a janya rāgam in Carnatic music and is classified as a symmetric hexatonic shadava-shadava scale. It omits madhyamam entirely, giving it a bright, open sound. The scale is considered a janya of Chakravakam, the 16th melakarta, though it can also be derived from Ramapriya by dropping madhyamam. The name is commonly understood as meaning "fragrant hill-breeze," reflecting the raga’s light, refreshing character. It is a morning raga and is regularly used in morning programmes and functions. The raga is described as pleasing, but it has only a limited number of classical compositions. It has also been used in film music and, in recent years, has been used in Hindustani music by instrumentalists.
gamaka_usage: Malayamarutam is generally rendered with restrained ornamentation rather than heavy gamaka density. R1 and G3 are the most characteristic notes and are often approached with gentle kampita or subtle slides to preserve the raga’s soft, buoyant feel. P is usually held steady as an anchor point, while D2 and N2 may be touched with light oscillation or smooth jaru-based movement, especially in descent. Because madhyamam is absent, phrases should avoid any M-like inflection and remain clean and open. The identity of the raga depends more on clear scalar movement and graceful phrase endings than on elaborate gamaka patterns. Compared with more ornate ragas, Malayamarutam is best sung or played with a light, flowing treatment and minimal force.
notable_features: Malayamarutam is a symmetric scale without madhyamam, using S R1 G3 P D2 N2 S / S N2 D2 P G3 R1 S. It is a shadava-shadava rāgam and is considered a janya of Chakravakam (16th melakarta), though it can also be derived from Ramapriya by omitting madhyamam. It is noted as a morning raga and is regularly used in morning programmes. The raga has only a few classical compositions but has also found use in film music and, in recent years, in Hindustani instrumental performance. Related-scale comparison in the source notes that Valaji differs by omitting shuddha rishabham, and Rasikaranjani differs by omitting nishadam.

mood: Mohanakalyani has a bright, graceful, and uplifting mood that blends the sweetness of Mohanam with the expansive, luminous color of Kalyani. The ascent feels open and buoyant, creating a sense of freshness, optimism, and gentle joy, while the descent adds a more expansive, devotional radiance. It can evoke serene happiness, light romantic charm, and a contemplative, prayerful atmosphere, making it suitable for pieces that feel elegant, soothing, and spiritually elevated.
description: Mohanakalyani is a janya rāgam derived from the 65th melakarta, Mechakalyani. It is an asymmetric audava-sampurna scale: the ascent is pentatonic and omits madhyamam and nishādham, while the descent is sampurna with prati madhyamam and kakali nishādham. The rāgam is described as a combination of the pentatonic Mohanam and the melakarta scale Kalyani. It is also considered the prati madhyamam equivalent of Bilahari, which is a janya of the 29th melakarta Dheerashankarabharanam. The raga is believed to have been invented by Swati Tirunal Maharaja. In Hindustani music, its equivalent is Bhoop Kalyan or Shuddha Kalyan, associated with the Kalyan thaat and similarly combining Bhoop and Kalyan elements. The scale used is S R2 G3 P D2 S / S N3 D2 P M2 G3 R2 S. The raga is popular for compositions and has a clear linkage to Mohanam in the ascent and Kalyani in the descent.
gamaka_usage: The source text does not explicitly describe gamakas, but the raga’s structure implies a clean, graceful treatment of the ascent with S, R2, G3, P, and D2 often rendered in a relatively direct, flowing manner, since the arohana is pentatonic and omits madhyamam and nishadam. In the descent, M2 and N3 should be handled with more expressive oscillation and glide, especially around G3–M2 and N3–D2, to bring out the Kalyani flavor. R2 and G3 are likely to be gently gamaka-laden rather than starkly flat, while P is typically stable. The raga’s identity depends on preserving the Mohanam-like ascent and the Kalyani-like descent, so over-emphasizing madhyamam in ascent or flattening the descent too much would weaken its character.
notable_features: Mohanakalyani is a combination of Mohanam and Kalyani. It is an audava-sampurna janya of Mechakalyani (65th melakarta). It is the prati madhyamam equivalent of Bilahari, whose parent is the 29th melakarta Dheerashankarabharanam. The raga’s ascent matches Mohanam exactly, while the descent introduces M2 and N3, giving it a Kalyani-like brightness. The text explicitly notes the scale similarity with Mohanam and Bilahari. Believed to have been invented by Swati Tirunal Maharaja.

mood: Mohanam has a bright, open, and uplifting mood, with a serene yet expansive character. Its pentatonic symmetry gives it a clean, uncluttered feel that often evokes joy, devotion, and a sense of calm beauty. It can suggest pastoral simplicity, festive cheer, or a contemplative stillness, and is equally at home in devotional pieces, beginner teaching material, and elaborated concert improvisation. The raga often feels accessible and universally pleasing, with a timeless, almost global melodic appeal.
description: Mohanam is a highly popular audava (pentatonic) rāga in Carnatic music, usually treated as a janya rāga of Harikambhoji (28th melakarta), though alternate opinions in the source suggest Mechakalyani or even Shankarabharanam may be a more appropriate classification based on lakshana. It is symmetric and omits madhyamam and nishādham entirely. The scale is S R2 G3 P D2 S / S D2 P G3 R2 S. The raga is one of the most common pentatonic scales worldwide and is especially prominent in East and Southeast Asian music, including China and Japan. The source notes that an ancient Tamil pentatonic scale, Mullaippann (3 BCE), used sa ri ga pa da and is considered equivalent to Mohanam in Carnatic style. Mohanam lends itself to extensive elaboration and exploration in both classical and film music. It is also closely related by scale to several ragas: Mohanakalyani (ascending Mohanam, descending Kalyani), Bilahari (ascending Mohanam, descending Shankarabharanam), Garudadhvani (ascending Shankarabharanam, descending Mohanam), Shivaranjani (differs only by gandharam), Hamsadhvani (uses nishadam in place of dhaivatam), and Niroshta (uses nishadam in place of panchamam).
gamaka_usage: Mohanam is generally rendered with smooth, flowing phrases rather than heavy oscillation. R2 and D2 are typically handled with light kampita or gentle jaru depending on phrase context, while G3 is a key note that is often given a graceful, rounded treatment and may be approached or left by slide. P is usually held steady as an anchor. Because the raga is pentatonic and symmetric, clean phrase-shapes such as S R2 G3 P D2 S and the descent S D2 P G3 R2 S are important, and the notes should sound open and unforced. Compared with ragas like Shivaranjani, the antara gandharam (G3) in Mohanam is brighter and more expansive; compared with Bhairavi or other heavily gamaka-driven ragas, Mohanam is less dependent on dense oscillation and more on elegant linear movement and phrase identity.
notable_features: Mohanam is an audava-audava raga with exactly five notes in ascent and descent. It is one of the first scales taught to beginners; the geetham Varaveena Mridupaani is specifically noted as an early teaching piece. The raga is widely used in classical and film music and is considered highly adaptable for elaboration. Its notes when shifted by graha bhedam yield four other major pentatonic ragas: Hindolam, Shuddha Saveri, Udayaravichandrika (Shuddha Dhanyasi), and Madhyamavati. The source also highlights its global prevalence and its connection to ancient Tamil Mullaippann. It is closely related by scale to Mohanakalyani, Bilahari, Garudadhvani, Shivaranjani, Hamsadhvani, and Niroshta.

mood: Mukhari has a grave, reflective, and deeply emotive character. It is often heard as sorrowful and introspective, yet it also carries a strong devotional serenity. The raga can evoke a sense of quiet lament, tender prayer, and inward contemplation, making it suitable for moments of emotional depth, surrender, and stillness. Its beauty lies in how sadness is softened by devotion and calm, so the listener may feel both pathos and peace at once.
description: Mukhari is a well-known and colourful janya rāga of Kharaharapriya (22nd melakarta). It is described as a strong raga with great emotive appeal, often spoken of as sorrowful and introspective, though it also effectively exudes bhakti and shanta. It is allied to ragas such as Huseni, Bhairavi, Salagabhairavi, Kokilavarali and Maanji. The raga is especially effective in very slow tempo, where each note and gamaka can be clearly enunciated, though crisper renditions are also appreciated. Its scale is asymmetric in practice: arohana S R2 M1 P N2 D2 S and avarohana S N2 D1 P M1 G2 R2 S. The notes include Sadja, Chatusruti Rishabha, Shuddha Madhyama, Panchama, Chatusruti Dhaivata in ascent, Shuddha Dhaivata in descent, and Kaisiki Nishada, with Sadharana Gandhara appearing only in avarohana. Important phrases include SRM G, RS with emphasis on Madhyama, and PDSR with emphasis on Rishabha. It is a special raga with rich melodic possibilities and requires precise swara placement to avoid drifting into neighbouring ragas. The raga is also associated with a number of well-known compositions across composers and languages, and is especially valued for its expressive depth in devotional and pathos-laden contexts.
gamaka_usage: Mukhari demands very precise swara positions and gamakas. The notes must be enunciated clearly, especially in slow tempo, and the raga gains much of its identity from careful phrase-by-phrase treatment rather than from a merely scalar rendering. Rishabha and madhyama are important anchor notes, with the phrase SRM G highlighting madhyama and PDSR highlighting rishabha. Gandhara appears only in the descent and must be handled carefully so the raga does not drift into neighbouring ragas. By implication, R2 and M1 are the most stable notes, while G2, N2, and the dhaivata notes require controlled oscillation and careful phrase-based treatment. D2 is used in ascent as an anya-swara-like ascent note, while D1 is used in descent; this contrast must be articulated cleanly. Compared with allied ragas, the gamaka and phrase treatment is what preserves Mukhari’s identity.
notable_features: Janya rāga of Kharaharapriya (22nd melakarta). The text explicitly calls it a strong raga with sorrowful, introspective, devotional, and shanta qualities. Allied ragas include Huseni, Bhairavi, Salagabhairavi, Kokilavarali and Maanji. The source highlights the need for precise swara positions to avoid drifting into neighbouring ragas. Important phraseology includes SRM G and PDSR. The raga is said to have many melodic possibilities. The source also notes a special wedding-context piece, Anandam Anandam Anandame, played by the orchestra immediately after the mangalsutra is tied, evoking mixed happiness and sorrow. Jalamiga Valadhura is described as a very rare, short swarajati in Telugu by an unknown composer.

mood: Nada Kalyani has a bright, uplifting, and expansive mood. Its pentatonic ascent gives it a clean, open, and graceful feel, while the fuller descent adds warmth and completeness. Because it is derived from Kalyani, it carries an auspicious, devotional, and serene character, but with a slightly modern and novel sheen due to its asymmetric structure. The raga can evoke calm devotion, gentle joy, and a sense of wonder, making it suitable for reflective and spiritually elevated listening.
description: Nada Kalyani is a Carnatic janya raga derived from the 65th melakarta, Mechakalyani (Kalyani). It was created by composer Mahesh Mahadev, who is noted in the source as having created many ragas. The raga is asymmetric and audava-sampurna: the आरोहणम् omits gandharam and dhaivatam, while the avarohanam is sampurna in the sense of using the full descending contour with G3 and D2. The scale is S R2 M2 P N3 S in ascent and S N3 D2 P M2 G3 R2 S in descent. It is described as equivalent to the Western Lydian mode, with the third and sixth notes omitted in the ascending scale. In Hindustani music it is called Nada Kalyan. The only composition explicitly mentioned in this raga is 'Achyutam Keshavam' by Mahesh Mahadev, sung by Priyadarshini.
gamaka_usage: The source does not specify gamakas, but the raga’s structure suggests a relatively smooth treatment of the ascent, with S, R2, M2, P, and N3 rendered cleanly and prominently. The omitted G3 and D2 in आरोहणम् should not be stressed in ascent, while the descent should bring them in with gentle slides and light oscillation, especially around M2–G3–R2 and N3–D2–P. As a Kalyani-derived raga, M2 and N3 can be given a bright, sustained emphasis, with graceful jaru phrases linking the notes rather than heavy kampita on the pentatonic ascent.
notable_features: Janya of Mechakalyani (65th melakarta). Audava-sampurna structure. Asymmetric scale with omitted G3 and D2 in ascent. Equivalent to the Western Lydian mode, but with the third and sixth omitted in the ascending phrase. Named Nada Kalyan in Hindustani music. Created by Mahesh Mahadev.

mood: Nāgasvarāvaḻi has a bright, open, and symmetrical pentatonic feel that sounds clean and uncluttered. With both rishabham and nishadam absent, the raga projects spaciousness, purity, and a restrained auspiciousness rather than intense emotion. It often evokes calm confidence, devotional uplift, and a gentle festive brightness, making it suitable for contemplative and prayerful expression.
description: Nāgasvarāvaḻi is an audava (pentatonic) janya rāgam in Carnatic music. It is symmetric and omits both rishabham and nishādam. Its scale is S G3 M1 P D2 S in ascent and S D2 P M1 G3 S in descent. The notes used are shadjam, antara gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, panchamam, and chathusruti dhaivatam. It is considered a janya of Harikambhoji, the 28th melakarta, though it can also be derived by dropping R and N from Chakravakam, Sooryakantam, Shankarabharanam, Vagadheeswari, or Shoolini. The raga is regarded as a rare janya in concert practice, though it has a substantial body of compositions associated with it. It is also noted for its theoretical relationship through graha bhedam to Hamsadhwani. In scale structure it is close to Mohanam and Shuddha Saveri, but differs by the presence of M1 and G3 respectively.
gamaka_usage: Detailed gamaka treatment is generally restrained in Nāgasvarāvaḻi. The raga relies more on plain, well-spaced phrases than on heavy oscillation, because its identity comes from the clean pentatonic framework S-G3-M1-P-D2-S. S, P, and D2 are usually rendered as stable anchor notes. G3 and M1 may take light kampita or gentle slides, especially in phrases such as G3-M1-P and D2-P-M1-G3, but the oscillation is typically subtle rather than forceful. Jaru-like glides between adjacent notes help preserve the raga’s bright, flowing character. Excessive gamaka is usually avoided so that the absence of R and N remains clear and the raga does not lose its open, symmetrical feel.
notable_features: Janya of Harikambhoji (28th melakarta). Can also be derived from Chakravakam, Sooryakantam, Shankarabharanam, Vagadheeswari, or Shoolini by dropping both R and N. Graha bhedam on Nāgasvarāvaḻi yields Hamsadhwani. Scale-similarity notes: Mohanam replaces M1 with R2; Shuddha Saveri replaces G3 with R2. The raga is an audava-audava pentatonic scale. It is described as a rare janya in concert use, though it has many compositions in the repertoire.

mood: Nattai is an auspicious, bright, and forceful raga that creates a sense of ceremonial uplift and forward motion. It is often felt as majestic and alert rather than soft or languid, making it especially effective at the start of a concert. The mood can suggest devotion, confidence, and a clean, radiant energy, with an undercurrent of solemnity and dignity.
description: Nattai, also known as Nata or Nāṭa, is a janya rāgam derived from the 36th melakarta, Chalanata. It is considered an auspicious raga and is commonly sung in the early part of a concert, where its bright and commanding character helps establish a strong opening atmosphere. The scale is asymmetric, with a vakra descent that gives it a distinctive melodic identity. In Carnatic practice, Nattai is valued for its grandeur and its ability to project a festive, devotional, and stately mood. It is also associated with the 6th chakra, Ritu, in the chakra classification mentioned in the snippets.
gamaka_usage: Nattai is rendered with a strong, majestic gamaka profile. The raga typically emphasizes kampita and oscillatory treatment on R and G, with M also taking a prominent, weighty role in phrases. D and N are usually approached with clear, firm intonation rather than excessive ornamentation, helping preserve the raga's bright and authoritative character. The descent is vakra, and the characteristic prayoga S N3 P M1 G3 M1 R3 S is important; the repeated M1 and the glide back through G3 and R3 are key to its identity. Compared with smoother ragas, Nattai tolerates a more forceful, open style, but the gamakas should remain crisp and regal rather than languid. The upper notes are often delivered with a ringing, declarative quality, while the lower phrases are anchored by strong kampita on R3 and G3.
notable_features: Nattai is a popular concert-opening raga and is regarded as auspicious. It is a janya of Chalanata (melakarta 36) and has the scale S R3 G3 M1 P D3 N3 S / S N3 P M1 G3 M1 R3 S. The raga is vakra in descent. The snippets also place it in the 6th chakra, Ritu. It is often discussed alongside Chala Nattai and Gambheera Nattai in comparative pedagogy.

mood: Nāttai Kurinji is a gentle, graceful, and auspicious raga with a soft devotional and compassionate glow. It evokes calm beauty, tenderness, and a composed intimacy, often feeling lyrical rather than dramatic. The raga can suggest quiet affection, reflective serenity, and a mild romantic sweetness, while also carrying a soothing, prayerful undertone that makes it suitable for reflective and devotional expression.
description: Nāttai Kurinji is a janya raga derived from the 28th melakarta, Harikambhoji. It is commonly treated as an audava-sampurna raga, with a pentatonic ascent and a fuller descent. The raga is considered gentle and expressive, and sources describe it as evoking devotion and compassion. It is also regarded as auspicious to sing in the evenings, though some traditions note that it can be rendered at other times as well. Nāttai Kurinji is popular in Carnatic music and is known as a raktiraga, with scope for elaboration in both slow and medium tempos. It is especially well suited to tanam and to raga alapana. The raga is distinct from Kurinji despite the similarity in name, and its identity is shaped more by kalpana-based usage than by a large body of fixed compositions.
gamaka_usage: Nāttai Kurinji is typically rendered with restrained, graceful gamaka treatment rather than heavy oscillation. The raga’s identity depends on smooth, rounded phrasing and gentle ornamentation. R2 and G3 are often touched with light kampita or subtle oscillatory movement, while M1 and P are usually held more steadily to anchor the phrase. D2 and N2 in the descent are commonly approached with soft jaru-like slides, helping the avarohana sound fluid and lyrical. The descent should be clearly articulated, since the raga’s character is strongly shaped by its full downward movement. Overly forceful kampita can make it sound closer to brighter or more forceful ragas; a controlled, elegant treatment preserves its devotional and compassionate mood.
notable_features: This raga is a janya of Harikambhoji (melakarta 28). It is commonly described as an audava janya raga and is considered auspicious for evening singing. Rajan Parrikar notes that Natakurinji is a raktiraga, can be sung at all times, and is especially well suited for tanam and elaboration in slow and medium tempos. The raga is also described as having its scope defined largely by kalpana rather than a large fixed-kalpita repertoire. The provided web snippets indicate that it is popular in Carnatic music and relatively rare in Hindustani music.

mood: Navarasa kannada has a bright, graceful, and compact melodic personality. Its ascent is terse and uplifting, moving quickly from shadjam to antara gandharam, madhyamam, panchamam, and upper shadjam, while the descent adds warmth and fullness through nishadam, dhaivatam, and rishabham. The result is a raga that feels elegant, poised, and pleasantly devotional rather than deeply plaintive or dramatic. It suits refined, flowing expression, and its character is especially effective in instrumental settings where its clean contours can evoke serenity, composure, and a gentle celebratory mood.
description: Navarasa kannada or Navarasakannada is a janya rāgam derived from the 28th melakarta, Harikambhoji. It is an asymmetric scale with a svarantara-shadava structure: four notes in ascent and six in descent. The arohana omits rishabham, gandharam, dhaivatam, and nishadham except for the direct leap to upper shadjam, while the avarohana omits panchamam. The scale is S G3 M1 P Ṡ / Ṡ N2 D2 M1 G3 R2 S. The raga is described as especially well suited for instrumental music, particularly veena and flute, and also for orchestral music. The source text does not mention a formal inventor or historical origin beyond its derivation from Harikambhoji. It is a compact, asymmetric janya with a clear melodic identity and is associated with several compositions, especially by Tyagaraja and other composers.
gamaka_usage: The source text does not specify gamaka treatment, but from the scale structure the raga is likely rendered with relatively clean, phrase-based movement rather than heavy oscillation. G3 is an important anchor note and would typically receive gentle kampita or stress in characteristic phrases, while M1 and P are generally held steady as structural tones. R2, D2, and N2 appear only in descent and are usually approached by smooth jaru-like slides within descending phrases. Because the ascent is sparse and direct, the raga’s identity depends more on phrase contour than on elaborate gamaka. Compared with more gamaka-heavy janyas, Navarasa kannada tends to sound crisp and instrumental when the notes are articulated clearly.
notable_features: This raga is an asymmetric janya of Harikambhoji (28th melakarta). It is classified as svarantara-shadava, with 4 notes in ascent and 6 in descent. The source explicitly says it is well suited for instrumental music, especially veena and flute, and also for orchestral music. The arohana is S G3 M1 P Ṡ and the avarohana is Ṡ N2 D2 M1 G3 R2 S. No panchamam appears in descent. The text notes that several compositions in this raga became popular due to Tyagaraja. The raga page does not provide graha bhedam, prati madhyama equivalent, or other theoretical relationships.

mood: Niroshta has a pleasing, gentle, and aesthetically satisfying mood. Because it omits madhyamam and panchamam entirely, the raga feels airy, open, and uncluttered, with a clean pentatonic brightness that sounds serene and uplifting. The sound is calm and attractive rather than intense, and it can evoke simplicity, purity, quiet delight, and a contemplative devotional atmosphere.
description: Niroshta is a derived Carnatic rāgam, classified as a symmetric pentatonic audava-audava scale. Its defining feature is the complete omission of madhyamam and panchamam, which is reflected in its name: 'without the lips,' referring to the inability to pronounce Ma and Pa without the lips touching. It is considered a janya rāgam of Shankarabharanam (29th melakarta), though it can also be derived from Kalyani by dropping both madhyamam and panchamam. The scale uses shadjam, chathusruthi rishabham, antara gandharam, chathusruthi dhaivatham, and kakali nishadham. The raga is described as very pleasing. Muthiah Bhagavatar is credited with creating this scale, inspired by the story of the King of Mysore who had a bee sting on his lip and could not sing the Ma and Pa syllables. The raga is related by scale similarity to Adbhut Kalyan in Hindustani music, and also shares note-sets conceptually with Mohanam and Hamsadhvani, though with different substitutions of panchamam, dhaivatam, and nishadam.
gamaka_usage: The source text does not specify detailed gamaka rules, but given the pentatonic, non-vivadi structure, the raga is likely rendered with smooth, light ornamentation rather than heavy oscillation. R2 and G3 can be approached with gentle slides or small kampita, while D2 and N3 may be touched with subtle oscillation or jaru in phrases. S is stable and anchoring. Since Ma and Pa are absent, they are not used at all, and the raga’s identity depends on clean phrase movement between R2–G3 and D2–N3 without introducing extra notes. The overall treatment should preserve its open, simple, and pleasing character.
notable_features: A symmetric audava-audava scale with no madhyamam or panchamam. The name 'Niroshta' explains the absence of Ma and Pa. It is credited to Muthiah Bhagavatar. It is a janya of Shankarabharanam (29th melakarta), and can also be derived from Kalyani by omitting M and P. Scale similarity is noted with Adbhut Kalyan, Mohanam, and Hamsadhvani. The text explicitly says Shankarabharanam is the major scale of Western music.

mood: Poornachandrika has a bright, serene, and gently uplifting mood. It carries a calm devotional glow rather than intense pathos, giving the listener a feeling of clarity, composure, and quiet joy. The raga can suggest a soft morning freshness or a composed, prayerful atmosphere, with enough melodic lift to feel graceful and optimistic without becoming heavy or dramatic.
description: Poornachandrika is a janya raga of Shankarabharanam, the 29th melakarta. Its scale is asymmetric and vakra, with the characteristic arohana S R2 G3 M1 P D2 P S and avarohana S N3 P M1 R2 G3 M1 R2 S. The raga is closely related in scale-feel to Madhyamavati in ascent, but differs by using G3 and D2, and by its distinctive descent with N3 and the vakra return through M1 and R2. It is described as a bright, fast-radiating raga and is often taught in intermediate stages because its zig-zag structure tests precision in phrase handling. The raga has a number of well-known compositions by major composers, indicating its established place in the Carnatic repertoire. It is not a melakarta, but a derived scale with a clear parent in Shankarabharanam.
gamaka_usage: Poornachandrika is shaped more by phraseology than by heavy oscillation. R2 and G3 are typically rendered with gentle kampita and smooth connection, while M1 and P act as relatively steady anchor notes. The characteristic D2 P in ascent should be given a clean jaru-like lift into the upper register, and the descent N3 P M1 R2 G3 M1 R2 S must be articulated with careful glide and phrase shaping rather than plain scalar motion. The repeated M1 R2 movement in the avarohana is a defining prayoga and is usually sung with light oscillation and a flowing return, not as a rigid step. Compared with more heavily gamaka-laden ragas, Poornachandrika prefers graceful, bright, and precise ornamentation, with the vakra phrases carrying much of its identity.

notable_compositions.name: Sri Ranganatham Upasmahe
notable_compositions.composer: Muthuswami Dikshitar or Ambi Dikshitar or one of Muthuswami Dikshitar's disciples

notable_features: Janya of Shankarabharanam (29th melakarta). The scale is vakra and asymmetric. Arohana uses the phrase D2 P before upper shadjam, and avarohana uses N3 P M1 R2 G3 M1 R2 S. The raga is related by scale similarity to Madhyamavati in ascent, but differs by the use of G3 and the specific descent. It is often taught in intermediate stages because its zig-zag structure demands precision. The provided text does not mention graha bhedam, prati madhyama equivalent, or a specific time theory. It is associated with several popular compositions by Tyagaraja, Muthuswami Dikshitar, Patnam Subramania Iyer, Swati Tirunal, and G.N. Balasubramaniam.

mood: Punnagavarali has a plaintive, ancient, and slightly eerie devotional mood. It is strongly associated with karuna rasa, and its nishadantya shape gives it a closed, circular feeling that can sound hypnotic and serpent-like. The raga evokes images of snake charmers, ritual settings, and wedding processions, and when rendered slowly it can feel regal, solemn, and deeply reflective. It suits moments of prayer, ceremonial dignity, and quiet emotional intensity rather than bright exuberance.
description: Punnagavarali is a derived raga from Hanumatodi, the 8th melakarta. It is an ancient raga with strong traditional associations, especially in Tamil musical culture. The name and usage connect it with snakes (naga), and it is said to attract snakes when sung perfectly; snake charmers are traditionally associated with this scale. It is commonly used in wedding ceremonies, where the Odam played at the muhurtam is often in Punnagavarali. The raga is nishadantya, meaning it begins and ends on nishada within a single octave, and is therefore usually rendered in madhyama sruti. The source notes that chatushruti rishabha may also occur sometimes in the ascent. Its regal presence is best appreciated in slow phrases. The scale given is N2 S R1 G2 M1 P D1 N2 in ascent and N2 D1 P M1 G2 R1 S N2 in descent. It resembles Nadanamakriya to some extent. Traditional Tamil pieces such as Nondichindu are set in this raga. The raga is a janya of Hanumatodi and is not a melakarta because it ends on nishadam rather than upper shadjam. Additional web sources note that it has folk-music origins and is an example of a raga that originated as a bhashanga raga.
gamaka_usage: The raga is best brought out with slow, weighty phrases rather than fast brigha passages. N2 is the framing note and should be clearly established at both ends. R1 and G2 are handled with gentle oscillation and slides; G2 in particular is central to the raga’s identity and should be rendered with careful kampita/jaru treatment rather than as a plain note. D1 is also important and should be approached with controlled movement from P. The text notes that chatushruti rishabha (R2) may occur sometimes in the ascent, so if used it should be treated as an occasional ornamental variant rather than a structural note. Overall, the gamaka style should preserve the raga’s solemn, snake-like, and regal character, avoiding overly bright or rigidly flat intonation.

notable_compositions.name: Ela Ontiga
notable_compositions.composer: Kshetrayya, Chettipunyam Srinivasa Iyengar or Muvvanallur Sabhapati

notable_features: A nishadantya raga usually rendered in madhyama sruti. Associated with karuna rasa, snakes, snake charmers, and wedding-ceremony music (Odam at muhurtam). It is an ancient Tamil-tradition raga and has some resemblance to Nadanamakriya. The source explicitly notes that it is derived from Hanumatodi (melakarta 8) and that R2 may sometimes appear in the ascent. Additional web snippets describe it as having folk-music origins and as a raga that originated straightway as a bhashanga raga.

mood: Purvi is portrayed as deeply serious, quiet, and somewhat mystical, with a contemplative and uncommon character. It evokes twilight stillness, inward reflection, and a sense of longing or separation, while also carrying an austere beauty and dignity. The raga suits a reflective late-afternoon atmosphere and can feel both solemn and tender, as if the listener is watching the day fade into a meditative calm.
description: Purvi or Poorvi (IAST: Pūrvī) is a Hindustani classical raga that exemplifies its own Poorvi thaat and is the main raga of that thaat. It is an old traditional raga said to have originated in the eastern part of India. Its ancient precursor, Purvagauda, had a scale similar to modern Bhairav. Purvi itself does not appear in literature before the 16th century, and it is counted among the 14 original compositions associated with the legend Tansen. The raga is uncommon in performances nowadays. Its vadi is Ga and samvadi is Ni. The source also notes its rasa imagery in older treatises: charming, beautiful, lotus-eyed, associated with separation, splendor, and moonlike grace. Samay is the 4th prahar of the day, roughly 3pm to 6pm.
gamaka_usage: The raga’s treatment emphasizes delicate intonation and restrained movement rather than heavy ornamentation. Ga is the vadi and should be clearly brought out, while Ni is the samvadi and also receives prominence. The arohana specifically avoids dwelling on Sa and Pa, especially in fast taans, so these notes are often de-emphasized in ascent. The avarohana uses the characteristic descent through Ni–Dha–Pa–Mā–Ga, with the gandharam and madhyamam handled in a flowing, meend-like manner. Rishabham and gandharam are typically approached with subtle oscillation and careful slides, while the scale’s serious mood is preserved by avoiding overly bright or flashy gamaka treatment.
notable_features: Purvi is the main raga of the Purvi thaat. It has a characteristic pakad/chalan: S Ṟ G M̄ G M G M̄ Ḏ P N Ḏ P G M̄ P M̄ G M G Ṟ S. The source explicitly notes that in the arohana, Sa and Pa are often avoided, especially in fast taans. Historical rasa descriptions portray Purvi as charming, beautiful, and associated with separation and evening imagery. It is uncommon in modern performance practice.

raga_type: pentatonic in aroha, hexatonic in avaroha
mood: Rageshree has a light, romantic, and graceful mood. It evokes tenderness, charm, and affectionate longing, with a soft evening-like sweetness rather than deep pathos or dramatic intensity. The raga feels lilting and elegant, often suggesting delicate beauty, gentle love, and a calm, pleasing emotional glow.
description: Rageshree is a raga in Indian classical music that is popular in both Carnatic music and Hindustani music. The source text identifies it as belonging to the Khamaj thaat in Hindustani classification. It is described as pentatonic in the आरोह (ascending movement) and hexatonic in the अवरोह (descending movement). A key distinguishing feature noted is that Rageshree uses Shuddha Gandhar, whereas the related raga Bageshree uses Komal Gandhar. The raga is associated with a light romantic character. No Carnatic-specific parent melakarta is stated in the source, and no composer or pedagogical lineage is given in the provided content.
gamaka_usage: The source does not explicitly describe gamakas, but from the scale and character the raga is likely rendered with gentle, flowing ornamentation rather than heavy oscillation. G3 (shuddha gandhar) is the defining note and should be clearly articulated, often with a soft glide into M1 or from R2 in descent. N2 and D2 in the upper and middle register are typically treated smoothly, with light jaru-like transitions supporting the romantic mood. P and S are generally stable anchor notes. Because the raga is light and lyrical, excessive kampita on the swaras would be avoided; instead, subtle meend and delicate phrasing would preserve its graceful, romantic identity. In practice, the descent may emphasize smooth phrases such as N2 D2 P M1 G3 R2 S, with restrained oscillation and a clean, unforced touch on the key swaras.
notable_features: The raga is explicitly said to be popular in both Carnatic and Hindustani music. It is from the Khamaj thaat in Hindustani classification. The main distinction from Bageshree is the use of Shuddha Gandhar instead of Komal Gandhar. The source also notes that it is pentatonic in ascent and hexatonic in descent. No compositions are listed in the provided text. In Carnatic terms, the scale fits under parent melakarta 29 (Shankarabharanam) by note content, though the source does not explicitly state this.

mood: Ranjani has a bright, pleasing, and attractive mood that feels immediately agreeable to the ear. Its pentatonic symmetry and omission of panchamam create a light, open, and graceful atmosphere, often evoking contentment, gentle devotion, and a sense of wonder at its simplicity. It suits ragamalikas, slokams, vrittams, and light popular songs, and can feel soothing yet sparkling rather than deeply heavy or tragic.
description: Ranjani is a Carnatic janya raga derived from the 59th melakarta, Dharmavati. It is an asymmetric pentatonic scale, classified as audava-audava, with panchamam omitted from the parent scale and an additional kakali nishadam appearing in the avarohana in place of the sadharana rishabham. The raga is noted as pleasing and has become more popular in the last 50 years. It is commonly used in ragamalikas, slokams, vrittams, and light popular songs. A distinctive catch phrase associated with the raga is S R2 G2 S, S N3 D2 S, with N3 and D2 in the lower octave. The source emphasizes its asymmetrical use of notes as the reason for its pleasing character.
gamaka_usage: The raga is treated with a light, graceful gamaka style rather than heavy oscillation. R2 and G2 are typically handled with gentle kampita and smooth jaru-like movement into and out of phrases, while M2 and D2 are usually sustained clearly to preserve the pentatonic outline. N3 in the descent is a characteristic note and should be touched distinctly, often with a soft glide from upper S into N3 and then to D2. The catch phrase S R2 G2 S and S N3 D2 S suggests phrase-based treatment more than continuous oscillation. Compared with closely related pleasing pentatonic ragas, the absence of P gives the raga a more open, floating feel, and the anya nishadam in descent must be handled carefully so it sounds idiomatic rather than foreign.
notable_features: Janya of Dharmavati (59th melakarta). Asymmetric audava-audava structure. Panchamam is removed from the parent scale. Uses kakali nishadam (N3) only in avarohana, making it bhashanga. Popularity has increased over the last 50 years. Frequently used in ragamalikas, slokams, vrittams, and light songs. Unique catch phrase: S R2 G2 S, S N3 D2 S (with N3 and D2 in the lower octave).

mood: Reetigowla has a deeply rakti, lyrical, and emotionally rich character, evoking devotion blended with immense joy. Its phrases feel graceful, tender, and flowing, often creating a sense of affectionate surrender and serene delight. The raga can suggest a contemplative devotional atmosphere while also carrying a bright, melodious charm that makes it feel warmly uplifting and aesthetically satisfying.
description: Reetigowla, also spelled Reethigowla, Reethi Gowla, or Reethigowlai, is a janya raga associated with the 22nd melakarta, Kharaharapriya. It is described as a vakra shadava-sampoorna raga, with six notes in the arohana and seven in the avarohana, and is regarded as a rakti raga with high melodic content. The raga is known for its unique sancharams and prayogams and for evoking bhakti mixed with immense joy through vadi-samvadi relationships such as Sa-Ma, Ri-Dha, and Ga-Ni. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, an asampurna/melakarta form of the scale is known as Nārīrītigowla, listed as the 20th melakarta in Venkatamakhin's original scheme. The raga is strongly associated with rich melodic expression and is a major concert and composition raga, especially for varnams and kritis.
gamaka_usage: Reetigowla relies on characteristic vakra prayogas and delicate phrase-based handling rather than straight scalar movement. G2 is central and is often approached in zig-zag fashion with R2; the G2–R2–G2 movement is a signature phrase. N2 is especially important, with the blueprint N2 N2 S giving the raga its distinctive identity. M1 is prominent and is often touched repeatedly in descent, as in G2 M1 P M1 G2 R2 S, creating a flowing, oscillating contour. D2 and N2 are used with careful phrase shaping rather than heavy scalar emphasis. The raga’s identity depends strongly on gamaka-inflected sancharams and on preserving its characteristic note relationships; in the Dikshitar school’s Nārīrītigowla, the scale uses D1 instead of D2, giving a different note color and treatment.
notable_features: Associated with the 22nd melakarta Kharaharapriya. Classified as vakra shadava-sampoorna. It is explicitly described as a rakti raga with high melodic content. The raga evokes bhakti mixed with immense joy through Sa-Ma, Ri-Dha, and Ga-Ni relationships. The blueprint N2 N2 S is a distinctive identifying phrase. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, the related asampurna/melakarta form is Nārīrītigowla, the 20th melakarta in Venkatamakhin's original list, with vakra prayoga and a D1-based scale.

mood: Revagupti has a calm, morning freshness with a simple, symmetrical contour that feels clean, serene, and lightly devotional. Its pentatonic structure gives it an uncluttered, meditative quality, often evoking quiet optimism, gentle prayer, and a sense of stillness at dawn. Because it avoids madhyamam and nishadam, the raga sounds open and airy rather than tense or dramatic, making it suitable for peaceful contemplation and soft auspiciousness.
description: Revagupti is a symmetric audava-audava janya rāgam in Carnatic music, meaning it is a pentatonic derived scale with five notes in both ascent and descent. It is a morning rāgam. The scale omits madhyamam and nishādham entirely, using shadjam, shuddha rishabham, antara gandharam, panchamam, and shuddha dhaivatam. Its ārohaṇa-avarohaṇa is S R1 G3 P D1 S and S D1 P G3 R1 S. It is considered a janya rāgam of Mayamalavagowla, the 15th melakarta, though it can also be derived from five other melakarta rāgams by dropping both madhyamam and nishādham. The similar scales in Hindustani music are Rewa and Bibhas. The raga is closely related in scale-similarity discussions to Bhupalam, Bhauli, and Karnataka Shuddha Saveri, differing from them by specific note choices or added notes. It is a compact, pedagogically approachable raga because of its symmetry and limited note set. The raga is regarded as auspicious and is often associated with early morning singing.
gamaka_usage: Revagupti is generally rendered with restrained, light gamaka treatment rather than heavy oscillation. R1 and D1 can take gentle kampita or subtle oscillation, especially in phrases that linger on the note, while G3 and P are usually approached more plainly and held steady to preserve the raga's clean pentatonic identity. Smooth jaru-like transitions between S–R1–G3 and P–D1–S help maintain its airy, balanced character. Because the raga is symmetrical and sparse, excessive gamaka can obscure its outline; the characteristic color comes from clear prayogas and delicate ornamentation rather than elaborate oscillation. Compared with more heavily gamaka-driven ragas, Revagupti should sound direct, serene, and uncluttered.
notable_features: Revagupti is a janya of Mayamalavagowla (15th melakarta) and can also be derived from five other melakarta ragas by omitting madhyamam and nishadam. It is a symmetric audava-audava scale with no madhyamam or nishadam. The raga is a morning raga and is considered auspicious for early morning singing. Its closest scale relatives mentioned are Bhupalam (differs only by gandharam), Bhauli (adds nishadam in descent), and Karnataka Shuddha Saveri (uses M1 instead of G3).

mood: Revati has a serene, plaintive, and devotional mood. Its pentatonic structure gives it a simple, uncluttered, almost meditative character, often evoking tenderness, pathos, and quiet surrender. The raga can feel like a gentle prayer or a reflective twilight landscape, suitable for contemplative and devotional settings. Because it is also associated with Vedic chanting, it carries an austere sacredness rather than exuberance.
description: Revati is a rāgam in Carnatic music and is classified as an audava rāgam, meaning it uses a pentatonic scale. It is a janya rāgam, as it does not employ all seven swaras. Its scale is S R1 M1 P N2 S in ascent and the same notes in descent, giving it a symmetrical and straightforward structure. The raga is considered a janya of Ratnangi, the 2nd melakarta, though it can also be derived from several other melakartas by omitting gandharam and dhaivatam. Revati is closely associated with devotional and contemplative expression, and is noted for its use in Vedic chanting. It is also linked by graha bhedam to Shivaranjani and Sunadavinodini. In comparison with Madhyamavati, Revati uses R1 instead of R2, giving it a more plaintive and introspective color. In Hindustani music it closely resembles Bairagi Bhairav, and in Western/Japanese contexts it is associated with the Insen scale.
gamaka_usage: Revati is generally rendered with restrained gamaka treatment, emphasizing its austere and devotional character. R1 is typically approached with a gentle kampita or subtle oscillation rather than a heavy flourish, while M1 and P are usually held steady and flat to preserve the raga’s simplicity. N2 may take a light oscillatory touch or a smooth jaru into the upper shadjam, but it should not be overdecorated. The raga’s identity depends on clear, unambiguous phrasing of S–R1–M1–P–N2 and the descent >S–N2–P–M1–R1–S; excessive gamaka can blur it toward related pentatonic ragas. Compared with Madhyamavati, the lower R1 in Revati gives a more plaintive color, and the gamakas should reinforce that softer, more introspective feel.
notable_features: Revati is a pentatonic janya rāgam with no gandharam or dhaivatam. It is considered a janya of Ratnangi (melakarta 2), though it can also be derived from several other melakartas by omitting G and D. Its notes can be used in graha bhedam to obtain Shivaranjani and Sunadavinodini. The raga is also noted for its use in Vedic chanting. The text explicitly compares it to Madhyamavati, which substitutes R2 for Revati’s R1.

mood: Sahana creates a soft, rakti-filled atmosphere of tenderness and repose. It feels devotional and surrendering, with a plaintive sweetness that can shade into gentle pathos, yet it remains graceful and soothing rather than heavy. The raga often evokes images of quiet prayer, emotional warmth, and intimate lyrical beauty; in some compositions it also carries a delicate romantic glow. Its curved phrases give it a distinctive, refined personality that is calm, emotionally rich, and immediately recognizable.
description: Sahana is a popular janya raga of the 28th melakarta Harikambhoji. It is widely regarded as one of the crown jewels of Carnatic music and is described as a rakti raga par excellence. The raga is an Ubhaya Vakra sampoorna ragam: all seven notes are present, but both ascent and descent are zig-zag rather than linear. It is also described as a desiya raga, indicating that it evolved in practice and later entered formal theory. Sahana is closely associated with devotional and lyrical compositions, and many composers in the Carnatic tradition have created works in it. The raga is noted for its subtle emotional coloring and for its ability to portray unconditional surrender, tenderness, and calm beauty. It is also said to be another name for Goddess Lakshmi or Maa Durga in one source, and the word is glossed there as meaning 'pure and blissfully happy.'
gamaka_usage: Sahana depends strongly on phrase-based gamaka treatment rather than plain scalar rendering. The main jeeva swaras are R2, G3, and N2, and these are handled with gentle kampita and nuanced oscillation. R2 is often approached and left with a soft glide, giving the raga its plaintive, yearning color. G3 is used sparingly as an anya swara and should not be overemphasized; when used, it is typically touched lightly within phrases such as R2 G3 M1 and M1 G3 M1 R2 G3 R2 S. M1 is prominent and often acts as a pivot in vakra prayogas. P is comparatively stable, while D2 and N2 are rendered with smooth jaru-like movement, especially in the phrase P M1 D2 N2 and the descent N2 S D2. The raga’s identity comes from these curved, ornamented phrases; flat scalar singing weakens it considerably. Compared with related ragas such as Malavi, Sahana shares the vakra contour but requires its own delicate oscillatory treatment and restrained use of G3.
hindustani_equivalent: Sahana (upper-tetrachord-dominant Kanada-anga raga, allied with Bageshree and Bhimpalasi)
notable_features: The raga is a janya of Harikambhoji (28th melakarta) and is explicitly described as Ubhaya Vakra sampoorna. It is a desiya raga that entered theory from practice. The source states that it is bhashanga because G3 occurs sparingly. Its characteristic phrases are R2 G3 M1 P, P M1 D2 N2, N2 S D2, and G3 M1 R2. It has a close relationship to Malavi, which shares an identical vakra scale formation, and some relationship to Dwijawanthi/Jujavanti, though the gandhara usage differs. The Hindustani Sahana is noted separately as a Kanada-anga raga from Kafi thaat, allied with Bageshree and Bhimpalasi, with shuddha Dhaivat as an important resting note. The raga is widely used in both Carnatic and Tamil film music.

mood: Salaga Bhairavi has a gentle, devotional, and somewhat plaintive emotional color. Its pentatonic ascent gives it a clean, open, and restrained feel, while the fuller descent adds depth and a touch of yearning. The raga can evoke calm devotion, quiet introspection, and a soft pathos, making it suitable for reflective and prayerful settings rather than overtly dramatic expression.
description: Salaga Bhairavi is a janya rāgam derived from the 22nd melakarta, Kharaharapriya. It is an asymmetric scale that omits gandharam and nishadham in the ascent, and is described as an audava-sampurna rāgam. The text also notes that, according to the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, it is a shadava-sampoorna type derived from Kharaharapriya, and that in that school the scale uses sadharana gandharam in the ascent with a vakra prayoga of P D2 P. It is presented as a combination of the pentatonic Shuddha Saveri and the sampurna Kharaharapriya scale. A graha bhedam relationship is given: Dhanyasi can be derived from Salaga Bhairavi when sung from Rishabham to Rishabham. The raga has many compositions and is used in both devotional and concert contexts.
gamaka_usage: The source text does not give a full gamaka prescription, but the raga’s treatment is implied by its Kharaharapriya-derived character. In the standard form, R2 and M1 are the most stable swaras and can be prolonged without gamakam. G2 and N2 are handled with careful oscillation and slide-like movement in descent, while D2 is used clearly in the ascent and descent. In the Dikshitar-school variant, the vakra phrase P D2 P is important, and the gandharam is treated with a deliberate oscillatory approach rather than as a plain note. Compared with closely related ragas, the absence of G in the ascent and the restrained handling of the upper notes help preserve its distinct identity.
notable_features: Janya of Kharaharapriya (22nd melakarta). Audava-sampurna structure in the common form; Dikshitar school describes it as shadava-sampoorna. The ascent omits gandharam and nishadham. The scale is related to Shuddha Saveri in ascent and Kharaharapriya in descent. Dikshitar-school variant includes sadharana gandharam in ascent and vakra P D2 P usage. Graha bhedam from Rishabham to Rishabham yields Dhanyasi.

mood: Saramati has a calm, devotional, and gently plaintive character. Its ascent carries a complete, balanced movement, while the descent becomes sparse and meditative by dropping rishabham and panchamam, creating a feeling of inwardness and restraint. The raga can evoke serenity, prayerful devotion, and a soft emotional ache, making it suitable for reflective and spiritually colored expression rather than overt drama. The overall effect is soothing yet slightly wistful, with a dignified, contemplative atmosphere.
description: Saramati is a janya rāgam derived from the 20th melakarta Natabhairavi. It is an asymmetric scale that combines the sampurna structure of Natabhairavi in the ascent with the pentatonic contour of Hindolam in the descent, and is therefore classified as sampurna-audava. The rāga omits panchamam and rishabham in the avarohaṇa. Its scale is S R2 G2 M1 P D1 N2 S in ascent and S N2 D1 M1 G2 S in descent. The source text explicitly notes its similarity to Hindolam, whose notes match Saramati’s descending scale. No specific inventor, chakra, or historical origin details are given in the provided text. The page lists many compositions set in Saramati, showing its use in both classical and devotional repertoire.
gamaka_usage: The provided text does not give explicit gamaka rules, but the scale strongly suggests a restrained, expressive treatment. R2 and G2 in the ascent are likely handled with gentle oscillation and connected phrases, while M1, D1, and N2 can be rendered with smooth jaru-like transitions. In the descent, the pentatonic Hindolam-like line S N2 D1 M1 G2 S is typically kept compact and flowing, with emphasis on the characteristic descent rather than heavy oscillation. Since panchamam and rishabham are absent in the avarohaṇa, they should be avoided in descent, and the raga’s identity depends on clean phraseology and the contrast between the fuller ascent and sparse descent.

notable_compositions.name: Sri Saradamba Bhaje
notable_compositions.composer: H. H. Sri Bharathi Tirtha Mahaswamigal

notable_features: Saramati is an asymmetric janya of Natabhairavi (20th melakarta). It is described as a combination of Natabhairavi and Hindolam, with the descending scale matching Hindolam exactly. The raga is sampurna-audava: all seven notes appear in ascent, but the descent is pentatonic and omits both R and P. The source also notes a film adaptation context: Ilaiyaraaja set Tyagaraja’s krithi 'Mari Mari Ninne' to this raga in the film Sindhu Bhairavi, though the original composition is in Kambhoji. The text explicitly mentions the scale similarity to Hindolam.

mood: Saveri has a deeply compassionate, plaintive, and dignified emotional color. It is strongly associated with karuna rasa, so it often feels tender, sorrowful, and empathetic rather than overtly tragic. The raga can evoke a sense of prayerful devotion, quiet nobility, and restrained pathos, making it suitable for reflective, emotionally weighty moments and devotional settings.
description: Saveri is a Carnatic raga and a janya of the 15th melakarta, Mayamalavagowla. It is an audava-sampurna raga, using five notes in ascent and a full seven-note descent. Its scale is characterized by the prominent use of shuddha rishabham and shuddha dhaivatam, which are specifically noted as the swaras that give the raga its distinctive identity. The ascending scale is S R1 M1 P D1 S, while the descending scale is S N3 D1 P M1 G3 R1 S. The raga is closely related in scale-feel to Karnataka Shuddha Saveri in ascent and to Mayamalavagowla in descent. The source text explicitly identifies it as a raga that brings out karuna rasa, i.e. compassion. It is also associated with a large body of compositions across composers and forms, including varnams, kritis, padams, geetams, javalis, and devotional songs. The text also notes that Bhavayaami Raghuraamam was originally fully in Saveri before being retuned into a ragamalika by Semmangudi Srinivasa Iyer.
gamaka_usage: The raga’s identity depends heavily on the treatment of R1 and D1, which are the most characteristic swaras and should be brought out with clear emphasis. In performance, these notes are typically handled with expressive kampita and gentle oscillation rather than being rendered mechanically. M1 and P are comparatively steadier anchor notes, while the descent should clearly outline the phrase N3-D1-P-M1-G3-R1-S to preserve the raga’s classical contour. The gamaka approach should highlight the plaintive weight of the raga: R1 often receives a delicate oscillation and D1 is stressed in descent and cadential phrases. Compared with lighter pentatonic ragas, Saveri’s descent requires more ornamented, emotionally laden phrasing to project its karuna character.
notable_features: Saveri is a janya of Mayamalavagowla (15th melakarta). It is an audava-sampurna raga with the ascent S R1 M1 P D1 S and descent S N3 D1 P M1 G3 R1 S. The raga’s hallmark is the strong identity of R1 and D1. It is explicitly said to evoke karuna rasa. A notable historical point is that Bhavayaami Raghuraamam was originally fully in Saveri, though Semmangudi Srinivasa Iyer later retuned it into a ragamalika with Saveri as the first raga. The source also compares Saveri’s ascending scale to Karnataka Shuddha Saveri and its descending scale to Mayamalavagowla. The text does not specify a melakarta number for Saveri itself because it is a janya raga.

mood: Shivaranjani has a serene, devotional, and gently uplifting mood, but it is also strongly capable of expressing pathos and tender melancholy. Its pentatonic core gives it a simple, open, and accessible emotional space, while the vakra movement in the Carnatic form adds a graceful, slightly meandering quality. It can evoke calm reflection, prayerful devotion, soft contemplative joy, and at times a plaintive, heart-touching sadness rather than intense drama. The raga suits intimate, meditative settings and can also carry a light, lyrical sweetness in compositions and film music.
description: Shivaranjani or Sivaranjani is a musical scale used in Indian classical music, with distinct Hindustani and Carnatic forms. In Carnatic music it is a janya raga derived from the 64th melakarta, Vachaspati. It is classified as audava-audava, meaning it has five notes in both ascent and descent. The Carnatic scale is vakra in prayoga, with zig-zag note phrases rather than a strictly linear movement. The scale uses shadjam, chatushruti rishabham, antara gandharam, prati madhyamam, chatushruti dhaivatam, panchamam, and kaisiki nishadam in the specific pattern given. The raga is related theoretically to other pentatonic scales through graha bhedam: shifting the tonic yields Sunadavinodini and Revati. The page also notes scale similarities with Mohanam, Pahadi, and Abhogi in the broader comparative discussion. Popular compositions in this raga include devotional and lyrical pieces by Papanasam Sivan, Madurai R. Muralidaran, and others. The Hindustani Shivaranjani is different and belongs to Kaafi thaat; the Carnatic Shivaranjani is the borrowed Carnatic form described here. It is especially valued for bhakti-laden and karuna-inflected expression, and is often chosen for songs of prayer, surrender, and emotional depth.
gamaka_usage: The source text does not give a detailed gamaka prescription, but the scale is explicitly vakra, so phrases should be rendered with smooth zig-zag movement rather than straight scalar runs. In practice, the raga is best approached with gentle oscillation and connected slides between R2, G3, M2, D2, P, and N2, preserving the pentatonic clarity. Because the scale is derived and relatively bright, the swaras are typically treated with light gamaka and graceful janta/jaru movement rather than heavy kampita. The vakra prayoga itself is an important part of the raga’s identity, so linear, plain-note rendering would understate its character. The descent often benefits from soft jaru-like connections, especially around D2-P-D2 and M2-G3-R2, while the notes are generally kept clean and unforced; the raga’s emotional effect comes more from phrase shape and contour than from heavy oscillation. Compared with more gamaka-heavy ragas, Shivaranjani is usually sung with restrained ornamentation and a plaintive, flowing touch.
notable_features: Carnatic Shivaranjani is a janya of the 64th melakarta Vachaspati. It is audava-audava and vakra. Graha bhedam on Shivaranjani yields two other pentatonic ragas: Sunadavinodini and Revati. The page also mentions that the traditional Marathi abhang Majhi Dev Puja has a popularized version in this raga. The raga is discussed alongside Mohanam, Pahadi, and Abhogi in scale-similarity comparisons. The text explicitly distinguishes the Carnatic and Hindustani Shivaranjani scales. It is also noted in external snippets as a raga associated with pathos, bhakti, and karuna, and is commonly rendered in late evening or midnight contexts in Hindustani usage, though that timing pertains to the Hindustani form rather than the Carnatic one.

mood: Shree is an ancient, highly auspicious raga with a serene, dignified, and devotional character. Its pentatonic ascent gives it a calm, uncluttered lift, while the vakra descent adds depth and a sense of graceful repose. The raga often evokes reverence, sacredness, and inner composure rather than overt drama, making it feel suitable for prayerful or ceremonial settings. It can also suggest majesty and auspicious blessing, especially because it is associated with the opening of the Pancharatna tradition and with compositions of devotional weight.
description: Shree is an ancient Carnatic raga and is also written as Sri or Sreeraga. It is the asampurna melakarta equivalent of Kharaharapriya, the 22nd melakarta rāgam, and is considered the last of the five Ghana rāgams of Carnatic music. It is a popular and highly auspicious raga. Carnatic Shree uses the lower madhyamam and is not related to the Hindustani raga Shree. Structurally it is an asymmetric audava-vakra-sampurna rāgam: the ascent omits gandharam and dhaivatam, while the descent is full and zig-zag. The source gives two descending possibilities, one standard vakra descent and another with an inserted D2 phrase. It is closely related by scale similarity to Madhyamavati, which shares the same ascent, and differs from Manirangu by the presence of G2 in descent. As the last of the Ghana ragas, it has strong pedagogical and concert significance, and it is famously the raga of Tyagaraja's Endaro Mahanubhavulu, the final Pancharatna kriti. The text also notes that it is the asampurna equivalent of Kharaharapriya, making it the prati-madhyama-less counterpart in the older scheme.
gamaka_usage: The raga’s gamaka treatment is centered on the rishabham, gandharam, nishadam, and the vakra descent. R2 and M1 are the only swaras explicitly noted as suitable for being prolonged without gamakam. G2 is handled with oscillation and is typically approached in conjunction with R2; the descent uses the characteristic phrase N2 P M1 R2 G2 R2 S, where G2 does not settle plainly but returns to R2, giving the raga its identity. The zig-zag descent itself is a structural feature, so jaru-like movement and curved phrases are important. Compared with related ragas, the avoidance of a plain, Kharaharapriya-like ending on G is significant; the raga’s flavor depends on ending the gamaka on R rather than resting on G. The source also mentions an alternate descent with D2, implying careful phrase-based handling of dhaivatam when used. Overall, the raga is not rendered with flat scalarity; its identity comes from nuanced oscillation and phraseology, especially around G2 and N2.
notable_features: Shree is the last of the five Ghana ragas (ghanapanchaka). It is the Carnatic asampurna melakarta equivalent of Kharaharapriya (22nd melakarta). The ascent matches Madhyamavati, while the descent distinguishes it from Madhyamavati by including G2 and from Manirangu by its specific descending phraseology. The raga is considered highly auspicious and is strongly associated with Tyagaraja's Endaro Mahanubhavulu, the final Pancharatna kriti. The source explicitly says it is not related to the Hindustani raga Shree.

mood: Shree ranjani is a pleasing, symmetric, and popular raga with a gentle, soothing, and devotional character. Its six-note structure and the absence of panchamam give it a clean, open, and slightly plaintive sweetness that feels calm rather than intense. It often evokes serenity, grace, and quiet devotion, making it suitable for reflective or auspicious moods and for compositions that aim for elegance and emotional warmth.
description: Shree ranjani is a derived (janya) rāgam in Carnatic music and is a hexatonic shadava-shadava scale, meaning it uses six notes in both ascent and descent. It is symmetric and omits panchamam entirely. Its structure is ārohaṇa S R2 G2 M1 D2 N2 Ṡ and avarohaṇa Ṡ N2 D2 M1 G2 R2 S. The raga uses shadjam, chatushruti rishabham, sadharana gandharam, shuddha madhyamam, chatushruti dhaivatham, and kaisiki nishadam. It is a janya of Kharaharapriya, the 22nd melakarta, and differs from its parent mainly by the omission of panchamam. The raga is also known as Sriranjani, Shri ranjani, and Shree Ranjani. It is a popular and pleasing raga used widely in classical compositions and even film music. Related scale comparisons in the source note that Abhogi lacks kaisiki nishadam, while Jayamanohari resembles Abhogi in ascent and Sriranjani in descent.
gamaka_usage: The source text does not give detailed gamaka rules for Shree ranjani, but its scalar shape implies a smooth, lyrical treatment. R2 and M1 are typically the most stable swaras and can be held clearly. G2 and N2 are likely to be rendered with gentle oscillation or light slides to preserve the raga’s sweetness, while D2 is approached cleanly and connected by jaru-like movement in phrases such as M1 G2 R2 and D2 N2 Ṡ. Since panchamam is absent, phrases should avoid P entirely. Compared with more gamaka-heavy ragas, Shree ranjani generally benefits from restrained ornamentation and flowing prayogas rather than heavy kampita, with emphasis on graceful transitions and the characteristic six-note contour.
notable_features: Hexatonic shadava-shadava raga; symmetric scale; omits panchamam; janya of Kharaharapriya (22nd melakarta). The source explicitly compares it with Abhogi and Jayamanohari by scale similarity. It is described as pleasing and popular, used in classical music and film music. The Wikipedia content does not mention a specific inventor or time of day. The raga is distinct from the Hindustani raga of the same name, which has a different scale.

mood: Shuddha Saveri has a calm, bright, and soothing pentatonic character that feels simple, pure, and uplifting. It often evokes peace, devotion, and gentle optimism rather than deep pathos or intense drama. Because of its clean scale and absence of gāndhāram and nishādam, it can sound open and uncluttered, making it suitable for beginner exercises as well as devotional and graceful compositions. It is often associated with a serene, auspicious atmosphere and can suit morning or contemplative settings.
description: Shuddha Saveri is an audava rāgam (pentatonic scale) in Carnatic music and Yakshagana music. It is a janya rāgam of the 29th melakarta, Dhīraśaṅkarābharaṇaṃ (Shankarabharanam), though it can also be derived by omitting gāndhāram and nishādam from Kharaharapriya, Gourimanohari, or Harikambhoji. It is a symmetric scale that omits both gāndhāram and nishādam. The scale uses chatushruti rishabham, shuddha madhyamam, panchamam, and chatushruti dhaivatam. In the Muthuswami Dikshitar school, this rāgam is called Devakriya; however, Karnataka Shuddha Saveri is a different raga in that school, derived from the 1st melakarta Kanakangi, with shuddha rishabham and shuddha dhaivatam. In Hindustani music, Shuddha Saveri corresponds to Durga. The raga has strong theoretical importance through graha bhedam: shifting its notes yields Mohanam, Hindolam, Madhyamavati, and Udayaravichandrika (Shuddha Dhanyasi). It is also closely related by scale similarity to Ārabhi, Jana Ranjani, and Yadukulakamboji. The page lists many compositions in this raga across major composers, and notes that Ānalekara by Purandara Dasa is one of the first geetams taught to beginners and is his first composition.
gamaka_usage: Shuddha Saveri is generally rendered with restrained, साफ/clean ornamentation rather than heavy oscillation. The raga’s identity is carried by clear landing on R2, M1, P, and D2, with S often approached by smooth jaru. Because G and N are absent, phrases tend to be open and direct, and the characteristic movement is often plain and symmetrical: S-R-M-P-D-S and the reverse. In practice, R2 and D2 may receive light kampita or gentle stress in sangatis, but they are not usually treated with the deep oscillation associated with more gamaka-heavy ragas. M1 and P are commonly held steady as anchor notes, while slides between R2-M1 and P-D2 help preserve the raga’s bright, devotional contour. Compared with closely related pentatonic ragas, Shuddha Saveri sounds less ornate than Mohanam and less plaintive than ragas with more oscillatory treatment; its gamaka profile is typically simple, elegant, and beginner-friendly.
notable_features: Shuddha Saveri is an audava-audava, symmetric pentatonic raga with no gāndhāram or nishādam. It is a janya of Shankarabharanam (29th melakarta) and is also derivable from Kharaharapriya, Gourimanohari, or Harikambhoji by dropping G and N. In the Dikshitar school, the name Devakriya refers to this raga, while Karnataka Shuddha Saveri is a different raga. Graha bhedam on Shuddha Saveri yields Mohanam, Hindolam, Madhyamavati, and  (Shuddha Dhanyasi). It is related by scale similarity to Ārabhi, Jana Ranjani, and Yadukulakamboji. Ānalekara by Purandara Dasa is noted as one of the first geetams taught to beginners and as his first composition.

mood: Sindhu Bhairavi has a plaintive yet flexible emotional world: it can sound devotional, tender, and deeply human, but also carry the ache of separation and sorrow. The raga often evokes a soft, reflective atmosphere that can move from gentle prayerfulness to poignant longing, making it suitable for both intimate devotional expression and emotionally charged lyrical passages.
